name: Home
title: Home
description: Homepage that loads dynamic overview content, exposes navigation to key sections, highlights featured services/projects/resources/tools, and provides newsletter subscription.

core_features: Load homepage overview data via getHomepageOverview and render overview heading, featured services, projects, resources, and tools.
Provide primary calls-to-action to request a quote and book a consultation, navigating to the appropriate pages with required URL parameters.
Expose quick navigation to Services, Projects, Resources, Tools, Our Team, Consultations, and About pages from the homepage.
Display interactive featured service cards that deep-link to corresponding service detail pages or the services overview.
Display interactive featured project case study cards that navigate to detailed project pages with project identifiers in the URL.
Display interactive featured resource items that navigate to resource detail pages with resource identifiers in the URL.
Display interactive featured tool items that navigate to tool-specific pages, including the Bearing Capacity Calculator.
Provide a newsletter subscription form that calls subscribeToNewsletter with user-specified preferences and shows feedback based on the response.
user_workflows: Homepage content exploration workflow: 1) User lands on index.html. 2) The page-level data loader component calls getHomepageOverview with no parameters. 3) While data is loading, the page shows a loading state for each dependent section. 4) Once the response is received, the page renders the overview heading using heroTitle and heroSubtitle, and distributes featuredServices, featuredProjects, featuredResources, and featuredTools to their respective components. 5) The user can scroll and click into any featured item (service, project, resource, tool) to navigate to its detail or overview page.

Request a quote workflow from homepage: 1) User clicks the primary 'Request a Quote' CTA. 2) The click handler builds the destination URL for quote_request.html with the required serviceType query parameter set to slope_stability_embankments. 3) Browser navigates to quote_request.html?serviceType=slope_stability_embankments where the quote request form is initialized for that service type.

Book a consultation workflow from homepage: 1) User clicks the 'Book a Consultation' CTA on the homepage. 2) The click handler navigates the browser to consultation.html with no additional URL parameters from this page. 3) The consultation page then handles the booking process using its own logic and interfaces.

Navigate to service details workflow: 1) User scrolls to the featured services section populated from featuredServices. 2) User clicks a featured service card. 3) The click handler inspects that service's pageFilename. 4) If pageFilename corresponds to a specific service page (e.g., service_slope_stability.html or service_subsurface_investigation.html), the browser navigates directly to that filename, optionally including identifying URL parameters such as slug for that service. 5) If the service does not have a dedicated pageFilename, the click handler navigates to services.html with a query parameter indicating the selected service slug so the services page can highlight it.

Browse all projects/resources/tools workflow: 1) User clicks 'View all projects', 'View all resources', or the 'Tools & Calculators' section link on the homepage. 2) The respective click handler navigates to projects.html, resources.html, or tools.html without additional URL parameters (or with optional parameters if needed by those pages to highlight sections). 3) The destination pages handle their own filtering and listing logic.

View project case study from homepage workflow: 1) User views the featured projects list rendered from featuredProjects. 2) User clicks a project case study card. 3) The click handler retrieves that project’s id and navigates to project_detail.html?id={projectId}. 4) The project detail page uses the id parameter to load and display full case study content.

View resource detail from homepage workflow: 1) User views the featured resources list rendered from featuredResources. 2) User clicks a resource item. 3) The click handler retrieves that resource’s id and navigates to resource_detail.html?id={resourceId}. 4) The resource detail page uses the id parameter to load and display the full resource (e.g., guide, checklist, technical note).

Access tools and calculators from homepage workflow: 1) User scrolls to the featured tools section rendered from featuredTools. 2) User clicks a tool item. 3) The click handler uses the tool’s url value to navigate to the tool page (e.g., bearing_capacity_calculator.html for the Bearing Capacity Calculator). 4) The tool page then handles its own input and result logic.

Newsletter subscription workflow: 1) User scrolls to the newsletter subscription form in the footer. 2) User enters an email address and selects one or more topics, an emailFrequency option, and optionally selects a professionalRole. 3) On form submission, the component validates that required fields are present and valid. 4) If validation passes, the component calls subscribeToNewsletter(email, topics, emailFrequency, professionalRole). 5) While waiting for a response, the form is disabled and a submitting state is shown. 6) On success (success=true), the component displays the returned message and may indicate that a confirmation email is required based on isConfirmed. 7) On failure (success=false), the component displays the error message and re-enables the form for correction.

Navigate to team and about pages workflow: 1) User clicks 'Meet our team' or 'Learn more about us' links from the homepage. 2) The respective click handler navigates to team.html or about.html without additional URL parameters. 3) The destination pages handle content rendering based on their own interfaces and data models.
interactions: Click on main navigation 'Services' link: navigates to services.html.
Click on main navigation 'Projects' link or 'View all projects' within the homepage: navigates to projects.html.
Click on main navigation 'Resources' link or 'View all resources' within the homepage: navigates to resources.html.
Click on main navigation 'Tools' link or 'Tools & Calculators' section within the homepage: navigates to tools.html.
Click on 'Request a Quote' primary CTA: navigates to quote_request.html?serviceType=slope_stability_embankments.
Click on 'Book a Consultation' CTA: navigates to consultation.html.
Click on 'Meet our team' link: navigates to team.html.
Click on 'Learn more about us' link: navigates to about.html.
Click on a featured service card in the featured services section: navigates to the corresponding service detail page using that service's pageFilename and, if needed, its slug as a URL parameter.
Click on a featured project case study card: navigates to project_detail.html?id={projectId}.
Click on a featured resource item: navigates to resource_detail.html?id={resourceId}.
Click on a featured tool item: navigates to the tool.url, such as bearing_capacity_calculator.html for the bearing capacity calculator.

Submit the newsletter subscription form: triggers client-side validation then calls subscribeToNewsletter; based on the response, shows either a success state with the returned message or an error state.
Click on the site logo or 'Home' navigation link in header or footer: navigates back to index.html.
page_functionality.state_logic: The Home page (index.html) does not require or parse any incoming URL parameters; it always calls getHomepageOverview with no arguments on initial load. Page state is driven by the results of this interface and by user interactions. The primary homepage data loading state includes: (1) initial 'loading' state before getHomepageOverview returns; (2) 'loaded' state where heroTitle, heroSubtitle, featuredServices, featuredProjects, featuredResources, and featuredTools are bound to their respective components; (3) 'error' state if the interface call fails, where dependent sections can display a generic error message and optional retry control. Navigation from the homepage to other pages uses URL parameters to convey selection context: (a) clicking the 'Request a Quote' CTA navigates to quote_request.html?serviceType=slope_stability_embankments so the quote form is preconfigured; (b) clicking a featured project uses project_detail.html?id={projectId}; (c) clicking a featured resource uses resource_detail.html?id={resourceId}; (d) clicking a service card may include query parameters such as slug in the destination URL, for example services.html?service={serviceSlug} or service-specific pages with an identifying parameter as required by those pages; (e) clicking specific tools navigates to tool.url, which may itself include parameters for that tool page. Newsletter subscription state is local to the page and not reflected in the URL: before submission, the form is 'idle'; on submit, the form enters a 'submitting' state; after subscribeToNewsletter returns, the state becomes either 'success' (with subscription data bound) or 'error' (with error message bound). No state is persisted via URL parameters on the Home page itself.

components.functionality: On page initialization, this component calls getHomepageOverview with no parameters, manages loading and error states, and distributes heroTitle, heroSubtitle, featuredServices, featuredProjects, featuredResources, and featuredTools to child components via props or context.
Displays the main homepage heading and subheading using heroTitle and heroSubtitle from getHomepageOverview, and includes primary CTAs for requesting a quote and booking a consultation.

Provides prominent, functionally-oriented buttons to start a quote request and to book a consultation. The quote request CTA always routes to the quote request page with a predefined serviceType specific to slope stability embankments.

Renders a grid or list of featured consulting services from featuredServices. Each service card is interactive and, when clicked, navigates either to a service-specific detail page defined by its pageFilename or to the services overview page with an identifying URL parameter.

Displays a list or carousel of featured project case studies from featuredProjects. Each item shows summary information and allows navigation to a detailed project page using the project id in the URL.

Displays a set of featured resources (such as guides, checklists, or technical notes) from featuredResources. Each resource item is clickable and navigates to a resource detail page with the resource id as a URL parameter.

Displays a list of featured geotechnical tools and calculators from featuredTools. Each item links to its url, allowing access to tools such as the Bearing Capacity Calculator directly from the homepage.
Provides a concise overview of project expertise by presenting a small subset of featuredProjects and a 'View all projects' link that navigates to projects.html.
Provides main navigation links for Home, Services, Projects, Resources, Tools, Our Team, and Consultations, enabling users to access all major site sections from any point on the homepage.

Provides footer-level navigation, including a link back to the homepage via the logo or text link, and quick links to important sections such as Services, Projects, Resources, Tools, Team, Consultations, and About.

Collects an email address, topic preferences, email frequency, and optional professional role for newsletter subscriptions and calls subscribeToNewsletter. Manages validation, submitting state, and displays success or error messages based on the API response.
Displays a non-visual-specific status message when getHomepageOverview fails or when subscribeToNewsletter returns an error, providing the ability to retry the relevant operation.

layout.grid_system: Implied 12‑column responsive grid (typical WordPress/corporate layout) with a full-width container for the hero and a centered content column.
layout.layout_pattern: Use a strict top-to-bottom, single-column page flow anchored by a full-width fixed header. Directly beneath the header, reserve the first screen for a full-bleed hero (overview-header) that behaves like a headline article: category-style tag, large multi-line headline, and compact meta row. All subsequent components (CTA panel, featured lists, summary panels, newsletter, footer nav) stack vertically in a centered content column that follows a 12-column responsive grid, with each section clearly separated by generous vertical spacing.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: Use a strict top-to-bottom, single-column page flow anchored by a full-width fixed header. Directly beneath the header, reserve the first screen for a full-bleed hero (overview-header) that behaves like a headline article: category-style tag, large multi-line headline, and compact meta row. All subsequent components (CTA panel, featured lists, summary panels, newsletter, footer nav) stack vertically in a centered content column that follows a 12-column responsive grid, with each section clearly separated by generous vertical spacing.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: Allocate 10–15% of viewport height to the fixed navigation header, and let the hero (overview-header) consume the remaining visible viewport height on initial load, minus any error banner if present. Below the hero, each major content section (CTA, services, projects, tools, resources, newsletter) gets its own band with 48–64px vertical margins between sections. Content is constrained to a central column about 70–80% of the page width on desktop (8–10 of 12 columns), with safe lateral padding on smaller screens. Visual weight is concentrated in the hero and the first one or two sections below it; lower sections feel progressively lighter.
layout_strategies.content_density: Maintain a moderately spacious density: few elements per section, large headings, and consistent 16–24px internal padding in cards and panels. The hero is particularly open, with big typography and clear breathing room around the title. Supporting grids and lists use comfortable card paddings and 24–32px gutters, ensuring readability and a corporate, premium feel even when many items are present.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: Reinforce an F/Z pattern: the eye starts at the logo in the top-left of the fixed header, sweeps across the centered navigation, then drops diagonally into the centered hero category pill and headline, finally moving down into the meta row. From there, the eye flows straight down through the primary CTA just below the hero, then through each featured section in order. Section headings sit at the top-left of their content area to anchor scanning, with cards and lists arranged left-to-right within the central column.

layout.overall_description: The page opens with a fixed, full-width teal navigation bar that spans the top 10–15% of the viewport, containing the logo on the left and a horizontally centered navigation menu aligned to the right portion of the inner container. Directly beneath this bar, a full-bleed hero occupies nearly the entire remaining initial viewport: a geotechnical/engineering image stretches edge-to-edge, softened by a subtle dark overlay so the centered white text stands out clearly. In the vertical middle of this hero, a tight stack of elements sits in a centered content column: a small pill-shaped category label at the top, a bold multi-line headline just below, and a single compact line of metadata (avatar, date, comment count) under that, all center-aligned. When content is loading, this same hero area shows a minimal loader in place of the text. As you scroll down past the hero, the layout transitions into a series of well-separated horizontal bands, each with its content centered in a consistent column: a strong primary CTA panel, then a services grid, then a projects summary and list, followed by tools and resources. Each section uses clear headings, generous spacing, and orderly grids or stacked cards. Near the bottom, a clean newsletter subscription block stands on its own band, followed finally by a structured footer navigation that echoes the header’s full-width, high-contrast bar treatment. The result is a cohesive, modern corporate page where the hero dominates first impression and subsequent sections read as clearly delineated, scannable slices of content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The navigation-header is a fixed-position bar anchored to the very top of the viewport, stretching full width from left to right and occupying approximately 10–15% of the viewport height (around 72–88px on standard desktops). It uses a teal background and spans edge-to-edge, with an inner content container centered horizontally at about 70–80% of the page width (max-width roughly 1200px) and 16–24px of horizontal padding on both sides. Inside this inner container, the logo sits on the far left, vertically centered in the bar. The navigation links form a single horizontal row on the right side of the container, also vertically centered, with even spacing between items and comfortable left/right padding so the menu does not appear crowded. There is clear separation between the header and any content below it, with the hero section beginning immediately under the header’s bottom edge.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Fixed at the top of the viewport; remains visible while scrolling, with the rest of the page content scrolling underneath.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The homepage-error-banner appears directly beneath the fixed navigation-header, spanning the full width of the viewport edge-to-edge. It is a relatively shallow horizontal strip, roughly 48–64px tall, with a high-contrast background (e.g., warm red or amber) and centered text to ensure immediate visibility. When present, it pushes the hero (overview-header) down so there is no overlap; when absent, the hero sits flush under the header. Internally, the banner content is constrained to the same centered content column as the rest of the page (about 70–80% width with 16–24px side padding), with the main error message text aligned left within that column and optional icon or close control aligned to the right, all vertically centered.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block positioned directly below the fixed navigation; appears and disappears without overlaying other components, simply shifting the starting point of the hero downward.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The homepage-overview-loader occupies the same spatial footprint as the hero’s main content area and is centered within the hero section when overview data is loading. It sits inside the full-bleed hero background (defined by the overview-header) and aligns to the central content column. Visually, it is centered both horizontally and vertically within the hero, roughly at the vertical midpoint of the viewport, directly where the category pill and headline would normally appear. It might consist of a central loader indicator (spinner or skeleton blocks) sized at about 25–35% of the hero’s width (max-width ~400px), with generous padding around it (24–32px) and clear breathing room to the top and bottom of the hero. The loader does not change the hero’s full-height behavior; it simply replaces the text stack until data is ready.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Appears conditionally within the hero area, overlaying or replacing the hero’s text content while preserving the same centered alignment and vertical spacing, then disappears once content loads.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The overview-header acts as the main hero section, starting immediately below the navigation-header or error banner if present. It is a full-width, full-bleed section with a tall height equal to roughly 85–90% of the viewport (ensuring the combination of header plus hero essentially fills the first screen). A high-resolution geotechnical/engineering image forms the background, extending to both left and right page edges, with a subtle dark overlay gradient to maintain legible contrast. In the center of this hero, a content column is horizontally aligned to the page center and constrained to a maximum width of about 720–840px (around 8–9 of 12 columns). This column is vertically centered, with the main text cluster occupying the central 40–60% of the hero’s vertical space. Within this column, elements are stacked and center-aligned: at the top of the cluster is a compact pill-shaped category label (for example, 'Geotechnical Insight') with small caps text, minimal height (about 32px), and 8–16px horizontal padding. Below it, separated by about 16–24px of vertical space, sits a large multi-line headline in white, taking up 2–3 lines of text and spanning roughly 70–90% of the column width. Under the headline, separated by another 16–24px gap, is a single row of post metadata: a small circular avatar on the left, followed by author name, date, and comment count laid out in a single horizontal line with modest spacing between each item. This meta row is horizontally centered under the headline. There is ample empty space above the category pill and below the meta line to maintain a clean, premium hero feel.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static hero section that anchors the top of the scrolling content; its background image remains fixed relative to the page content (no parallax by default), while inner text content centers dynamically with viewport height.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The primary-cta-panel sits immediately below the hero (overview-header), separated from it by 48–64px of vertical space to clearly mark the transition from hero to main content. It stretches full-width across the page as a distinct horizontal band, with a solid background color that contrasts with both the hero and the body background (e.g., a lighter teal or neutral light gray) but does not overpower the hero. Inside, content is constrained to the central column (max-width ~1200px) with 24–32px of vertical padding and 16–24px horizontal padding on each side. On desktop, the panel content is arranged in a two-column layout: on the left (about 60% of the content width) sits a concise section title and 1–2 lines of supporting text, both left-aligned; on the right (about 40% of the content width) sits a primary CTA button (or button group) vertically centered relative to the text block. On smaller screens, the layout becomes single-column, with text stacked above the CTA button, all center-aligned, maintaining the same internal padding. The CTA panel visually feels like the first actionable step after reading the hero.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static section block that follows the hero; maintains its two-column layout on wider viewports and gracefully stacks into a single centered column on tablets and mobile.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The featured-services-grid is the first major content section after the primary CTA panel, separated by about 64px of vertical space to signify a new section. It forms a full-width horizontal band with a neutral background (usually the main page background) and a centered content container at around 70–80% page width (max-width ~1200px) with 32–48px of vertical padding. At the top of the container, aligned left, is a clear section heading ('Featured Services') with a short optional description underneath, forming a compact text block occupying roughly the left two-thirds of the container. Below this heading block (after 24–32px vertical spacing), the services are displayed as a responsive grid of cards. On desktop, cards are typically arranged in three columns with even horizontal gutters (about 24–32px) and vertical spacing of 24px between rows. Each card occupies roughly one-third of the content width, with consistent internal padding (16–24px) and a subtle outline or shadow to distinguish it from the background. On tablets, the grid shifts to two columns; on mobile, it collapses to a single column with cards stacked vertically. The grid maintains clear side margins so card content never touches the viewport edges, reinforcing a tidy, corporate presentation.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static section with a responsive grid; cards reflow from three columns to two to one as the available width decreases, while the section header remains left-aligned at the top.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The projects-summary-panel sits directly above the featured-projects-list, acting as an introductory band for recent or flagship projects. It is separated from the featured-services-grid by about 48–64px of vertical space. This panel stretches full width but uses a slightly differentiated background (e.g., a faint tint of the brand color) to stand out from the previous section. Within the centered content container (same width as other sections), the panel presents its content in a horizontally oriented layout on desktop: on the left side (about 50–60% of the width) sits a section title like 'Recent Projects' with 1–2 concise lines describing the type of work or expertise; on the right side (about 40–50% of the width) sits a compact summary of key stats (e.g., number of projects, locations, sectors), optionally formatted as 2–3 small metric blocks aligned horizontally. Vertical padding of 24–32px around the panel ensures it feels like a distinct but compact overview. On smaller screens, this layout becomes a single column with the title and description stacked on top and the key stats placed beneath, all center-aligned.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static horizontal band that bridges into the projects list; shifts between two-column and single-column layouts depending on viewport width.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The featured-projects-list appears immediately beneath the projects-summary-panel, with a modest 24–32px vertical gap to visually tie them together. It uses the same central content width, and the section itself adopts the main page background to keep focus on the project content. At the top-left of the list area is a subtle subheading or filter row (optional) aligning with the section title above. Below this, project entries are laid out in a responsive grid or structured list: on desktop, projects appear as two columns of wide cards (each roughly half the container width) with 24–32px horizontal and vertical spacing between them. Each project card includes a thumbnail image at the top (spanning the card width) and text details (project name, brief description, location or sector tags) beneath, all left-aligned with 16–24px internal padding. On tablets, the cards may still display in two columns if the width allows; on smaller devices they stack into a single column for clarity. The entire section maintains 32–48px of vertical padding at top and bottom so the list doesn’t feel cramped, even with multiple projects.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static section with a responsive card list; number of columns adapts to viewport width, while maintaining consistent gutters and card padding.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The featured-tools-list section follows the featured-projects-list after a clear 48–64px vertical separation, forming another full-width band with the main page background. Its centered content container mirrors the width and padding of prior sections. At the top-left, a section heading such as 'Engineering Tools' is presented, with a one-line explanation beneath to explain their value (e.g., calculators, modeling tools). Below this, tools are displayed as a compact grid or horizontal tiles emphasizing utility over imagery. On desktop, this may be a three-column grid of relatively small, clean cards, each containing a tool name, a brief one-line description, and a compact secondary CTA like 'Open Tool' or an icon, with 16–20px internal padding. Horizontal gutters are around 24px, vertical spacing between rows is about 24px. On narrower screens, the grid collapses to two or one column so each tool remains easy to tap and read. The section is visually simpler than services or projects, with less imagery and more emphasis on crisp labels and clear affordances.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static responsive grid; cards reflow across breakpoints, maintaining consistent spacing and alignment within the central content column.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The featured-resources-list is positioned below the featured-tools-list with 48–64px of vertical spacing, forming a content band that showcases articles, guides, or downloads. The background remains neutral, letting content titles stand out. Inside the centered container, the section heading ('Resources' or 'Insights') is aligned to the left at the top, optionally accompanied by a brief description or a subtle filter (e.g., 'All / Guides / Papers') aligned right. Resource items are arranged in a vertically stacked list or a two-column layout depending on available width. On desktop, a two-column layout works well: each column contains resource cards, each card spanning about half the container width with 24px horizontal gutters. Cards have 16–24px internal padding and consist primarily of text: a bold resource title, a smaller snippet or summary line, and meta info such as category or reading time. On tablet and mobile, the layout simplifies to a single column of resource cards stacked with 16–24px vertical spacing. The overall vertical padding (top and bottom) is around 32–48px, maintaining a rhythm consistent with other sections.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static section that adjusts from a two-column to single-column card list as the viewport narrows, while keeping headings anchored at the top-left of the content area.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The newsletter-subscription-form sits near the bottom of the page, following the featured-resources-list with a generous 64px vertical gap so it feels like a distinct closing call-to-action. It is presented as a full-width horizontal band with a slightly contrasted background (e.g., soft gray or a very light brand tint) to separate it from the content sections above. At the center, a card-like container (approximately 60–70% of the page width on desktop, max-width ~800px) is centered both horizontally and visually within the band, with 32–40px of padding and gently rounded corners or a subtle shadow. Inside the card, the newsletter title and a short explanation are aligned to the top center, forming a compact text block. Below this, the actual form fields (email input and subscribe button) are arranged in a single horizontal row on larger screens—input occupying about 70% of the card width, button about 30%—with 8–16px spacing between them. On smaller devices, the form fields stack vertically, with the input field above the button, both stretched to nearly full card width and center-aligned. Generous whitespace above and below the card maintains the calm, corporate feel.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, centered call-to-action card that adapts its form layout from horizontal to vertical as screen width decreases.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The footer-navigation anchors the very bottom of the page as a full-width bar, echoing the header in structure but with slightly more height to accommodate multi-column content. It uses a darker background (possibly the same or slightly darker teal as the header) and extends edge-to-edge horizontally. Within it, a central content container (same 70–80% width, max-width ~1200px) is centered with 32–48px of vertical padding. At the top-left of this container, the logo or site name may appear again, with brief company details or tagline; navigation links are then arranged to the right in 2–4 vertical link columns, each column representing a navigation group (e.g., Services, Projects, Resources, Company). On desktop, these columns are aligned in a horizontal row with even spacing between them; on smaller screens, columns stack vertically, with the logo and any legal text moving to the bottom or top as needed. The footer content is left-aligned inside the container, while the entire bar feels visually grounded and heavier than the main page sections, serving as a clear endpoint.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static element fixed to the bottom of the document flow (not the viewport); remains at the end of the scroll, with its internal layout shifting from multi-column to stacked on smaller screens.

name: Services Overview
title: Services Overview
description: Page that loads and lists all consulting services, helps users narrow down by discipline, highlights a specific service via URL, and routes users to detailed service pages or the quote request form.

core_features: Fetch all consulting services using getServicesOverview and expose them to child components.
Display all consulting services in a filterable list, using ConsultingService fields such as name, slug, summary, and primaryDiscipline.
Allow users to filter services by primaryDiscipline and optionally focus on featured services only.
Support an incoming highlightServiceSlug URL parameter that scrolls to and visually emphasizes the matching service card.
Provide per-service navigation to each service’s detailed page using its pageFilename.
Provide a global 'Request a Quote' call-to-action that routes to quote_request.html without specifying a service.
Provide per-service 'Request a Quote' actions that route to quote_request.html and, where applicable, include a serviceType URL parameter derived from the selected service.
Offer a simple helper to narrow down services based on discipline, assisting users who are unsure which service to select.
user_workflows:
Workflow 1: Browse all services and open a detailed service page
1. User navigates to services.html without any URL parameters.
2. services-data-loader calls getServicesOverview and retrieves an array of ConsultingService items.
3. services-state-manager initializes default state (no filters, no highlight) because highlightServiceSlug is absent.
4. services-filter-bar shows available discipline options based on the primaryDiscipline values present in the loaded ConsultingService data.
5. services-list renders all services as service-card instances.
6. User clicks on a service card’s primary action (e.g., its title or 'View details').
7. The service-card onClickViewDetails handler navigates to the service’s detailed page using the ConsultingService.pageFilename (e.g., navigating to service_slope_stability.html or another filename provided by the API).

Workflow 2: Arrive with a specific service highlighted from another page
1. User clicks a link on index.html or about.html that points to services.html with a highlightServiceSlug URL parameter (e.g., services.html?highlightServiceSlug={some-slug}).
2. On page load, services-state-manager reads highlightServiceSlug from the URL and stores it in page state.
3. services-data-loader calls getServicesOverview and passes the loaded data to services-list and service-highlight-scroller.
4. service-highlight-scroller waits until the list is rendered, then locates the service whose ConsultingService.slug matches highlightServiceSlug.
5. service-highlight-scroller scrolls the corresponding service-card into view and triggers a highlight state (e.g., a flag passed to that card).
6. The highlighted service-card renders in a visually emphasized state (implementation-specific) but remains functionally identical.
7. If the user changes filters in services-filter-bar such that the highlighted service is filtered out, the highlight state is preserved in URL/state but the card is not displayed until filters are cleared or adjusted to include it again.

Workflow 3: Filter services by discipline and featured status
1. After services have loaded, the user interacts with services-filter-bar.
2. User selects a discipline value from a dropdown (values match ConsultingService.primaryDiscipline enum values present in the data).
3. services-filter-bar triggers onChangePrimaryDiscipline, which updates the URL (e.g., services.html?primaryDiscipline=geotechnical_engineering[&highlightServiceSlug=...]) and notifies services-state-manager.
4. services-state-manager updates in-memory filter state and passes the active filter criteria to services-list.
5. services-list recomputes the visible subset of ConsultingService items whose primaryDiscipline matches the active filter.
6. Optionally, the user toggles a 'featured only' control in services-filter-bar.
7. onToggleFeaturedOnly updates the URL (e.g., &featuredOnly=true) and filter state, and services-list restricts services to those with isFeatured=true.
8. User can click 'Clear all filters' in services-filter-bar, which removes filter-related URL parameters and restores the full list.

Workflow 4: Get help choosing a service via a simple discipline-based helper
1. User is unsure which service is appropriate and focuses on the service-selection-helper component.
2. service-selection-helper presents discipline-oriented options derived from the ConsultingService.primaryDiscipline values available in the dataset.
3. User clicks on one of the discipline options.
4. service-selection-helper triggers onSelectDisciplineOption, which delegates to services-state-manager’s onChangePrimaryDiscipline.
5. The page state and URL are updated with the chosen discipline filter, and services-list updates to show only services in that discipline.
6. User scans the filtered services and either opens a detailed page from a service-card or uses that card’s 'Request a Quote' action.

Workflow 5: Request a generic quote from the overview page
1. User decides to request a quote without choosing a specific service.
2. User clicks the global CTA in page-level-quote-cta.
3. onClickRequestQuoteGeneric triggers navigation to quote_request.html without additional URL parameters.
4. The quote_request.html page initializes in a generic mode, allowing the user to select the serviceType there.

Workflow 6: Request a quote for a specific service directly from its summary card
1. User identifies a suitable service in services-list.
2. Within the corresponding service-card, the user clicks the 'Request a Quote' action.
3. service-card onClickRequestQuote inspects the ConsultingService instance.
4. If the service corresponds to a known serviceType (e.g., based on pageFilename or slug matching a configured rule), the handler navigates to quote_request.html with a serviceType query parameter. For example:
   - If the service’s ConsultingService.pageFilename corresponds to the slope stability detail page (e.g., service_slope_stability.html), navigate to quote_request.html?serviceType=slope_stability_embankments.
   - If the service’s ConsultingService.pageFilename corresponds to the subsurface investigation detail page (e.g., service_subsurface_investigation.html), navigate to quote_request.html?serviceType=subsurface_investigation.
5. If there is no known mapping between the selected ConsultingService and a serviceType, onClickRequestQuote falls back to navigating to quote_request.html without a serviceType parameter.
6. The quote_request.html page preselects or configures the form based on the provided serviceType, if present.

Workflow 7: Use browser navigation and URL parameters to restore state
1. User applies filters and/or has a highlighted service (via highlightServiceSlug) while browsing the services list.
2. User navigates away from services.html to another page (e.g., a service detail or quote_request.html) and then uses the browser Back button.
3. When services.html is reactivated, services-state-manager re-reads all relevant URL parameters (highlightServiceSlug, primaryDiscipline, featuredOnly, and any view-related parameters) from the location.
4. services-data-loader re-fetches data via getServicesOverview (or uses cached data, depending on implementation), then passes it to the child components.
5. Filters are reapplied, and service-highlight-scroller re-applies highlighting and scrolling according to the highlightServiceSlug value, thus restoring the user’s context.
interactions: Click on discipline filter (services-filter-bar): Updates the primaryDiscipline filter state, writes the selection to the URL query string, and triggers re-rendering of the filtered services list.

Toggle 'featured only' (services-filter-bar): Switches a boolean filter that restricts the list to ConsultingService items with isFeatured=true, synchronizing this state with a URL parameter (e.g., featuredOnly=true/false).
Click 'Clear all filters' (services-filter-bar): Clears all active filters, removes related URL parameters (e.g., primaryDiscipline, featuredOnly), and restores the full services list.
Click on a discipline option in service-selection-helper: Delegates to the same handler as the discipline filter, updating the discipline filter state and URL, and narrowing the services list.

Click on a service card’s title or 'View details' action (service-card): Navigates to the service detail page using ConsultingService.pageFilename. The navigation may include the ConsultingService.slug as a URL parameter if needed by the target page.

Click on 'Request a Quote' within a service card (service-card): Navigates to quote_request.html. If the selected ConsultingService can be mapped to a specific serviceType (e.g., subsurface_investigation, slope_stability_embankments), the navigation includes that serviceType as a URL parameter; otherwise it omits the parameter for a generic quote.
Click on the global 'Request a Quote' button (page-level-quote-cta): Navigates directly to quote_request.html without specifying any serviceType parameter.

Page load with highlightServiceSlug present (services-state-manager + service-highlight-scroller): Reads highlightServiceSlug from the URL, waits for data from getServicesOverview, then instructs service-highlight-scroller to scroll the matching service-card into view and flag it as highlighted.

Change in URL via browser navigation (Back/Forward) (services-state-manager): Re-reads all relevant URL parameters and reapplies filters and highlighting, triggering re-rendering of services-filter-bar and services-list to reflect the restored state.
Retry loading after an error (services-data-loader): If getServicesOverview fails, the component exposes a retry control; clicking it calls getServicesOverview again.
page_functionality.state_logic:
The page state is driven entirely by URL parameters and data from getServicesOverview, without any localStorage or other persistent client storage.

Incoming URL parameters:
- highlightServiceSlug (string, optional): If present, services-state-manager stores this value as the 'active highlight' key. After getServicesOverview resolves, service-highlight-scroller searches the loaded ConsultingService items for a matching slug. If found, it scrolls to and highlights that service-card. This parameter remains in the URL until explicitly removed (e.g., by navigation or manual URL editing).

Additional URL parameters managed by this page (introduced for functional state):
- primaryDiscipline (string, optional): Expected to match one of the ConsultingService.primaryDiscipline enum values present in the dataset. When present, services-state-manager filters the list of services to those with a matching primaryDiscipline. services-filter-bar and service-selection-helper both read this parameter to set their current selection.
- featuredOnly (string, optional): Interpreted as a boolean. When featuredOnly=true, services-state-manager filters the services to those where ConsultingService.isFeatured is true. Any other value (or absence of the parameter) is treated as false.
- viewMode (string, optional): Represents a purely client-side preference for how to arrange the service list (e.g., 'list' or 'grid'). services-state-manager uses this value to configure services-list. Changing view mode updates this parameter; reading it on load restores the user’s preferred presentation without affecting the data set.

State initialization and synchronization:
1. On initial page load, services-state-manager parses the URL query string for highlightServiceSlug, primaryDiscipline, featuredOnly, and viewMode, storing them in a central state object.
2. services-data-loader calls getServicesOverview to obtain an array of ConsultingService items and passes them, along with the current state filters, to services-list and related components.
3. services-list computes a derived 'visibleServices' collection based on primaryDiscipline and featuredOnly state, and renders one service-card per item.
4. service-highlight-scroller responds to the combination of (a) data loaded and (b) highlightServiceSlug state set, performing the scroll and highlight behavior.
5. Any user action that changes filters, highlight, or view mode updates the central state and writes the corresponding parameters back into the URL (using history.pushState/replaceState or equivalent). This ensures that the visible state of the page is always reproducible from the URL alone.
6. When the browser Back/Forward buttons change the URL, a listener in services-state-manager re-parses the URL and updates the state, causing all dependent components (filters, list, highlighter) to re-render accordingly.

components.functionality: Initializes data loading for the Services Overview page by calling getServicesOverview, manages loading and error states, and exposes the resulting ConsultingService array to child components.

Centralizes page state derived from URL parameters (highlightServiceSlug, primaryDiscipline, featuredOnly, viewMode), synchronizes that state back to the URL when user interactions occur, and exposes the resulting filter and view configuration to other components.

Provides interactive controls to filter the services list by primaryDiscipline and featured status, and optionally switch view modes. It reads available discipline options from the ConsultingService dataset and reflects the current filter state derived from services-state-manager.

Offers a simplified way for users to narrow down services by discipline when they are unsure which specific service they need. It presents options based on available ConsultingService.primaryDiscipline values and delegates selection to the main filter logic.

Renders the filtered collection of ConsultingService items as a list or grid of service-card components, based on current filter and viewMode state. It determines which services are visible from the full dataset and passes highlight flags to individual cards.

Displays a single ConsultingService item, including its name, summary, and primaryDiscipline. Provides actions to open the detailed service page and to request a quote for that service. Supports a 'highlighted' state when its ConsultingService.slug matches highlightServiceSlug.

Listens for changes to highlightServiceSlug and the completion of data loading; when both are available, locates the matching ConsultingService by slug and scrolls the corresponding service-card into view, also triggering its highlighted state.
Provides a page-level 'Request a Quote' button that routes users directly to quote_request.html without specifying a serviceType, enabling generic quote requests.

layout.grid_system: Implied 12‑column responsive grid (typical WordPress/corporate layout) with a full-width container for the hero and a centered content column.
layout.layout_pattern: The page follows a linear, top-to-bottom structure mirroring the mockup: a fixed-height full-width header at the very top, followed by a full-bleed hero that fills the remainder of the initial viewport. Below the hero, all functional page components (filters, helper, list, CTA) are arranged in a single centered content column aligned to an implied 12‑column grid (content spanning roughly 8–10 columns on desktop). Within this column, components stack in this order: services-filter-bar, service-selection-helper, services-list (containing repeated service-card elements), and finally the page-level-quote-cta as a bottom-band section.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page follows a linear, top-to-bottom structure mirroring the mockup: a fixed-height full-width header at the very top, followed by a full-bleed hero that fills the remainder of the initial viewport. Below the hero, all functional page components (filters, helper, list, CTA) are arranged in a single centered content column aligned to an implied 12‑column grid (content spanning roughly 8–10 columns on desktop). Within this column, components stack in this order: services-filter-bar, service-selection-helper, services-list (containing repeated service-card elements), and finally the page-level-quote-cta as a bottom-band section.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The header occupies around 10–15% of the viewport height, the hero approximately the remaining 85–90% of the first screen. Below the hero, the core services content column is centered and spans about 70–80% of the viewport width, with generous left/right margins on large screens. Vertical spacing between major sections (filter bar, helper panel, list, CTA) uses 48–64px gaps, while spacing between related elements (cards within the list, controls within the filter bar) uses 16–32px, keeping alignment with the 8px spacing scale.
layout_strategies.content_density: The layout maintains a moderately spacious density: few high-priority components per vertical slice, with ample white space. The filter bar is visually clear and not crowded, the decision helper reads as a single, compact card, and each service card has comfortable padding and breathing room around its text. The list may be long, but individual cards are not overly dense; they use large, legible type and clear hierarchy rather than packing too much information. The page-level quote CTA is bold but uncluttered, echoing the hero’s simple, focused messaging.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: Visual flow follows a hybrid F/Z pattern adapted to this page: logo and navigation in the fixed header establish the top horizontal scan, which then drops into the hero’s centered title and supporting line. From there, attention moves downward into the services domain: first to the centrally aligned filter bar, then slightly down to the service-selection-helper card, and then into the vertically stacked service cards. A highlighted service (via URL) is automatically scrolled into a central on-screen position, becoming the focal point in the mid-screen area. Finally, as users reach the end of the list, the eye lands on the strong, horizontally centered page-level quote CTA that spans the width of the content band (or full-width on large screens).

layout.overall_description: The Services Overview page sits within a clean, corporate framework: a fixed, full-width header with the firm’s logo on the left and evenly spaced navigation on the right, sitting over a full-bleed hero with a technical, geotechnical-themed background image. In the hero, a compact category pill (e.g., “Services”) appears near the top of the visual center, followed by a large, multi-line headline that clearly states the consulting offer, and a compact line of supporting information beneath. Below this hero, the page content snaps into a centered column sitting on a light background, shielded from the viewport edges. At the top of this column, a prominent but tidy filter bar stretches horizontally, allowing users to narrow services by discipline. Immediately below, a card-like decision helper panel offers guidance for users who are unsure which service they need. Beneath that, a vertical stack of generously spaced service cards runs down the page, each card occupying the full width of the content column. When a specific service is referenced in the URL, the corresponding card is smoothly scrolled into the middle of the viewport and visually emphasized. After the list, a bold, full-width quote CTA band anchors the bottom of the page, re-introducing the brand color in a solid or subtly textured block and centering a concise message with a clear call-to-action button, echoing the hero’s clarity and focus.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The services-data-loader is a non-visual structural layer that conceptually wraps the main services content area. In the layout tree, it sits directly beneath the hero section and above the services-list, spanning the full width of the centered content column. It aligns with the same left and right margins as the filter bar and list, but occupies no visible height or space on its own. As data loads, the vertical footprint of this area corresponds to the combined height of the services-list (and any loading skeletons) within the content column, maintaining the clean, uninterrupted visual column from the filter bar down to the bottom CTA.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual, zero-height wrapper that expands only as its child content (primarily the services-list and any loading state elements) grows; it scrolls naturally with the page and has no fixed or sticky behavior.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The services-state-manager is also non-visual and sits logically at the top of the services content column, just beneath the hero and in parallel with the services-data-loader. It shares the same horizontal alignment as the main content column (centered on the page, with wide left/right gutters) but does not introduce any additional visible spacing. Its presence does not alter the visual rhythm; instead, it quietly synchronizes the URL state with filters and highlighted service cards while the user scrolls through the column of content.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual controller anchored near the top of the services content region; it moves with the page scroll and has no sticky or floating behavior, acting purely as a logical layer attached to the main column.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The services-filter-bar is the first visible interactive element beneath the hero, occupying the full width of the centered content column. On desktop, it spans roughly 70–80% of the viewport width (aligned to about 8–10 columns of a 12-column grid) and is horizontally centered, with consistent left and right margins echoing the hero’s text column. Vertically, it sits about 48–64px below the bottom of the hero, creating a clear separation between marketing messaging and functional filtering. The bar itself is a horizontal strip about 56–72px tall, with 16–24px of internal vertical padding and 24–32px horizontal padding, containing filter controls (pills, dropdowns, and/or a search field) laid out in a left-to-right flow. The overall look is clean and uncluttered, with sufficient spacing between controls (8–16px) so that the bar feels ordered and easy to scan, rather than dense. On smaller screens, the filter bar still spans nearly the full width, with inner padding preserved and controls wrapping to new lines while maintaining clear vertical separation between control rows.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Sticky within the scrolling viewport: once the user scrolls past the hero, the filter bar pins itself directly beneath the fixed global header and remains visible at the top of the content area as the services-list scrolls underneath. It respects the header height offset and maintains its full width within the content column while sticky.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The service-selection-helper appears immediately below the services-filter-bar, separated from it by a modest vertical gap of approximately 16–24px to visually tie them together as part of the same ‘decision’ zone. It is a full-width card within the same centered content column, matching the filter bar’s left and right alignment. The card has a light, neutral background (or very subtle tint), 24px internal padding on all sides, and rounded corners (e.g., 8px radius) to differentiate it from the page background while still feeling understated. Its height is compact—roughly 120–200px depending on content—with a short headline at the top and supporting text or a compact control (e.g., a small question flow entry point) arranged vertically. The helper does not compete with the primary list; instead, it reads as a guiding panel users encounter on their first scroll past the hero and filter bar. On mobile, it stretches width-wise to nearly edge-to-edge, with consistent 16–24px outer margins and slightly reduced vertical spacing to keep the layout efficient while maintaining breathing room.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the document flow, positioned between the sticky filter bar and the services-list. It scrolls away with the content as the user moves down the page; there is no sticky or floating behavior, ensuring that only the filter bar remains persistently in view.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The services-list occupies the main vertical body of the page beneath the service-selection-helper. It begins approximately 24–32px below the helper card to clearly separate ‘guidance’ from ‘results’ while preserving continuity. The list is a single, vertical stack of service-card components, each card spanning the full width of the centered content column. On desktop, this column sits centered with generous left and right gutters, matching the width of the filter bar and helper. Each service-card is separated from the next by 24–32px of vertical white space, creating a rhythm of clearly segmented yet cohesive items. As the user scrolls, this list constitutes the dominant visual content of the page, potentially extending well beyond the initial viewport. On tablet and mobile, the list maintains a single-column structure, occupying most of the screen width but preserving 16–24px side margins to keep the cards from touching screen edges.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, vertically scrolling container; it expands to accommodate the total number of service-card items. The list itself does not stick or float, but it collaborates with the service-highlight-scroller, which can programmatically adjust the scroll position to bring a specific service-card into view within this list.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Each service-card is a full-width card within the services-list, aligning flush with the list’s left and right bounds inside the centered content column. Horizontally, the card uses 24px internal padding on all sides and a subtle elevation (thin border or soft shadow) with an 8px corner radius, standing off from the page background while maintaining a clean, corporate feel. On desktop, the internal layout can follow a subtle two-column pattern: the left two-thirds devoted to text (service name in larger type, brief description, key bullet points), and the right one-third reserved for secondary content such as a small technical graphic, icon, or discipline label stack. The primary CTA (e.g., “View details” or “Request quote”) sits toward the bottom-right of the text block, aligned along a consistent vertical axis across cards, reinforcing scanability. Card height is flexible but typically falls around 200–260px for average content. On narrower screens, the card reflows into a single-column layout: the graphic or meta content moves below the text, maintaining 16–24px spacing between text blocks. The card for the currently highlighted service (derived from the URL state) receives an additional visual accent: a slightly thicker or colored left border using the brand teal, a subtle background tint, or a faint glow, drawing attention without overwhelming the overall minimalist aesthetic.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the vertical list; each card scrolls naturally with the page. When the service-highlight-scroller is triggered (e.g., from a URL parameter or interaction elsewhere), the targeted card becomes the destination anchor: the page scrolls so that this card is positioned in the central 40–60% of the viewport, accounting for the fixed header and sticky filter bar. The highlighted card may also animate lightly (e.g., a brief background color fade) to reinforce its prominence.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The service-highlight-scroller is non-visual but directly influences how users perceive the layout of the services-list. It operates in the background, monitoring URL state or interactions and then adjusting the page scroll position to bring the relevant service-card into the ideal viewing zone. Spatially, it targets the vertical region just below the sticky filter bar so that the selected card sits comfortably in the middle of the viewport, within the central 40–60% of the screen height. This ensures that the card is neither cramped at the very top under the header nor partially off-screen, and that surrounding cards above and below remain visible to preserve context.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual scroll manager that performs smooth, eased scrolling to align a highlighted service-card within the viewport, using offsets for the fixed header and sticky filter bar. It does not occupy layout space but dynamically manipulates scroll position and can trigger transient visual emphasis on the target card.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The page-level-quote-cta anchors the bottom of the Services Overview page as the final major section. It appears below the last service-card in the services-list, separated by a generous vertical gap of approximately 48–64px to clearly signal a transition from browsing to conversion. On desktop, this CTA can either occupy the full viewport width as a full-bleed band or sit within a slightly narrower max-width container; in either case, the inner content column aligns with the same centered width as the services content above. The band’s height is visually substantial but not overpowering—roughly 25–35% of the viewport height—with a solid brand-color background (e.g., teal) or a subtle technical image overlay at low opacity. The call-to-action content is vertically and horizontally centered within the band: a concise, high-contrast heading at the top, a short supporting line beneath it, and a prominent button (e.g., “Request a Quote”) directly below, all center-aligned. Internal vertical spacing follows the 16–32px scale, keeping the section visually bold yet simple. On mobile, the band reduces in height but maintains full-width coverage, with the text and button stacked in a centered column and adequate padding (24–32px) from the edges.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static section at the end of the page’s main scroll. It does not stick or float by default but is designed to be a strong visual endpoint. On very long lists, it becomes the final destination of the scroll and can be brought into full view by a scroll-to-CTA interaction elsewhere on the page, reinforcing its role as the primary conversion target after users have explored the services.

name: Slope Stability & Retaining Walls Service
title: Slope Stability & Retaining Walls Service
description: Functional service page that loads slope stability & retaining wall service content via getSlopeStabilityServicePage, presents methods, applications, deliverables, and related case studies/resources, and routes users to quote and consultation flows with appropriate URL parameters.

core_features: Load slope stability & retaining wall service data (service metadata, overview HTML, typical applications, methods, deliverables, related case studies, related resources) using getSlopeStabilityServicePage on page load.
Display a structured service overview including service name, summary, and HTML-formatted technical overview content.
List typical applications of slope stability and retaining wall services (e.g., embankments, landslides, retaining structures) using the typicalApplications array from the interface.
List analysis and design methods used for slope stability and retaining walls using the methods array from the interface.
List typical deliverables for slope stability projects using the deliverables array from the interface.
Show related slope stability and retaining wall case studies using relatedCaseStudies (ProjectCaseStudy entities) with navigation to individual project_detail pages.
Show related slope stability and retaining wall resources using relatedResources (ResourceItem entities) with navigation to individual resource_detail pages.
Provide a primary 'Get a Quote' call-to-action that navigates to quote_request.html with serviceType pre-set to slope_stability_embankments via URL parameter.
Provide a secondary 'Book a slope stability consultation' call-to-action that navigates to consultation.html with specialty pre-set to slope_stability via URL parameter.

Provide navigation links from this page to filtered project and resource listings: slope stability projects (projects.html?foundationType=slope_stabilization), slope stability resources (resources.html?topic=slope_stability), and retaining wall design guides & checklists (resources.html?topic=retaining_walls).
Adjust minor UI aspects (such as contextual back-links or emphasis of certain sections) based on the incoming ref URL parameter without changing the underlying data.
user_workflows:
Explore service, then request a quote:
1. User navigates to service_slope_stability.html (optionally with ?ref=home or another ref value).
2. The page triggers getSlopeStabilityServicePage and shows loading state until data is returned.
3. Service overview, applications, methods, and deliverables are rendered from the interface response.
4. User reviews the content and clicks the primary 'Get a Quote' button.
5. The click handler navigates the browser to quote_request.html with serviceType=slope_stability_embankments in the query string. The handler may also append the existing ref parameter to the destination URL if present (e.g., quote_request.html?serviceType=slope_stability_embankments&ref=home).
6. quote_request.html then uses serviceType to preconfigure its form for slope stability embankments (outside the scope of this page).

Explore related case studies:
1. User scrolls to the related case studies section, populated using relatedCaseStudies from getSlopeStabilityServicePage.
2. The user scans case study cards (each bound to a ProjectCaseStudy.id).
3. The user clicks on a specific case study card.
4. The click handler navigates to project_detail.html?id=<ProjectCaseStudy.id>.
5. Alternatively, the user clicks 'View slope stability projects', and the click handler navigates to projects.html?foundationType=slope_stabilization to view a filtered list of projects.

Explore related resources and retaining wall guides:
1. User scrolls to the related resources section, populated using relatedResources from getSlopeStabilityServicePage.
2. The user scans resource items (each bound to a ResourceItem.id).
3. The user clicks a resource card, and the click handler navigates to resource_detail.html?id=<ResourceItem.id>.
4. The user can click 'View all slope stability resources' to navigate to resources.html?topic=slope_stability.
5. The user can click 'Retaining wall design guides & checklists' to navigate to resources.html?topic=retaining_walls.

Book a slope stability consultation:
1. User reviews service content and decides they want to speak with an expert.
2. User clicks the 'Book a slope stability consultation' button.
3. The click handler navigates to consultation.html?specialty=slope_stability (optionally including &ref=<ref> if the page was opened with a ref parameter).
4. The consultation page then filters/initializes available slots for slope stability specialists (out of scope for this page).

Ref-dependent contextual flow:
1. User arrives from a known source, e.g., services.html, with URL service_slope_stability.html?ref=services.
2. On page load, the state logic reads ref=services from the URL.
3. A small contextual UI element (e.g., a back link or contextual label) is shown or configured to direct the user back to services.html.
4. When the user clicks outbound CTAs (Get a Quote, View slope stability projects, View resources, Book consultation), the navigation handlers include ref=services in the target URL where applicable to preserve referral context.
interactions: Page load: On initial load of service_slope_stability.html, the page reads the ref query parameter (if present) and invokes getSlopeStabilityServicePage. It manages loading and error states until data is successfully returned.

Section navigation: User can scroll or use internal navigation (e.g., section links) to jump between overview, applications, methods, deliverables, case studies, and resources. Section visibility and ordering are static, but content within is dynamic from the interface.

Get a Quote button click: User clicks the primary 'Get a Quote' button. Event handler composes a URL to quote_request.html with serviceType=slope_stability_embankments and optional ref propagation, then performs client-side navigation.
Book consultation button click: User clicks 'Book a slope stability consultation'. Event handler composes URL consultation.html?specialty=slope_stability and optional ref propagation, then navigates.
Case study card click: User clicks on a related case study card. Event handler reads the card’s bound ProjectCaseStudy.id and navigates to project_detail.html?id=<id>.
View all slope stability projects link click: User clicks 'View slope stability projects'. Event handler navigates to projects.html?foundationType=slope_stabilization.
Resource card click: User clicks on a related resource card. Event handler reads the bound ResourceItem.id and navigates to resource_detail.html?id=<id>.
View all slope stability resources link click: User clicks 'View all slope stability resources'. Event handler navigates to resources.html?topic=slope_stability.
Retaining wall design guides link click: User clicks 'Retaining wall design guides & checklists'. Event handler navigates to resources.html?topic=retaining_walls.
page_functionality.state_logic: The page state is driven by URL parameters and the response from getSlopeStabilityServicePage. Incoming state: The page accepts an optional ref query parameter (e.g., service_slope_stability.html?ref=services). On load, the router or initialization logic parses the current URL to extract ref. This value is stored in in-memory page state (not persisted to localStorage) and is used to adjust minor UI elements (such as displaying a contextual back link to the referring section) and to optionally propagate ref to outbound navigation URLs. Data loading state: After reading URL parameters, the page calls getSlopeStabilityServicePage with no arguments. Before the response returns, a loading state flag (e.g., isLoading) is set to true; on success, isLoading is set to false and the interface data (service, overviewHtml, typicalApplications, methods, deliverables, relatedCaseStudies, relatedResources) is stored in state. On error, an error flag (e.g., hasError) is set, and a non-data-dependent error message is displayed. Rendering state: The visibility of content sections depends on the presence and length of the corresponding arrays or fields from getSlopeStabilityServicePage. For example, if typicalApplications is empty or undefined, the applications section is hidden; the same applies to methods, deliverables, relatedCaseStudies, and relatedResources. Navigation state: Outbound links constructed by event handlers always use URL query parameters as specified: quote_request.html?serviceType=slope_stability_embankments[&ref=<ref>], projects.html?foundationType=slope_stabilization[&ref=<ref>], resources.html?topic=slope_stability[&ref=<ref>], resources.html?topic=retaining_walls[&ref=<ref>], project_detail.html?id=<projectId>[&ref=<ref>], resource_detail.html?id=<resourceId>[&ref=<ref>], consultation.html?specialty=slope_stability[&ref=<ref>]. The original ref value is not modified within this page and is never stored outside the URL or in persistent client storage.

components.functionality: Initializes the page by reading URL parameters (notably ref) and invoking getSlopeStabilityServicePage. Manages loading, success, and error states, and exposes the resulting data (service, overviewHtml, typicalApplications, methods, deliverables, relatedCaseStudies, relatedResources) to child components via a shared state or props mechanism.

Displays the core service metadata: service name, summary, and primary discipline. This component provides a concise identification of the service and may show a small contextual hint depending on the ref parameter (for example, indicating the user came from the services overview).

Renders the overviewHtml field from getSlopeStabilityServicePage as sanitized HTML, providing a detailed technical overview of the slope stability & retaining walls service. This may include paragraphs, lists, and inline formatting, all supplied dynamically by the interface.

Displays the typicalApplications array as a structured list. If the array is empty or missing, this component stays hidden. Each list item corresponds to one entry in typicalApplications, allowing users to quickly see common use cases for the service.

Displays the methods array from getSlopeStabilityServicePage as a list of analysis and design methods. The component is only rendered when methods contains at least one entry. It is purely informational and does not trigger navigation.
Displays the deliverables array from getSlopeStabilityServicePage as a list of typical project deliverables. If deliverables is empty or undefined, the component is not shown.

Provides the primary 'Get a Quote' call-to-action specific to slope stability and retaining walls. When the button is clicked, the component constructs a URL to quote_request.html with the query parameter serviceType=slope_stability_embankments and optionally appends the current ref parameter if present.

Provides a secondary call-to-action to book a slope stability consultation. On click, it navigates to consultation.html with specialty=slope_stability in the query string and optionally ref, allowing the consultation page to pre-filter availability slots for the appropriate specialty.

Displays a grid or list of related case studies using the relatedCaseStudies array from getSlopeStabilityServicePage. Each card shows key details from ProjectCaseStudy (such as title and summary, supplied dynamically) and is clickable. A footer link allows users to view all slope stability projects by navigating to projects.html with foundationType=slope_stabilization.

Displays a list or grid of related resources using the relatedResources array from getSlopeStabilityServicePage. Each item corresponds to a ResourceItem and is clickable to navigate to its detail page. The section also includes two navigation actions: 'View all slope stability resources' which goes to resources.html?topic=slope_stability, and 'Retaining wall design guides & checklists' which goes to resources.html?topic=retaining_walls.

Reads the ref parameter from the URL and, if present, renders a small contextual element such as a back-link or label indicating the user's navigation context (e.g., from services overview, from a project case study). It does not alter API data and only adjusts client-side navigation targets to optionally include ref.

layout.grid_system: Implied 12‑column responsive grid (typical WordPress/corporate layout) with a full-width container for the hero and a centered content column.
layout.layout_pattern: Below the site’s fixed full-width header, the page opens with a full-bleed hero that visually carries the service-header-summary content. Inside this hero, all key service information is stacked and center-aligned in a constrained central column: first a compact context/category pill (ref-context-banner), then the primary service title, then a short meta/summary line. Immediately below the hero, the content flows in a linear top-to-bottom structure: a concise overview, a prominent quote CTA, then deeper detail sections (applications, methods, deliverables), followed by proof/validation (related case studies, related resources) and a final consultation CTA near the bottom. All main sections sit within a centered content column that respects generous side margins.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: Below the site’s fixed full-width header, the page opens with a full-bleed hero that visually carries the service-header-summary content. Inside this hero, all key service information is stacked and center-aligned in a constrained central column: first a compact context/category pill (ref-context-banner), then the primary service title, then a short meta/summary line. Immediately below the hero, the content flows in a linear top-to-bottom structure: a concise overview, a prominent quote CTA, then deeper detail sections (applications, methods, deliverables), followed by proof/validation (related case studies, related resources) and a final consultation CTA near the bottom. All main sections sit within a centered content column that respects generous side margins.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The fixed global header occupies about 10–15% of the viewport height at the top. The hero (service-header-summary region) takes roughly the remaining initial viewport height (around 70–80% of the viewport), so the title and summary sit comfortably in the mid-screen area. Below the hero, each major section (overview, combined applications/methods band, deliverables, case studies, resources, bottom CTA) is separated by 48–64px of vertical spacing. Content is constrained to a central column (roughly 70–80% of the page width, max-width around 1100–1200px) with consistent internal padding of 16–24px, while the hero background image remains full-width edge-to-edge.
layout_strategies.content_density: The hero remains minimal and spacious: a small number of elements (context pill, title, summary/meta) set large, high-contrast type over a busy technical background. Below the fold, sections are moderately dense but never crowded—list sections (applications, methods, deliverables) use clear headings, comfortable line spacing, and sufficient separation between items. CTAs are concise, with short copy and one or two buttons, framed by ample padding and whitespace to preserve a corporate, easy-to-scan feel.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: Visually, the user’s eye follows the mockup’s F/Z pattern: it first lands on the global logo top-left and sweeps across the fixed navigation, then drops to the centrally positioned context pill (ref-context-banner) and large service title in the middle of the hero. From there, the eye moves down to the brief summary/meta line, then continues vertically into the overview and primary quote CTA. Below, headings for applications and methods create a subtle left-right zig-zag within a two-column band, before the flow returns to a single centered column for deliverables, case studies, resources, and the final consultation CTA, which serves as the closing focal point.

layout.overall_description: The page presents as a polished corporate service detail view. Under the fixed site header, a full-width hero image suggests geotechnical terrain—slopes, retaining structures, or soil strata—tinted with the brand’s teal overlay. Floating in the center of this hero is a clean content column: a compact, pill-shaped banner labels the context, followed by a bold, multi-line "Slope Stability & Retaining Walls" title and a concise one-line descriptor. This stack is vertically centered in the mid-portion of the viewport, surrounded by generous breathing room that contrasts with the technical background.
