Scrolling down, the background shifts to a plain, light surface, and the rest of the page flows in a clear, stacked sequence within a centered column: a succinct overview of the service, a highlighted quote CTA band, two balanced columns detailing applications and methods, a full-width deliverables list, then card-based grids of related case studies and resources. The layout ends with a strong, wide consultation CTA band that spans the content width, giving the impression of a confident, structured marketing page that guides users smoothly from understanding to action.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The service-content-loader is conceptually placed at the very top of the main content area, just below the global fixed header and above the visual hero content. It does not render a persistent visible block in the final layout; instead, during data fetching it temporarily occupies the same vertical space as the hero (service-header-summary), displaying skeleton lines or a subtle loading state centered within the hero’s content column. Horizontally, it spans the full browser width but constrains any loading indicators to the same central column (approximately 70–80% page width, max-width ~1100–1200px) used by the hero text so that the loading state aligns exactly where the title and summary will appear. Once content is loaded, it collapses to occupy no visible height, allowing the hero to sit directly under the global header.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Ephemeral and non-layout-breaking: overlays or temporarily replaces the hero content region while loading, then collapses away entirely. Does not scroll independently or remain sticky.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The service-header-summary forms the hero section. It is a full-bleed band directly below the fixed site header, with a background image related to slopes and retaining walls stretching from the left edge of the viewport to the right. The hero has a minimum height of about 70–80% of the viewport, so that on first load the user sees mostly the hero plus a hint of content below. In the center of this band, a vertically and horizontally centered content column (max-width ~960–1100px, occupying roughly 70% of the page width on large screens) contains all hero text. Within this column, elements are stacked and center-aligned: at the top of the text cluster sits the ref-context-banner as a compact pill; directly beneath it, with about 16–24px vertical gap, is the large multi-line service title (e.g., "Slope Stability & Retaining Walls"), set in high-contrast white or near-white. Below the title, separated by another 16–24px, is a single-line meta/summary row—slightly smaller text that might note the service category, typical project types, or regions served—kept to one line whenever possible for a compact appearance. The overall cluster of text occupies the central 40–60% of the vertical space of the hero, leaving generous top and bottom margins of image-only area that frames the content. Side margins of at least 48–64px ensure the text never approaches the edges, even on smaller screens.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, non-sticky hero section that scrolls away with the page. Background image behaves as a full-width, possibly fixed or softly parallaxed layer, while the content column remains centered and responsive.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The service-overview-section sits immediately below the hero, on a solid, light background that clearly differentiates it from the image-heavy header. There is a generous vertical gap of about 48–64px between the bottom edge of the hero and the start of this section, preserving a clear sectional break. The content is contained within the same central column used in the hero (max-width ~960–1100px, centered horizontally with equal left and right margins). Internally, the block has 24px of padding on all sides. At the top of this section, a section title such as "Service Overview" is left-aligned within the column, sitting about 8–16px above the body text. Below this heading, one to three short paragraphs and/or a compact bullet list explain the scope, value, and typical scenarios for slope stability and retaining walls. Text is left-aligned for readability, with line length kept to roughly 60–75 characters. The overall height is moderate, occupying roughly 25–35% of the viewport on a desktop, with 32–48px of whitespace below before the next CTA block.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block that scrolls normally within the main content column with no sticky or floating behavior.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The quote-cta-block appears directly beneath the service-overview-section as a prominent, yet compact, highlight band intended to be visible within or just below the initial scroll. It sits in the same central content column (max-width ~960–1100px), centered on the page, but uses a subtle brand-tinted background (e.g., a light teal) with rounded corners to stand out from the white/neutral page background. The block spans nearly the full width of the column, leaving 16–24px internal padding on all sides. Vertically, it is relatively shallow, roughly 15–20% of the viewport height, with comfortable breathing space. Inside, content is center-aligned: a concise headline like "Need a Slope Stability Assessment?" sits at the top, followed by a single short line of explanatory text, and then a primary button centered beneath. The button is sized to about one-third of the column width on desktop (capped at a practical max width) so it feels substantial but not full-width. There is about 16px between text and button, and 24px from button to the bottom edge of the block. Generous whitespace above and below this band (about 32px to the section above and 48px to the band below) ensures it reads as a standalone call to action without cluttering the layout.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, non-sticky CTA that remains in the content flow. It expands and contracts responsively with the central column but does not overlay other sections.
Static block that scrolls normally within the main content column with no sticky or floating behavior.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The applications-list is part of a two-column detail band positioned beneath the quote-cta-block, with approximately 48–64px of vertical spacing separating them. This band extends across the same central content width (max-width ~1100–1200px) but uses a 12-column grid to create two balanced columns. On desktop, the applications-list occupies the left column, spanning about 5 of 12 grid columns, with a 1-column gutter separating it from the methods-list on the right. The section starts with a left-aligned heading like "Applications" at the top-left of its column, followed by a vertically stacked list of application items (e.g., types of slopes, wall systems, or project contexts). Each list item is separated by 8–12px to maintain clarity without feeling dense. The height of this column is determined by the number of items; content is top-aligned so that the headings of both columns (applications and methods) are visually aligned horizontally. On mobile and narrow screens, the applications-list drops to a full-width block stacked above the methods-list, each maintaining the same internal padding and heading/list relationship.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static column within a responsive two-column layout; it reflows to full-width stacking on smaller screens while retaining its internal spacing.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The methods-list sits directly to the right of the applications-list in the same two-column band. On desktop, it takes up the right column, also spanning about 5 of 12 grid columns, mirroring the left side. A consistent 1-column gutter (roughly 24–32px depending on screen width) separates it from the applications-list. Its top edge aligns with the top of the applications-list so that both headings form a single horizontal line across the section. Inside this column, a left-aligned heading like "Methods" appears at the top-left, with a list of analytical and design methods (e.g., limit equilibrium, finite element analysis) stacked below. Each list item uses similar spacing to the applications-list (8–12px between items), maintaining a balanced visual weight between the two columns. On narrower screens, this component flows below the applications-list as a full-width block with the same max-width and internal padding, preserving the linear, easy-scanning structure.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static column within a responsive two-column layout; it aligns with and mirrors the applications-list, then stacks beneath it on smaller viewports.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The deliverables-list is positioned directly beneath the two-column applications/methods band, separated by a vertical gap of about 48px to clearly signal a new section. It returns to a single-column layout within the central content width (max-width ~960–1100px), centered horizontally with equal side margins. A left-aligned section heading such as "Deliverables" appears at the top, with 8–16px of space before the list itself. The list may be presented as a simple vertical bullet list or as a set of lightly separated rows; in either case, items are spaced 8–12px apart, with comfortable line spacing to keep the section readable and uncluttered. Internally, the block has 24px padding, and the entire section occupies roughly 20–30% of the viewport height depending on content length. The overall shape is a clean, rectangular band that feels integrated with the previous detail sections but distinct enough to be scannable on its own.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, full-width (within the content column) block that scrolls normally, with no sticky or floating elements.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The related-case-studies-section follows the deliverables-list, with approximately 64px of vertical separation to create a clear transition into proof/portfolio content. It uses the full central content width (max-width ~1100–1200px) and adopts a grid layout of cards. At the top of the section, a center-aligned heading like "Related Case Studies" spans the full content width, with 16–24px of whitespace below it. Under this heading, case study cards are arranged in a responsive grid—typically three columns on desktop, each card occupying about 3–4 of 12 grid columns with consistent horizontal gutters of 16–24px and vertical spacing of about 24–32px between rows. Each card includes a compact image or colored header band, a short title, a one-line description, and a subtle link or button. The grid maintains generous surrounding padding (24px inside the section) so that, despite multiple cards, the section retains a clean, organized marketing feel. On smaller screens, the layout collapses to two columns and then a single column, keeping card width comfortable for reading.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static grid section that responds to viewport width by adjusting the number of columns; it scrolls as part of the main page flow.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The related-resources-section is placed directly beneath the related-case-studies-section with about 48–64px of vertical spacing, continuing the evidence/support narrative. It mirrors the structural approach of the case studies: full-width within the central content column (max-width ~1100–1200px) with a center-aligned heading such as "Related Resources" at the top, followed by a grid of resource tiles or cards. These cards may represent technical notes, whitepapers, or blog posts. They are arranged in a two- or three-column grid on desktop, depending on available width, with consistent horizontal gutters (16–24px) and vertical spacing (24–32px) between rows. Each card is visually slightly lighter-weight than case study cards—perhaps simpler typography and less imagery—to reinforce their tertiary informational role. The total section height is moderate to high depending on the number of resources, but generous top and bottom margins prevent the area from feeling cramped before the final CTA.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, responsive grid that scrolls with the rest of the page and reflows from multiple columns to a single column on small screens.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The consultation-cta-block serves as the final, strong call to action at the bottom of the page, positioned after the related resources with a comfortable 48–64px vertical gap. It spans the central content width but is visually treated as a more substantial band than the quote CTA: a slightly darker brand-tinted or muted teal background that stands out from preceding white/neutral sections. Internally, it has 32–40px of vertical padding and 24px side padding. Content is arranged in a horizontal layout on desktop: a left-aligned headline such as "Talk to a Geotechnical Engineer" and one-line supporting text occupy the left two-thirds of the band, while a single prominent button ("Schedule Consultation") sits right-aligned in the remaining third. This creates a gentle Z-shaped eye movement across the band. On smaller screens, the layout stacks: headline and text appear at the top, with the button centered below, separated by about 16px. The band visually anchors the page, occupying roughly 20–25% of the viewport height and providing a clear endpoint and next step.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static bottom-of-page CTA band that scrolls into view; it is not sticky but visually anchors the end of the content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The ref-context-banner is integrated into the hero as the compact category-like pill at the top of the hero’s text stack. Visually, it appears within the service-header-summary region, centered horizontally within the same text column and positioned just above the main service title. It is a small, rounded-rectangle badge, approximately 25–35% of the column’s width on desktop (auto-sizing to content), with 8–12px vertical padding and 16–24px horizontal padding. The background is a semi-opaque, slightly darker or lighter teal than the hero overlay, and the text is concise (e.g., "Service: Slope Stability" or project/reference context), set in small uppercase or semi-bold type. There is a 8–16px vertical gap between the bottom of this banner and the top of the title, so the pill reads as a discrete label rather than part of the heading body. Vertically, the ref-context-banner participates in the same central cluster that occupies the middle 40–60% of the hero height, with ample image space above and below. On mobile, it remains centered and shrinks to fit the narrower column while preserving the pill shape and spacing.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static element within the hero text stack; it scrolls with the hero and does not behave independently (no stickiness or floating outside the hero region).

name: Subsurface Investigation Service
title: Subsurface Investigation Service
description: Functional service page that loads subsurface investigation content, shows capabilities and typical use cases, and provides a filterable list of investigation packages with navigation to package details and a custom quote request.

core_features: Load and display subsurface investigation service overview, capabilities, and use cases via getSubsurfaceInvestigationServicePage.

Initialize and display investigation package filter options (project type and price range) using getInvestigationPackagesFilterOptions, merged with incoming URL parameters and initialFilterDefaults from getSubsurfaceInvestigationServicePage.

Render a Packages & Pricing section that lists investigation packages, filtered by project type and maximum price, using data from getSubsurfaceInvestigationServicePage.initialPackages or getInvestigationPackages.
Provide interactive filters that update the URL query parameters (projectType, maxPrice) and refresh the package list via getInvestigationPackages.
Allow users to open a specific investigation package detail page by clicking on a package card title or 'View Details', navigating to package_detail.html with packageId in the query string.
Offer a 'Request custom investigation quote' call-to-action that navigates to quote_request.html with serviceType=subsurface_investigation in the query string.
user_workflows:
Workflow 1 – Arrive with pre-selected project type and budget:
1. User navigates from index.html, services.html, or projects.html to service_subsurface_investigation.html with optional projectType and/or maxPrice in the URL query.
2. On page initialization, the page reads the URL query parameters projectType and maxPrice.
3. The page calls getSubsurfaceInvestigationServicePage to retrieve service overview content, capabilities, typical use cases, packagesIntroText, initialFilterDefaults, and initialPackages.
4. In parallel, the page calls getInvestigationPackagesFilterOptions to retrieve the available projectTypes (values and labels) and priceRange (minPrice, maxPrice, currency).
5. The page determines effective filter values by reconciling URL parameters with allowed filter options and initialFilterDefaults, then sets the filter controls accordingly.
6. If the effective filters match initialFilterDefaults and URL parameters did not override them, the page uses initialPackages for the package list; otherwise it calls getInvestigationPackages with the effective filters.
7. The user sees a pre-filtered package list matching their project type and budget without additional input.

Workflow 2 – Adjust filters to find suitable packages:
1. User changes the project type using the project type control in the Packages & Pricing filter bar.
2. The filter component updates the internal filter state and updates the URL query parameter projectType to the selected value (removing it if the user chooses an "all"-type option, if supported by filter options).
3. The filter component calls getInvestigationPackages with projectType set to the selected value, maxPrice set to the current maximum price filter, minPrice derived from the current minimum price setting (from priceRange), and onlyActive=true.
4. The package list component receives the new array of InvestigationPackage items and re-renders the package cards.
5. User optionally adjusts the maximum price filter, which updates the URL parameter maxPrice and triggers another getInvestigationPackages call, further refining the list.
6. If no packages match the chosen filters, the page displays an empty-state message and prominently shows the custom quote call-to-action.

Workflow 3 – View details of a specific investigation package:
1. User scans the filtered investigation package cards in the Packages & Pricing section.
2. The user clicks either the package title or the 'View Details' action on a specific card.
3. The click handler reads the id of the selected InvestigationPackage from the bound data and navigates to package_detail.html, appending ?packageId=<selected_package_id> to the URL.
4. The new page (package_detail.html) can then use packageId to load detailed information about the selected investigation package.

Workflow 4 – Request a custom investigation quote:
1. User reviews the filtered package list and determines that none of the packaged options fully fits their project requirements or budget.
2. User clicks on the 'Request custom investigation quote' call-to-action on the page (available regardless of whether packages are shown).
3. The click handler navigates to quote_request.html with ?serviceType=subsurface_investigation in the URL query string.
4. The quote request page can then use serviceType to pre-select the appropriate service type in the quote form.

Workflow 5 – Refresh / share page state using URL parameters:
1. User refines filters (project type and max price) until they find a suitable set of packages.
2. The page keeps the current filters synchronized into the URL query parameters projectType and maxPrice.
3. The user copies the page URL and shares it with a colleague or saves it for later.
4. When the URL is opened again, the initialization process reads projectType and maxPrice from the query string, re-applies them to the filter controls, and fetches or uses appropriately filtered packages, recreating the same view the user had when copying the link.
interactions: On page load, the page reads URL query parameters projectType (string) and maxPrice (number) and passes them into the initial filter resolution logic before calling getInvestigationPackages as needed.

On page initialization, the service-content loader calls getSubsurfaceInvestigationServicePage (no parameters) to obtain service content, capabilities, typical use cases, initialFilterDefaults, and initialPackages.
On page initialization, the filter-options loader calls getInvestigationPackagesFilterOptions (no parameters) to obtain available projectTypes and priceRange for configuring the filter controls.

When the user changes the project type filter control, the packages-filter-bar component updates the internal filter state, updates the projectType query parameter in the URL, and triggers a new getInvestigationPackages call with projectType set to the chosen value and other filter values preserved.

When the user adjusts the maximum price filter (e.g., slider or numeric input), the packages-filter-bar component validates the input against priceRange, updates the maxPrice query parameter in the URL, and triggers a new getInvestigationPackages call with the updated maxPrice.

When the user clears or resets filters (if implemented), the filter component removes projectType and maxPrice from the URL query string, restores defaults derived from initialFilterDefaults and priceRange, and triggers getInvestigationPackages or reuses initialPackages when appropriate.

When the user clicks on a package card title or 'View Details' button, the package-card component reads the InvestigationPackage.id from its bound data and navigates the browser to package_detail.html?packageId=<id>.
When the user clicks the 'Request custom investigation quote' call-to-action, the custom-quote-cta component navigates the browser to quote_request.html?serviceType=subsurface_investigation.

If getSubsurfaceInvestigationServicePage or getInvestigationPackagesFilterOptions fails or returns incomplete data, the page shows an error or fallback message within the relevant components and disables dependent interactions until data is successfully loaded.

If getInvestigationPackages returns an empty array for the current filters, the packages-results-list component hides the package cards grid and instead shows an empty-state message plus the custom quote call-to-action.
page_functionality.state_logic:
The page uses URL query parameters and in-memory state to manage filters and data loading, without using localStorage or other persistent client storage. On load, the page parses the current URL for projectType (string) and maxPrice (number). It then calls getSubsurfaceInvestigationServicePage and getInvestigationPackagesFilterOptions. From getSubsurfaceInvestigationServicePage, it obtains initialFilterDefaults (which may specify default projectType and maxPrice) and initialPackages. From getInvestigationPackagesFilterOptions, it obtains the allowed projectTypes and overall priceRange (minPrice, maxPrice, currency).

To determine the effective initial filter state:
1. For projectType: If the URL contains a projectType value that matches one of the projectTypes.value from getInvestigationPackagesFilterOptions, that value is used. Otherwise, if initialFilterDefaults.projectType exists and is within the allowed projectTypes list, that is used. If neither is available or valid, the page may treat projectType as unset (meaning "no project-type filter"), depending on how projectTypes options are defined by getInvestigationPackagesFilterOptions.
2. For maxPrice: If the URL contains maxPrice and it can be parsed as a number within the priceRange (between minPrice and maxPrice from filter options), that value is used. Otherwise, if initialFilterDefaults.maxPrice exists and is within the allowed priceRange, that is used. If neither is available, the page uses the upper bound of priceRange.maxPrice as the effective maximum price.

Once the effective filters are determined, the page decides how to populate the initial package list. If no URL overrides were provided and the effective filters exactly match initialFilterDefaults (where provided), the page uses initialPackages from getSubsurfaceInvestigationServicePage directly to render the package list. This avoids an extra API call. If URL parameters exist or the effective filters differ from initialFilterDefaults, the page calls getInvestigationPackages with the computed filter values: projectType set to the effective project type (or omitted/null if no filter), maxPrice set to the effective maximum price, minPrice set to the lower bound from priceRange (or another value derived from the UI if a minimum price control exists), and onlyActive set to true.

When the user interacts with the filters, the page maintains a single source of truth for filter state in component-level state (e.g., in the packages-filter-bar component). After each change, the component updates the browser URL's query string to reflect the new state: projectType is set to the selected value (or removed if no project-type filter is applied), and maxPrice is set to the currently selected maximum price (or removed when reverting to default if that behavior is chosen). The updated query parameters allow the current filter state to be sharable and restorable on reload.

Each time the filters change, the page calls getInvestigationPackages with a parameter set derived from the updated filter state. The response overwrites the current packages array, and the packages-results-list component re-renders accordingly. The page does not store any of this state in localStorage; all persisted state is encoded only via URL query parameters. Navigating away and back to the page without a preserved URL will reset to initialFilterDefaults and priceRange-based defaults, while re-opening the page with a saved URL will reconstruct the filters from the query string and fetch matching data.

components.functionality: On page initialization, orchestrates loading of subsurface investigation service data and filter options by calling getSubsurfaceInvestigationServicePage and getInvestigationPackagesFilterOptions, then computes effective initial filters based on URL parameters, initialFilterDefaults, and priceRange, and finally either uses initialPackages or calls getInvestigationPackages to populate the initial package list.

Displays the subsurface investigation service name, summary, and overviewHtml content, along with high-level contextual information for this service, using data from the service object returned by getSubsurfaceInvestigationServicePage.

Renders the list of subsurface investigation capabilities (e.g., types of borings, CPT, lab tests) based on the capabilities array returned by getSubsurfaceInvestigationServicePage, allowing users to scan what can be included in packages or custom investigations.

Displays typical use cases for subsurface investigations using the typicalUseCases array returned by getSubsurfaceInvestigationServicePage, helping users understand common project scenarios where these services apply.
Shows introductory explanatory text for the Packages & Pricing section using packagesIntroText from getSubsurfaceInvestigationServicePage, providing context for how to interpret packages and filters.

Provides interactive filter controls for project type and maximum price in the Packages & Pricing section. It initializes its controls using effective filter values computed by service-content-loader (from URL parameters, initialFilterDefaults, and priceRange from getInvestigationPackagesFilterOptions), updates URL parameters when the user changes filters, and triggers getInvestigationPackages to refresh the package list.

Shows a summary above the package list, such as the count of matching packages and an indication of the currently applied filters (e.g., selected project type and maximum price), using the current filter state and the length of the InvestigationPackage array returned from getSubsurfaceInvestigationPackages or initialPackages.

Displays the current list of investigation packages as a collection of package cards. It binds to the active array of InvestigationPackage objects, updates whenever getInvestigationPackages or initialPackages provide new data, and controls whether to show the grid of package cards or an empty-state message when no packages match the current filters.

Represents a single investigation package within the results list. It displays key fields from the InvestigationPackage (such as name, description, price, features, estimatedDurationDays if present), and exposes an interactive title and 'View Details' control that navigate to package_detail.html with the selected packageId. It may also visually highlight whether CPT or consolidation testing is included based on includesCptSoundings and includesConsolidationTesting flags.

Appears when the current InvestigationPackage array is empty after applying filters. It informs the user that no packages match the selected project type and price range and encourages them to either adjust their filters or use the custom quote call-to-action.

Provides a consistent call-to-action element allowing users to request a custom subsurface investigation quote. On click, it navigates to quote_request.html with serviceType=subsurface_investigation appended as a URL parameter. This CTA is accessible both when packages are available and when the list is empty.

Keeps the filter state (projectType, maxPrice) synchronized with the URL query parameters. It listens for changes in the filter controls and updates the URL accordingly without reloading the page when possible, and also listens for URL changes (e.g., browser back/forward) to update the filter controls and re-trigger getInvestigationPackages with the restored filter state.

layout.grid_system: Implied 12‑column responsive grid (typical WordPress/corporate layout) with a full-width container for the hero and a centered content column.
layout.layout_pattern: The page follows a strict top-to-bottom, section-based flow anchored by the global fixed header and a full-bleed hero. Immediately below the hero, the main service content is organized into a single centered column that runs down the page: first the service overview, then capabilities, then typical use cases. Below these, a visually distinguished packages section presents filters, results, and calls to action in a stacked order. All components align to an implied 12-column grid, with the main content constrained to a comfortable central width while background bands (hero, packages section) stretch edge-to-edge.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page follows a strict top-to-bottom, section-based flow anchored by the global fixed header and a full-bleed hero. Immediately below the hero, the main service content is organized into a single centered column that runs down the page: first the service overview, then capabilities, then typical use cases. Below these, a visually distinguished packages section presents filters, results, and calls to action in a stacked order. All components align to an implied 12-column grid, with the main content constrained to a comfortable central width while background bands (hero, packages section) stretch edge-to-edge.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The fixed header occupies roughly 10–15% of the viewport height; the hero consumes the rest of the initial viewport, with its content vertically centered. Below the fold, each major section (overview, capabilities, use cases, packages) is separated by 48–64px of vertical whitespace to maintain a spacious, corporate feel. Within each section, internal padding and gaps between elements follow the 8px base scale, typically 16–24px, ensuring generous breathing room around typography and lists. The packages area uses a full-width background band but keeps interactive content within a centered column that spans about 8–10 of 12 grid columns on desktop.
layout_strategies.content_density: The density remains moderate and readable: relatively few elements per section, larger headings, and clear visual grouping. Text blocks (overview, intro copy) are limited in line length by the central column and paired with ample whitespace above and below. Lists (capabilities, use cases, package cards) are visually structured with consistent spacing between items and optional multi-column layouts within the central column on larger screens, avoiding clutter. Interactive elements like the filter bar and package cards are clearly delineated, with comfortable padding and separation to prevent the interface from feeling busy despite data-rich content.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The visual flow echoes a hybrid F/Z pattern: users first engage with the logo and global nav in the fixed header, then the eye drops into the hero’s centered category pill and large service title. Scrolling down, the flow becomes a vertical scan along the left-aligned content column: overview heading and copy, then capabilities, then typical use cases. Entering the packages section, the eye hits the section heading, then the horizontal filter bar, followed by the results summary and the grid of package cards. The final downward pull is the prominently styled custom quote CTA centered at the bottom of the packages section, acting as a terminal anchor for the page journey.

layout.overall_description: Below the fixed teal header and full-bleed hero, the page transitions into a clean, centered content column that runs down the middle of the screen, framed by generous lateral margins. The first viewport is dominated by the hero: a technical background image running edge-to-edge, overlaid with a small category pill, a bold, multi-line service title, and a compact line of supporting meta content, all vertically and horizontally centered to mirror the observed mockup. As the user scrolls, the busy hero background gives way to a calm, white main content area where the Subsurface Investigation overview, capabilities, and typical use cases are stacked sequentially with clear section breaks and consistent typographic hierarchy. Further down, a full-width, softly tinted band introduces the investigation packages area; within it, a left-aligned heading and intro text lead into a horizontally arranged filter bar, a concise results summary, and a responsive grid of package cards. If no packages match the filters, a centered empty state message occupies the same central column. The section concludes with a visually prominent, centered custom quote button, giving the entire page a structured, marketing-led narrative that naturally guides users from understanding the service to exploring specific offerings and finally requesting a tailored quote.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The service-content-loader sits logically at the top of the main content stack, just below the hero and above the service overview section, but it manifests primarily as an overlay state rather than a persistent visual element. When active, it places semi-transparent white overlay and skeleton elements over the hero text area and the upper portion of the central content column, mimicking the positions of the hero headline, section headings, and first text blocks. The overlay extends horizontally across the central content width (about 70–80% of the page width on desktop, following the same margins as the main column) and vertically covers roughly the first 60–70% of the viewport below the header, fading out softly into the background. This maintains the perception of the underlying layout while clearly indicating loading, without altering the intrinsic positioning of subsequent sections.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Positioned as an absolute/fixed overlay aligned to the top of the main content area; it covers the hero and upper main column while active and is completely removed (no space reserved) once loading is complete.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The service-overview-section is the first stable content block the user encounters after the hero. It begins approximately 48–64px below the bottom edge of the hero, creating a clear separation between the immersive hero and the informational body. The section aligns to the central content column, spanning roughly 8–10 of the 12 grid columns (about 960–1120px max-width on large screens), centered horizontally with equal left and right margins. Inside, a compact eyebrow label (e.g., “Subsurface Investigation”) sits at the top left of this column, followed by a prominent left-aligned section heading and one to three paragraphs of copy. Vertical spacing within the section follows the 8px scale, with 16–24px between heading and paragraphs and about 32px padding above and below the content, creating a calm, readable block that anchors the rest of the service details.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard block-level section that scrolls with the page, occupying the full width of the central content column without any fixed or sticky behavior.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The capabilities-list is positioned directly below the service overview, continuing within the same central column to maintain a linear narrative. It begins after a vertical gap of about 32px from the last paragraph of the overview, so the user perceives it as an immediate extension of the explanation. A small section title (e.g., “Key Capabilities”) is left-aligned at the top of this block, followed by the list itself. On desktop, the list items are arranged in two columns inside the central column: each column occupying roughly half of the available width with a 24px gutter between them, creating a balanced but still spacious grid of bullet points. Each capability item has 8–16px vertical spacing from the next, and the entire capabilities block is padded with about 24px of vertical breathing room before the next section starts, preserving the mockup’s comfortable reading rhythm.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard block element within the main content column, expanding vertically as items are added, with responsive behavior so that columns collapse into a single vertical list on narrow screens.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The typical-use-cases-list appears as the next major section in the vertical flow, following the capabilities-list with a more pronounced vertical gap of approximately 48px to clearly indicate a new topic. It also resides within the same centered main content column, matching the width and left alignment of previous text for visual continuity. At the top of this section, a left-aligned heading such as “Typical Use Cases” introduces the content, followed by a series of use case items laid out either as a simple vertical list or as compact stacked cards. On larger screens, the list can be formatted in a two-column layout similar to the capabilities, but with slightly taller rows (e.g., brief titles plus one-line descriptions), while on mobile it collapses to a single column. The section maintains 24–32px of internal top and bottom padding and clear 16–24px spacing between items, keeping the density readable even if several use cases are listed.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard vertical section within the content column, scrolling naturally with the page and adjusting from two columns to one on smaller viewports.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The packages-intro-text acts as the visual and conceptual entry point to the investigation packages area and sits below the typical-use-cases-list with a clear sectional break. About 64px of vertical space separates it from the previous section, and it is housed inside a full-width background band that spans edge-to-edge across the viewport, typically using a very light teal or neutral gray tint to distinguish this area from the white content above. Within this band, content is constrained again to the central column width, centered horizontally and aligned to the same 12-column grid as earlier sections. A section heading such as “Investigation Packages” is left-aligned at the top of this column, with a short explanatory paragraph beneath it; there is 16–24px spacing between the heading and copy, and 24–32px of padding above the heading and below the paragraph inside the band. This creates a strong, but not overwhelming, visual cue that the user has reached the interactive packages segment.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard content block within a full-width background section, scrolling with the page and serving as the top of a visually grouped packages area.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The packages-filter-bar is placed immediately below the packages-intro-text within the same tinted background band, with a modest 24px gap separating it from the intro paragraph above. It spans the full width of the central content column, appearing as a horizontal strip or card with a subtle background (slightly lighter than the band) and a thin border or shadow to give it structure. On desktop, filter controls (e.g., dropdowns, checkboxes, tag pills) are arranged in a single row, left-aligned, with 16–24px horizontal spacing between each control; the bar’s height is determined by its contents but typically occupies around 64–96px. On narrower screens, the controls wrap to multiple rows while maintaining equal left/right padding of about 16–24px. The bar is visually tied to the packages results below, forming the top edge of the results area and signaling interactivity in a clean, uncluttered way.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Scrolls with the page by default but may adopt a sticky behavior on larger screens, pinning to the top of the viewport within the packages band once scrolled into view, so that filters remain accessible while the user scrolls through the results.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The packages-results-summary sits directly below the filter bar, sharing the same central column alignment and background band. A small vertical gap of about 16px separates it from the bottom of the filter bar, keeping it visually associated with the filters yet distinct. The summary is a single, left-aligned horizontal line of text (e.g., “Showing 8 packages” plus sorting information), occupying only the left portion of the content column, with ample blank space to the right. It is compact in height—roughly the size of a standard text line with a small padding buffer above and below—so that the user’s attention can quickly pass through it to reach the more visually substantial package cards below while still providing helpful context.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline text element within the main column that scrolls naturally with the rest of the packages content; no special fixed or sticky behavior.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The packages-results-list occupies the majority of the visual real estate in the packages band and is positioned immediately below the results summary with about 24px of vertical spacing. The list adheres to the central content column width and presents investigation-package-card components in a responsive grid layout. On large screens, cards are arranged in three columns across the central column (each card taking up roughly 1/3 of the column width with 24px horizontal gutters and 24–32px vertical gaps); on medium screens, this reduces to two columns, and on small screens to a single column. The list stretches downward, potentially occupying more than one viewport height as the user scrolls, with about 48px of bottom margin before any closing CTAs. The grid maintains consistent card alignment and spacing so that, despite multiple elements, the overall impression remains orderly and in line with the mockup’s balanced density.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Expands vertically as more cards are loaded, scrolling within the page. It does not fix or float, but its top edge is visually anchored by the filter bar and results summary above.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Each investigation-package-card is a self-contained, clickable card within the packages-results-list grid. On desktop, a card occupies approximately one-third of the central column width, with equal left and right padding of 16–24px inside the card and 24px grid gutters between adjacent cards. The card has a white background, subtle shadow or border, and slightly rounded corners to stand out against the tinted packages band. Internally, content is stacked from top to bottom: a small category pill or label at the top left, followed by a multi-line package title set in a larger, high-contrast type; beneath that, a short 1–2 line description. At the bottom of the card, a horizontal row contains pricing or a key metric on the left and a primary “View details” or “Request quote” button aligned to the right. Vertical spacing inside the card follows the base 8px scale (8–16px between text elements, 16–24px between groups), ensuring the card feels spacious and scannable. Cards align to the same baseline across rows, creating a clean, corporate grid aesthetic.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard card elements within a responsive grid. Each card is fully clickable, but it scrolls with the list and does not have any fixed or sticky behavior.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The packages-empty-state appears in place of the packages-results-list when no packages match the current filters. It is centered within the same central content column and background band, starting at roughly the same vertical position where the first row of cards would normally appear. The content is center-aligned horizontally: a simple icon or illustration (optional) at the top, followed by a concise message explaining that no packages were found, and a clear action button such as “Clear filters” or “Request a custom quote.” There is generous vertical padding—approximately 64px above and below the message—so that the empty state feels intentional and calm rather than like a missing error. The width of the content is narrower than the full column (around 60–70% of the column width), with ample whitespace on both sides, echoing the spacious hero typographic treatment and keeping the focus on the message.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline replacement for the results list; it occupies the same space in the vertical flow, scrolls with the page, and is only rendered when there are no matching results.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The custom-quote-cta sits at the bottom of the packages area, acting as the final focal point in the page’s vertical flow. It is positioned approximately 48px below the last row of package cards or the bottom of the empty state, still within the full-width tinted background band but visually separated by that vertical space. The CTA appears as a prominent button or short CTA block centered horizontally within the central column; on desktop it takes up its natural width (e.g., 280–320px), while on mobile it can stretch closer to full width with 16–24px lateral padding. There may be a short one-line supporting text above the button, left-aligned or center-aligned, with 8–16px spacing between them. Below the CTA, an additional 48–64px of vertical space is left before the start of the footer or next site section, giving the call to action room to breathe and echoing the hero’s sense of importance.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard inline CTA block within the packages band; it scrolls with the page and does not stick, but its centered position and surrounding whitespace make it a natural stopping point in the layout.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The url-state-sync component is non-visual and does not occupy any explicit space in the layout. It operates behind the scenes, synchronizing the state of the packages-filter-bar and current results (such as selected filters, sorting, and pagination) with the URL query parameters. This allows users to share or reload the page while preserving selected filters, without altering the spatial relationships between visual elements. From a layout perspective, it is conceptually attached to the filter bar and results list but has no rendered footprint.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: No visual presence; it does not affect spacing or positioning. It behaves purely as a logical layer connected to the filter and results components.

name: Investigation Package Detail
title: Investigation Package Detail
description: Displays full details for a selected subsurface investigation package and allows the user to save it to a named project plan.

core_features: Read the investigation package identifier from the URL (packageId) and load full package details using getInvestigationPackageDetail.
Display all key attributes of the selected InvestigationPackage, including name, description, project type, price, currency, features, estimated duration, notes, and active/inactive status.
Highlight important subsurface investigation tests such as CPT soundings and consolidation testing based on includesCptSoundings and includesConsolidationTesting flags.
Show any applicable assumptions, limitations, or notes sourced from the InvestigationPackage.notes field.
Provide a 'Select this package' action that opens a Save Plan dialog for naming a project and adding optional notes.
Allow the user to submit a Save Plan form that calls saveInvestigationPackageToPlan with investigationPackageId, projectName, and notes.
Display success or error feedback after attempting to save the package to a plan, using the response from saveInvestigationPackageToPlan.
On successful save, offer a 'View in My Plans' navigation control that redirects to my_projects.html with URL parameters tab=plans and highlightPlanId set to the saved plan id.
user_workflows: View package details: 1) User navigates to package_detail.html with a packageId query parameter. 2) The page reads packageId from the URL. 3) The page invokes getInvestigationPackageDetail(packageId). 4) While loading, a loading state is shown. 5) On success, package data is bound to detail components (summary, features, notes). 6) If the package is inactive, the page clearly indicates that selection is unavailable and disables the 'Select this package' action.

Select a package and open the Save Plan dialog: 1) With package details visible and isActive true, the user clicks the 'Select this package' button. 2) The click event opens the Save Plan dialog in a modal or inline panel. 3) The dialog displays the selected package name and price for context (read-only). 4) The dialog shows a form with fields for projectName (required) and notes (optional) that the user can edit.

Save a package as a plan: 1) User fills in the projectName and optionally notes in the Save Plan form. 2) User submits the form (e.g., clicks 'Save to My Plans'). 3) The form validates that projectName is not empty; if invalid, inline validation messages are shown and the API is not called. 4) On valid input, the page calls saveInvestigationPackageToPlan with investigationPackageId equal to the current package id, and projectName and notes from the form. 5) While waiting for a response, the form shows a saving state and prevents duplicate submissions. 6) On success (success = true), the dialog switches to a confirmation view showing a success message and summary of the savedPlan. 7) On failure (success = false), the dialog shows an error message using the message field and allows the user to retry.

Navigate to My Plans after saving: 1) After a successful save, the confirmation view presents a 'View in My Plans' button. 2) When the user clicks the button, the page navigates to my_projects.html with query parameters tab=plans and highlightPlanId equal to savedPlan.id. 3) No additional API calls are made from this page for the navigation; the target page handles the parameters.

Return visit via plan or service pages: 1) A user coming from service_subsurface_investigation.html, my_projects.html, or index.html clicks a link to this page that includes the appropriate packageId. 2) Regardless of the source page, this page repeats the same loading logic based solely on the URL parameter packageId. 3) Optionally, if an additional URL parameter indicates the Save Plan dialog should open automatically (for example, autoOpenSaveDialog=1), the Save Plan dialog opens immediately after package details are successfully loaded.
interactions: On initial page load, the page reads the packageId URL parameter and triggers data loading via getInvestigationPackageDetail.
If packageId is missing or empty, the page displays an error state indicating that no package was specified and does not call the API.
If getInvestigationPackageDetail returns an error (e.g., invalid id) or no package object, the page shows an error state and hides package-dependent actions.
If the loaded package has isActive set to false, the 'Select this package' button is hidden or disabled, and an informational message explains that the package is not available for selection.
The 'Select this package' button is clickable only when package data is successfully loaded and isActive is true; clicking it opens the Save Plan dialog.
Within the Save Plan dialog, the projectName field is required; the notes field is optional. The user can type freely in both fields, and their values are stored in in-memory component state.

Submitting the Save Plan form triggers client-side validation (projectName not empty). If validation passes, the component calls saveInvestigationPackageToPlan with the current package id and the form values.
During the saveInvestigationPackageToPlan API call, the Save Plan form shows a loading indicator and disables submit and close actions to avoid double submissions.

On a successful saveInvestigationPackageToPlan response, the dialog replaces the form with a confirmation state that uses savedPlan data (including id, projectName, packageName, packagePrice, currency, projectType, includesCptSoundings, includesConsolidationTesting).
On a failed saveInvestigationPackageToPlan response (success=false), the dialog displays the returned message and re-enables form controls so the user can correct inputs or retry.
Clicking 'View in My Plans' in the confirmation state triggers navigation to my_projects.html with tab=plans and highlightPlanId set to savedPlan.id.
Clicking a dialog close control (e.g., 'Cancel' or an explicit close icon) in the Save Plan dialog closes the dialog and returns the user to the package detail view without saving.

If an optional URL parameter such as autoOpenSaveDialog is present and truthy, after getInvestigationPackageDetail succeeds, the page automatically opens the Save Plan dialog as if the user clicked 'Select this package'.
page_functionality.state_logic: The primary state driver is the packageId URL parameter on package_detail.html. On page load and whenever the URL changes, the page reads packageId. If packageId is present and non-empty, the page calls getInvestigationPackageDetail(packageId) exactly once per distinct packageId value. The resulting InvestigationPackage object populates all display components and determines whether the selection workflow is enabled (isActive). No InvestigationPackage values are hardcoded; all values are taken from the API response fields. The presence and value of optional URL parameters can adjust behavior: for example, a parameter like autoOpenSaveDialog=1 can instruct the page to open the Save Plan dialog automatically once the package details have loaded successfully. Page state for the Save Plan dialog (open/closed, current projectName and notes input values, loading/success/error states) is maintained in-memory and is not persisted to URL parameters or any storage. When the user successfully saves a plan, the page uses the returned savedPlan.id to form a redirect URL to my_projects.html?tab=plans&highlightPlanId={savedPlan.id}, ensuring that the My Plans page can highlight the newly created SavedInvestigationPackagePlan. All navigation between pages relies solely on URL parameters and no localStorage is used.

components.functionality: Reads and monitors URL parameters relevant to this page (at minimum packageId and optionally autoOpenSaveDialog or similar), and exposes the current packageId and dialog-opening flags to other components.
event_handlers: onPageLoadReadUrlParams: triggered once when the page loads to parse packageId and any optional parameters from the query string.
onUrlChangeUpdateParams: triggered if the browser URL changes (e.g., via history navigation) to update the current packageId and other flags and notify dependent components.
onPackageIdResolved: emitted when a valid packageId has been read, so that the data loader can call getInvestigationPackageDetail.

components.functionality: Fetches InvestigationPackage details from the backend using getInvestigationPackageDetail with the packageId obtained from the URL, handles loading and error states, and provides the loaded package to child components.
event_handlers: onPackageIdResolved: when invoked with a new packageId, triggers getInvestigationPackageDetail(packageId).
onLoadStart: sets an internal loading state to true before calling the API.
onLoadSuccess: receives the package object from getInvestigationPackageDetail, stores it in component state, sets loading to false, and clears previous errors.
onLoadError: handles cases where the API call fails or returns no package, storing an error state and setting loading to false.
onPackageDataReady: emitted after a successful load so that detail and action components can render with the InvestigationPackage data.

components.functionality: Displays high-level details of the loaded InvestigationPackage, including name, projectType, price with currency, estimatedDurationDays, active/inactive status, and a primary 'Select this package' action when selection is allowed.
event_handlers: onPackageDataReady: populates the summary view fields with the current package properties (name, projectType, price, currency, estimatedDurationDays, isActive).

onClickSelectPackage: triggered when the user clicks the 'Select this package' button; if package.isActive is true and no loading or error states are present, it requests that the save-plan-dialog be opened and passes along the current InvestigationPackage.

onInactivePackageRender: if isActive is false, disables or hides the 'Select this package' button and shows an informational message; this is a render-time logic hook rather than a user-triggered event.

components.functionality: Shows the list of included features and tests for the InvestigationPackage, using the features array and boolean flags for CPT soundings and consolidation testing to emphasize certain items.
event_handlers: onPackageDataReady: renders a dynamic list from package.features and uses includesCptSoundings and includesConsolidationTesting to annotate or highlight the corresponding entries in the list.
onToggleFeatureDetails: optional event for expanding or collapsing detailed descriptions per feature if such behavior is implemented; uses only data from the InvestigationPackage response.

components.functionality: Displays the detailed narrative description and any notes associated with the InvestigationPackage, including textual assumptions or limitations sourced entirely from the API fields description and notes.
event_handlers: onPackageDataReady: binds package.description and package.notes to the description and notes sections, hiding sections where the corresponding field is empty or undefined.

components.functionality: Provides a modal or inline dialog container that can open and close around the Save Plan workflow, showing either the input form or the post-save confirmation state depending on internal state.
event_handlers: onOpenDialog: opens the dialog when triggered (e.g., by onClickSelectPackage from package-summary-panel or automatically if an autoOpenSaveDialog URL flag is set). It receives the current InvestigationPackage so contextual information such as packageName and price can be shown read-only.
onCloseDialog: closes the dialog when the user cancels, after a successful save when the user chooses to dismiss it, or when an external component requests closure.
onSaveSuccess: switches the dialog content from the Save Plan form to the confirmation view using savedPlan data from the saveInvestigationPackageToPlan response.
onSaveError: keeps the form open and displays the error message from the API, allowing the user to adjust inputs and retry.

components.functionality: Collects the projectName and optional notes from the user and submits them, together with the current InvestigationPackage id, to saveInvestigationPackageToPlan in order to create a SavedInvestigationPackagePlan.
event_handlers: onFormInit: initializes form fields when the dialog opens, ensuring projectName and notes fields start empty or with any pre-filled values if such logic is added later.
onChangeProjectNameInput: updates in-memory form state with the user's projectName input and clears any validation error related to an empty projectName.
onChangeNotesInput: updates in-memory form state with the user's notes input.
onValidateForm: runs when the user attempts to submit; checks that projectName is non-empty. If invalid, sets validation error state and prevents the API call.

onSubmitSavePlan: if validation passes, calls saveInvestigationPackageToPlan with parameters investigationPackageId (from the current InvestigationPackage.id), projectName, and notes (from form state), and sets an internal submitting/loading flag.
onSavePlanApiSuccess: handles a successful API response by clearing the loading state, storing savedPlan from the response, and emitting onSaveSuccess for the dialog to render the confirmation view.

onSavePlanApiError: handles an API error or a successful response with success=false by clearing the loading state, setting an error message using the response.message, and keeping the form visible so the user can make corrections.

components.functionality: Shows confirmation details after a successful saveInvestigationPackageToPlan call, including key fields from savedPlan and actions to navigate to My Plans or close the dialog.
event_handlers: onSaveSuccess: receives the savedPlan object from the save-plan-form component and renders a success message that may include savedPlan.projectName, savedPlan.packageName, savedPlan.packagePrice, savedPlan.currency, savedPlan.projectType, savedPlan.includesCptSoundings, and savedPlan.includesConsolidationTesting.
onClickViewInMyPlans: constructs a URL for my_projects.html with tab=plans and highlightPlanId set to savedPlan.id, then navigates the browser to that URL.
onClickCloseConfirmation: closes the dialog without navigating, typically by calling onCloseDialog on the save-plan-dialog component.

components.functionality: Centralizes display of loading indicators and error messages for the package detail loading process so the user understands whether the page is fetching data or has encountered issues.
event_handlers: onLoadStart: when invoked by package-detail-loader, shows a loading indicator and hides previous error messages.
onLoadSuccess: hides the loading indicator once package data is successfully loaded.
onLoadError: displays a user-readable error message when getInvestigationPackageDetail fails or returns no valid package, and can optionally provide a retry action.
onClickRetryLoad: when provided, re-triggers getInvestigationPackageDetail with the current packageId for another attempt at loading data.

layout.grid_system: Implied 12‑column responsive grid (typical WordPress/corporate layout) with a full-width container for the hero and a centered content column.
layout.layout_pattern: The page uses a linear, top-to-bottom flow that mirrors the mockup: a fixed-height teal header (outside this component list) sits at the very top, followed immediately by a full-bleed hero area that houses the package-summary-panel as its core content. Inside this hero, the package category, package name, key summary details, and a primary 'Save to Project Plan' action are stacked and center-aligned over a technical/geotechnical background image. Below the hero, the page transitions into a centered content column where the package-features-list appears first as a structured, scannable section, followed by the package-description-and-notes as a more text-heavy, explanatory block. Dialog-related components (save-plan-dialog, save-plan-form, save-plan-confirmation) appear as an overlay on top of all content when invoked, while the state-manager, data-loader, and error/loading handlers operate behind the scenes without occupying visible layout space.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page uses a linear, top-to-bottom flow that mirrors the mockup: a fixed-height teal header (outside this component list) sits at the very top, followed immediately by a full-bleed hero area that houses the package-summary-panel as its core content. Inside this hero, the package category, package name, key summary details, and a primary 'Save to Project Plan' action are stacked and center-aligned over a technical/geotechnical background image. Below the hero, the page transitions into a centered content column where the package-features-list appears first as a structured, scannable section, followed by the package-description-and-notes as a more text-heavy, explanatory block. Dialog-related components (save-plan-dialog, save-plan-form, save-plan-confirmation) appear as an overlay on top of all content when invoked, while the state-manager, data-loader, and error/loading handlers operate behind the scenes without occupying visible layout space.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The header consumes roughly 10–15% of the viewport height and stays fixed; the hero, dominated by the package-summary-panel, occupies the remaining initial viewport height with generous lateral padding for text. The hero content cluster (category pill, title, meta row, and primary action) sits within the central 40–60% of the vertical space. Below the hero, each main section (features list, description/notes) is separated by 48–64px vertical margins. The central content column spans about 70–80% of the page width on desktop, capped at a comfortable max width, with consistent 16–24px internal padding in each block. The modal dialog for saving plans floats above everything else, centered both horizontally and vertically, taking up roughly 40–50% of viewport width and adapting on smaller screens to nearly full width with preserved internal padding.
layout_strategies.content_density: The layout maintains a moderately spacious feel: the hero has very few elements but uses large, high-contrast typography and ample breathing room so that the busy technical background never overwhelms the text. The navigation and metadata are clean and uncluttered, with comfortable spacing between elements. The features list is more content-dense but organized into clearly separated rows or groups, each with enough vertical padding to remain legible. The description and notes section favors line length and readability over packing in content, using generous line spacing and margins. Dialog content is compact but not cramped, with clear groupings of form fields and concise messaging.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The eye follows an F/Z hybrid path: starting at the top-left logo (in the global header), sweeping across the navigation to the right, then dropping into the hero where the category pill and bold package title form the primary focal point at mid-screen. From there, the eye moves downward through the metadata row (avatar, date, etc.) and the primary 'Save to Project Plan' action. Scrolling continues the vertical flow into the features list as the next scanning target, then into the descriptive and notes content. When the save-plan-dialog is open, it interrupts the flow and becomes the new visual anchor at the center of the screen, with the form fields and confirmation/status messages guiding the eye from top to bottom within the dialog. Error and loading states either overlay or replace these focal areas while preserving the same primary visual anchors.

layout.overall_description: The page presents as a polished, corporate geotechnical service detail view: a fixed teal header anchors the top, while a full-width, edge-to-edge hero image of a subsurface or construction scene fills the initial viewport beneath it. In the center of this hero, a semi-transparent overlay houses a compact category pill, a large multi-line package title, concise meta information, and a prominent 'Save to Project Plan' button, all stacked and center-aligned with generous spacing. This cluster of text and controls sits within the horizontal middle of the screen and roughly the central half of the vertical space, creating a calm focal island against the technical backdrop. Below the hero, the layout narrows to a centered content column where the package’s features appear first as a structured list with clear rows and icons, followed by a more text-heavy section for detailed descriptions and notes, both separated by generous vertical spacing. When the user chooses to save the package, the background gently fades, and a clean, white dialog card slides into focus at the center of the viewport, containing a short form and clear confirmation state while leaving the hero and details subtly visible behind it. Invisible state and loading components orchestrate data in the background, while any errors or loading overlays respect the same central alignment and spacious aesthetic, maintaining the tidy, marketing-style presentation.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This component operates entirely behind the scenes and does not occupy any visible space in the layout. Conceptually, it lives at the page level, initializing as soon as the Investigation Package Detail page is loaded. It binds URL parameters to the visible components, such as the package-summary-panel and package-detail-loader, but does not render any UI elements, spacing, or containers. From a visual perspective, the layout appears unchanged whether this manager is present or not.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual behavioral component; it is effectively fixed to the page lifecycle and does not participate in spatial layout or consume any screen real estate.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This component also has no visible footprint of its own; instead, it orchestrates data fetching for the package-centric sections (hero summary, features list, description/notes). Functionally, it wraps these sections so that when data is loading, the error-and-loading-state-handler can replace or overlay the hero and main content column. While conceptually positioned at the top of the content area, it does not render any dedicated visual container or occupy layout space.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual wrapper; logically spans the hero and main content zones but does not change their layout. It triggers visual loading or error states in child components without itself being rendered.

component_layouts.layout_narrative:
The package-summary-panel is embedded as the core content of the full-bleed hero section directly beneath the fixed teal header. It sits centered within the viewport, both horizontally and vertically, occupying roughly 60–70% of the page width on desktop (with a max-width similar to a 12-column grid container) while the hero background image extends edge-to-edge across the screen. Vertically, the panel’s content cluster is positioned in the middle 40–60% of the hero’s height, giving ample space above and below. The panel itself appears as a lightly translucent or subtly shaded content area with 24px internal padding on all sides, allowing the text to breathe against the busy technical background.

Inside this panel, elements follow a strict vertical stack: at the top, a compact category pill (e.g., 'Investigation Package') is aligned to the horizontal center, with tight vertical padding and slightly rounded corners. Below this, the main package title appears as a large, multi-line, high-contrast heading centered in the panel, potentially spanning two to three lines. Under the title, a single horizontal row of metadata runs across the center: on the left side of this row (but still within the centered column), a small circular avatar or icon is followed by the package author or organization name; to its right, the investigation type or date and comments/count are laid out in a single line with consistent spacing between items. Either directly beneath this meta row or slightly offset to the right within the same central column, a primary 'Save to Project Plan' button is placed, keeping a 16–24px gap from the metadata. The overall group is visually compact yet comfortably spaced, ensuring it remains the immediate focal point against the sprawling background image.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Behaves as a central, responsive hero content block fixed within the hero section. It remains vertically anchored in the center of the hero while the header above stays fixed; as the user scrolls, the entire hero containing this panel scrolls out of view in a standard way (no parallax or sticky behavior).

component_layouts.layout_narrative:
The package-features-list sits directly below the hero section in the main content area. It occupies a centered content column that spans roughly 70–80% of the viewport width on large screens, constrained by a max width and aligned with the hero’s text column. There is a generous vertical margin of about 48–64px between the bottom of the hero and the top of this section, reinforcing the spacious corporate feel. The section itself is contained within an invisible or very subtle card-like block, with 24px internal padding on the left and right to ensure text and icons never approach the screen edges.

Within this area, a short section heading (e.g., 'Included Features' or 'What’s in this Investigation') is placed at the top-left of the column but still aligned to the same central grid, with 16–24px of space beneath it. The features are then listed vertically as discrete rows: each row spans the full content width, with an icon or bullet on the far left of the row and text (feature name and optional concise description) to the right. Each feature row has 12–16px padding above and below, creating a rhythm of evenly spaced items that feel organized but not crowded. On tablet or mobile, the list simply stacks in a single column maintaining the same vertical spacing, with the section width shrinking responsibly but still centered.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard block-level section in the document flow. It scrolls naturally with the page, always positioned immediately following the hero and before the package-description-and-notes, with no fixed or sticky behavior.

component_layouts.layout_narrative:
The package-description-and-notes section appears directly beneath the package-features-list, separated by a vertical gap of roughly 48px to maintain the spacious, tiered hierarchy. It shares the same centered content column as the features list, aligning perfectly with its left and right boundaries and respecting the same 70–80% width and max-width constraints. The section is presented as a text-focused block with generous 24px internal padding and a subtle visual distinction from the background (slight tonal shift or faint card treatment) to emphasize readability.

At the top of this section, a heading such as 'Detailed Description' or 'Engineering Notes' is placed aligned to the left of the content column, with about 16–24px of space below it. The main description text flows underneath in medium-width paragraphs that maintain an optimal line length for reading. If notes or sub-sections exist (e.g., 'Assumptions', 'Limitations', 'Recommended Uses'), these are separated with 16–24px vertical spacing and may be styled as subheadings with bold text, but everything remains within a single-column structure to echo the mockup’s linear layout. On wider screens, light two-column behavior can occur only for clearly related items (e.g., description on the left and brief notes on the right), but both columns remain within the same central container and never exceed the defined max width. On smaller screens, all content collapses into a single vertical column.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard flowing content block. It follows the features list in the normal scroll order and expands vertically as needed with the amount of text, without any fixed or overlay behavior.

component_layouts.layout_narrative:
The save-plan-dialog appears only when triggered (for example, when the user clicks the 'Save to Project Plan' button in the hero’s package-summary-panel). When active, it overlays the entire page content from edge to edge with a semi-transparent darkened scrim that subtly mutes the hero image and underlying sections while still allowing their shapes to be faintly visible. Centered within the viewport, both horizontally and vertically, is a white dialog card with softly rounded corners and a clean, minimal appearance.

On desktop, this dialog card occupies roughly 40–50% of the viewport width, with a comfortable max width (around 480–640px) and 24–32px internal padding on all sides. Vertically, the card auto-sizes to its content, typically reaching into the central 30–60% of the viewport height, while maintaining at least 64px of margin from the top and bottom edges. At the top of the card, a concise dialog title (e.g., 'Save to Project Plan') is left-aligned within the card, with a close icon aligned to the top-right. Below the title, there is a short explanatory line, followed by the save-plan-form elements stacked vertically. At the bottom of the dialog, primary and secondary action buttons (e.g., 'Save' and 'Cancel') are aligned to the right edge of the card, with 16–24px of vertical spacing above them. On smaller screens, the dialog width expands to approximately 90–100% of the viewport width, while preserving internal padding and central vertical placement, effectively becoming a full-bleed mobile modal with margin at the top and bottom.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Modal overlay that floats above all other page content. It is fixed relative to the viewport so that it remains centered even when the page behind it is scrolled. Background scrolling is typically disabled while the dialog is open to keep focus on the dialog content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative:
The save-plan-form is nested inside the save-plan-dialog and defines the structured content area between the dialog’s explanatory text and its action buttons. It uses a compact, vertically stacked layout consistent with the page’s spacing system. From top to bottom, the form fields are aligned in a single column that spans nearly the full width of the dialog’s interior, leaving 16–24px of horizontal padding on each side. Typical fields (project plan name, optional description, project selection dropdown, etc.) appear in sequence with 16px vertical spacing between them.
