Each form field label is left-aligned above its associated input, mirroring the left edge of the dialog’s content column, ensuring a clean, straight left line from title to labels to fields. The inputs themselves have consistent heights and internal padding (using 8–16px) and stretch almost the full width of the dialog’s content area, minus the standard horizontal padding. Error messages or helper texts appear directly below the relevant input, in smaller text, with about 4–8px spacing. Overall, the form remains visually simple and calm, matching the corporate aesthetic, and occupies roughly the middle 40–60% of the dialog’s internal vertical space.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Contained entirely within the save-plan-dialog’s fixed modal card. It scrolls within the dialog only if there are many fields; otherwise, it adjusts its height along with the dialog but is not independently sticky or floating.

component_layouts.layout_narrative:
The save-plan-confirmation appears after a successful save action and is visually tied to the save-plan-dialog rather than the full page. Within the dialog card, it takes the place of or appears above the save-plan-form content. Typically, it manifests as a compact success state: a small icon (such as a checkmark or brief success graphic) is placed near the top of the dialog, centered horizontally, followed by a short success headline and a single-line message explaining that the package was saved to the specified project plan. This confirmation block remains centered within the dialog’s content area with generous vertical spacing—about 24px above and below the text—to maintain a clean, celebratory feeling.

If inline within the dialog, the confirmation may sit above the action area, with buttons like 'View Project Plan' and 'Close' aligned along the bottom-right corner as usual. On smaller screens, the confirmation message and buttons stack vertically but remain centered within the dialog’s width. If implemented as a brief toast-like bar instead, it would appear at the bottom edge of the dialog content, spanning its full width with 12–16px vertical padding, but still contained within the dialog’s bounds and aligned to its grid, not attached to the overall viewport.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Contextual status indicator within the modal. It does not create a separate overlay; instead, it is fixed relative to the dialog’s interior, replacing or augmenting the form content and moving with the dialog as it remains centered on the viewport.

component_layouts.layout_narrative:
The error-and-loading-state-handler controls how loading and error information is visually presented, primarily in the hero (package-summary-panel) and the main content sections (features list and description/notes). When data is loading initially, the hero area still occupies its normal space below the header, but the package-summary-panel shows either a centered spinner or skeleton placeholders in place of the category pill, title, and metadata. These placeholders mimic the eventual layout: a pill-shaped block at the top, a few wide bars where the title will be, and thinner bars for metadata. The features list and description sections beneath the hero similarly show skeleton rows and text blocks aligned to the central content column, preserving overall spacing and alignment even in loading state.

In error states where the package cannot be loaded, a clear, concise error message appears centered within the hero’s content area, taking the place of the package-summary-panel text. This message is wrapped in a compact block with comfortable padding and might include a small warning icon to the left. A retry or back navigation action is placed below the message, centered, with 16–24px vertical spacing. Below the hero, the main content column may show a brief explanatory line or remain empty, keeping vertical spacing consistent. At no point does the handler change the fixed header or the full-bleed background pattern; it only replaces the inner content of these central sections, keeping the overall page structure intact.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Overlays or replaces content within existing sections rather than adding new sections. Loading indicators are centered within their parent containers, while error messages take over the main focal areas (hero content and primary column) in place of normal content. It does not introduce additional fixed elements at the viewport level, except potentially a brief inline alert at the top of the main content column, which scrolls naturally with the page.

name: Quote Request
title: Quote Request
description: Configure and submit a geotechnical consulting quote request with dynamic service, service-level, and budget options.

core_features: Load available service types and project types for the quote form using getQuoteFormOptions, honoring any preferred service type from URL parameters.
Allow the user to select a consulting service type and project type, with defaults provided by getQuoteFormOptions or URL parameters.
Capture project context, including optional project name and required location details (city and ZIP), plus optional state and country.
Capture an anticipated construction start date as a required field, optionally prefilled from the startDate URL parameter.
Capture a maximum budget (USD), optionally prefilled from the maxBudgetUsd URL parameter and used when loading service level options.

Retrieve and display service level options for the selected service type via getServiceLevelOptions, including names, descriptions, prices, delivery timeframes, default flags, and whether they include stability modeling.
Enable the user to compare service level options and select one service level to be associated with the quote request.

For slope stability quotes (when the selected service type matches the slope stability service type), enable an action that automatically selects the cheapest service level that includes stability modeling based on the loaded ServiceLevelOption data.
Validate that all required fields are filled in and that a service level is selected before allowing submission.
Submit the completed quote request via submitQuoteRequest and display success or error feedback based on the response.
After a successful submission, provide a navigation link to consultation.html with meetingType and specialty URL parameters for a follow-up consultation.
user_workflows:
Workflow 1: Start a new quote with prefilled context from another page
1. User navigates to quote_request.html with one or more URL parameters (serviceType, projectType, locationCity, locationZip, startDate, maxBudgetUsd) set by a source page.
2. The page reads the URL parameters and passes the serviceType (if present) as preferredServiceType to getQuoteFormOptions.
3. The page sets initial selected service type and project type by combining URL parameters (if valid) with the defaultServiceType and defaultProjectType returned by getQuoteFormOptions.
4. The form pre-populates locationCity, locationZip, anticipatedConstructionStartDate, and maxBudgetUsd from URL parameters where provided.
5. The page calls getServiceLevelOptions with the currently selected serviceType and the current maxBudgetUsd (if any) to load service level options.
6. The user reviews and, if needed, adjusts the prefilled values before proceeding to select a service level and complete the quote.

Workflow 2: Adjust service type and budget to select an appropriate service level
1. User changes the service type selection in the form.
2. The page updates the current serviceType in local state and may update the serviceType in the URL query string.
3. The page calls getServiceLevelOptions with the new serviceType and the current maxBudgetUsd value to reload service level options.
4. The service-level list updates to show options for the new serviceType.
5. User enters or updates the maximum budget in USD.
6. On budget change, the page updates maxBudgetUsd in local state (and optionally in the URL) and calls getServiceLevelOptions again with the same serviceType and the updated maxBudgetUsd.
7. The user reviews the refreshed service level options, compares descriptions and prices, and manually selects the preferred option, or uses an action to select the cheapest option that includes stability modeling when available and applicable.

Workflow 3: Complete and submit quote, then book consultation
1. User fills in required fields: serviceType, projectType, locationCity, locationZip, anticipatedConstructionStartDate, maxBudgetUsd, and selects one service level option.
2. User optionally fills projectName, locationState, locationCountry, and additionalNotes.
3. User clicks the form submit button.
4. The page validates required fields and, if valid, calls submitQuoteRequest with current form values, including selectedServiceLevelId and maxBudgetUsd.
5. If submitQuoteRequest returns success=true, the page displays a confirmation state, disables or locks the form from further editing, and may display key fields from quoteRequest in the response for reference.
6. A 'Book a follow-up consultation' link becomes visible. When the user clicks this link, the browser navigates to consultation.html with meetingType=virtual_meeting and specialty=general_geotechnical as URL parameters.
7. If submitQuoteRequest returns success=false or an error occurs, the page displays an error message and allows the user to correct inputs and resubmit.
interactions: On page load, read URL parameters and invoke getQuoteFormOptions to populate serviceType and projectType dropdown options and defaults.
On service type dropdown change, update internal state, optionally update the serviceType URL parameter, and invoke getServiceLevelOptions to refresh service-level options.
On project type dropdown change, update internal state and optionally update the projectType URL parameter without additional API calls.
On locationCity, locationState, locationZip, and locationCountry input changes, update internal form state for those fields.
On anticipatedConstructionStartDate change, update internal state with the selected date/time representation.

On maxBudgetUsd input change or blur, parse and validate the numeric input, update internal maxBudgetUsd state and the URL parameter, and call getServiceLevelOptions with the current serviceType and updated maxBudgetUsd.
On service level option click (e.g., selecting a radio button or selection control within the list), set selectedServiceLevelId in internal state.

On 'Select cheapest service level that includes stability modeling' action (available when the chosen serviceType is the slope stability service type and service-level data has loaded), compute the minimum-price ServiceLevelOption where includesStabilityModeling is true and set that option as selectedServiceLevelId.

On form submit, validate that required fields are present and that selectedServiceLevelId is set; if valid, call submitQuoteRequest with the current form state and handle the returned success, message, and quoteRequest data.

On successful submit, show a success status component, display any relevant details from the returned quoteRequest, and expose a navigation link to consultation.html with fixed meetingType and specialty URL parameters.
On error from submitQuoteRequest or validation failure, show an error status message and keep the form editable for correction.
On 'Book a follow-up consultation' link click (available only after successful submission), navigate the browser to consultation.html?meetingType=virtual_meeting&specialty=general_geotechnical.
page_functionality.state_logic: On initialization, the page parses URL parameters serviceType, projectType, locationCity, locationZip, startDate, and maxBudgetUsd. The serviceType URL parameter, if present, is passed to getQuoteFormOptions as preferredServiceType. The response from getQuoteFormOptions provides serviceTypes, projectTypes, defaultServiceType, defaultProjectType, and budgetHint. The initial selected serviceType is chosen by prioritizing a valid URL serviceType, then defaultServiceType, then (if necessary) a fallback from the serviceTypes list. The initial selected projectType is chosen by prioritizing a valid URL projectType, then defaultProjectType, then (if necessary) a fallback from the projectTypes list. locationCity and locationZip initial values come from their respective URL parameters when present; otherwise they start empty and must be completed by the user before submission. anticipatedConstructionStartDate initial state is set from the startDate URL parameter if provided and valid; otherwise it starts empty. maxBudgetUsd initial state is set from the maxBudgetUsd URL parameter when provided and valid; otherwise it starts empty. After establishing the initial serviceType and maxBudgetUsd state, the page calls getServiceLevelOptions with serviceType and maxBudgetUsd to populate the service-level list. Whenever the user changes serviceType, the page updates its internal state, may update the serviceType query parameter in the URL (preserving other parameters), and calls getServiceLevelOptions again with the new serviceType and current maxBudgetUsd. When the user changes maxBudgetUsd, the page updates its internal state, updates the maxBudgetUsd query parameter in the URL, and calls getServiceLevelOptions again with the current serviceType and new maxBudgetUsd. The selectedServiceLevelId is maintained in internal state, initialized either from a default option determined by the UI (for example, the option flagged isDefault by the API) or left unset until the user explicitly selects one; no selection is permanently stored outside the current page. For slope stability quotes, identified when current serviceType equals the defined slope stability service type, the component that manages service-level selection can compute, on demand, the cheapest ServiceLevelOption where includesStabilityModeling is true and set selectedServiceLevelId accordingly; this computation is based solely on the currently loaded ServiceLevelOption data and not stored outside local page state. On submit, the page constructs the payload for submitQuoteRequest entirely from the current in-memory state, including serviceType, projectType, projectName, location fields, anticipatedConstructionStartDate, selectedServiceLevelId, maxBudgetUsd, and additionalNotes; it does not read from or write to localStorage or other persistent client storage. The response from submitQuoteRequest is used to update a transient submission state (success or error) and to hold the returned quoteRequest object for display, but this state is not persisted to the URL except that the URL for a follow-up consultation is constructed with its own parameters when the user clicks the consultation link.

components.functionality: Coordinates initial data loading, URL parameter parsing, and shared form state for the quote request page. It reads URL parameters, invokes getQuoteFormOptions and getServiceLevelOptions, merges defaults with URL-driven values, and exposes current state (serviceType, projectType, location data, dates, budget, service level options, selected service level, submission status) to other components.

Displays and manages selection of service type and project type for the quote. It populates dropdown options from getQuoteFormOptions and applies default or URL-specified selections. Changes in these selections update the central state and trigger service-level reloads when the service type changes.

Captures project context fields: projectName (optional), locationCity (required), locationState (optional), locationZip (required), locationCountry (optional), and anticipatedConstructionStartDate (required). It initializes values from URL parameters where present and enforces required fields during overall form validation.

Manages the maximum budget in USD field. It optionally initializes from the maxBudgetUsd URL parameter, displays any budgetHint returned by getQuoteFormOptions, validates that the input is a numeric value, and propagates changes so that getServiceLevelOptions can be re-invoked with the updated budget.

Displays a selectable list or table of service level options returned by getServiceLevelOptions for the current serviceType and maxBudgetUsd. For each ServiceLevelOption it exposes selection controls and shows fields such as name, description, price, deliveryTimeframe, includesStabilityModeling, and isDefault. It supports manual selection of a service level and provides an action to automatically select the cheapest option that includes stability modeling when the current serviceType is the slope stability service type and such options exist.

Wraps all quote request fields into a single submission workflow. It triggers client-side validation of required fields, aggregates current state (including serviceType, projectType, projectName, location data, anticipatedConstructionStartDate, selectedServiceLevelId, maxBudgetUsd, and additionalNotes), and calls submitQuoteRequest. It updates submission state (pending, success, error) based on the response and delegates display of messages to the status component.

Provides a multi-line input for additionalNotes, allowing the user to describe special conditions, constraints, or context for the quote. Its contents are included in the payload sent via submitQuoteRequest.

Displays submission feedback messages based on the result of submitQuoteRequest. It shows success messages and key identifiers from the returned quoteRequest object on success, or error messages when submission fails. It also controls whether the main form remains editable after success.

After a successful quote submission, renders a link that navigates the user to consultation.html with meetingType=virtual_meeting and specialty=general_geotechnical as URL parameters to book a follow-up consultation.

layout.grid_system: Implied 12‑column responsive grid (typical WordPress/corporate layout) with a full-width container for the hero and a centered content column.
layout.layout_pattern: The page follows the mockup’s linear, top-to-bottom flow with a fixed full-width header at the very top, a full-bleed hero section for the Quote Request introduction, and then a single, centered content column that contains all quote-related components stacked vertically. Within this column, components are organized into logical sections: a thin state/progress row just under the hero title, followed by major form sections (service & project selection, project context, budget, service level), then secondary/supporting sections (additional notes, review & submit, consultation link). Components align to the same vertical axis, with internal content generally left-aligned for form fields and center-aligned for titles and summaries, mirroring the hero’s centered text treatment.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page follows the mockup’s linear, top-to-bottom flow with a fixed full-width header at the very top, a full-bleed hero section for the Quote Request introduction, and then a single, centered content column that contains all quote-related components stacked vertically. Within this column, components are organized into logical sections: a thin state/progress row just under the hero title, followed by major form sections (service & project selection, project context, budget, service level), then secondary/supporting sections (additional notes, review & submit, consultation link). Components align to the same vertical axis, with internal content generally left-aligned for form fields and center-aligned for titles and summaries, mirroring the hero’s centered text treatment.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The header occupies roughly 10–15% of the viewport height and remains fixed. The hero fills most of the remaining initial viewport height, with its copy centered vertically in the middle 40–60%. Below the hero, the main form column spans about 8 of 12 grid columns on desktop (~60–70% of page width) and is centered horizontally, with 16–24px internal padding and generous outer margins to the viewport edges. Each major form section is treated as a card-like block separated by 48–64px vertical gaps, while tightly related elements within a section use 16–24px spacing. The quote submission status banner, when visible, spans nearly the full page width but is kept shallow in height (about 48–64px) to avoid overwhelming the hero.
layout_strategies.content_density: The form uses a moderately spacious layout to match the clean, corporate hero: relatively few fields per visible block, ample 16–24px padding within each section, clear grouping of related fields, and plenty of whitespace between sections. Labels and helper text are not crowded; multi-column layouts are used sparingly (e.g., for closely related fields) and collapse to single-column on smaller screens. The density is optimized for clarity and ease of scanning, avoiding long, uninterrupted walls of inputs. Visual emphasis is placed on primary decision areas (service selection, budget, service level, review & submit) while secondary content (additional notes, consultation link, status messages) remains visually lighter and less dense.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: Visual flow mirrors the mockup’s F/Z pattern: the user’s eye starts at the logo in the fixed header, moves across the navigation, then drops to the centered hero title for the Quote Request. From there, it moves downward to the thin state-manager row (step/progress indicator), then continues down the central column through each form section in order. Within sections, left-aligned labels and occasional side-by-side fields create mini Z-patterns from left to right. The review & submit card at the bottom acts as a strong visual anchor with a clear primary action button, while the consultation link is a softer off-ramp at the very bottom, discovered after scanning the main path. Any submission status banner appears near the top and is horizontally spanning, briefly intercepting the visual flow before returning attention to the central column.

layout.overall_description: The page sits within a modern corporate shell: a fixed, full-width teal header with the geotechnical firm’s logo on the left and centered or right-aligned navigation at the top. Below it, a full-bleed hero image with a technical, geotechnical theme stretches edge-to-edge. In the center of the hero, a compact pill label (e.g., “Quote Request”) sits above a large, multi-line white headline that explains the purpose of the page, aligned to the center and occupying roughly the middle half of the hero’s height. Just below this title, a subtle, centered row controlled by the state manager presents the current step in the quote process. As the user scrolls past the hero, a centered column appears beneath, spanning about two-thirds of the page width on desktop and nearly the full width on mobile, with generous lateral padding. Within this column, discrete card-like sections stack one after another: first the service & project selector, then project context details, budget entry, and a horizontal service-level options list. Each section is separated by generous vertical spacing, making the form feel segmented and digestible. Lower down, an additional notes area and a visually distinct review & submit card provide closure, with a primary call-to-action button drawing the eye. A more subtle, center-aligned link offers an alternative path to schedule a consultation. Throughout, the design maintains clean typography, plenty of breathing room, and a clear central spine for content, closely echoing the mockup’s spacious hero and organized navigation.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The quote-request state manager manifests as a slim, horizontally centered row positioned immediately below the main hero headline and any brief supporting text. It sits within the same central content column used throughout the page but visually belongs to the bottom of the hero area, occupying about 60–70% of the page width on desktop and the full width of the central column on smaller screens. Vertically, it uses modest height (around 48px including padding), with 8–16px of spacing above and below to separate it from the hero text above and the first form section below. Within this row, the current step label (e.g., “Step 1 of 4 · Project basics”) is left-aligned relative to the column, with an optional slim progress bar or step dots extending horizontally to the right, reinforcing the left-to-right flow. The background remains transparent or subtly tinted so it does not compete with the hero; typography is medium-weight and smaller than the hero headline, signaling a supportive, guiding role.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Initially positioned within the lower portion of the hero, centered with the main text. As the user scrolls past the hero, the state-manager row becomes sticky relative to the top of the content column, pinning just below the fixed header so that it stays visible at the top of the form while the user moves through the steps.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The service & project selector is the first major form block in the main content column, anchoring the start of the interactive flow. It sits directly below the state manager with a comfortable 32–48px gap between them, clearly separated from the hero area. The block appears as a card-like section spanning roughly 8 of 12 grid columns (about 60–70% of page width) and is centered on the page. It has a light background (white) with a subtle border or shadow and internal padding of 24–32px on all sides. Inside, a section heading (e.g., “Project & Service Details”) is aligned to the left at the top, with 16–24px of space before the first row of fields. On desktop, key fields such as “Service type” and “Project type” are arranged in one or two columns across the card, using a 50/50 split that respects the underlying grid; on smaller screens, these fields stack vertically with 16px gaps. The bottom of the section has 24–32px of breathing room before ending, and there is a 48–64px vertical gap between this card and the next section below, preserving the page’s spacious, corporate feel.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block element within the central column, expanding vertically based on the number of fields. It scales responsively: full-width within the central column on mobile, and approximately two-thirds of the viewport width on larger screens while remaining horizontally centered.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The project context form sits directly under the service & project selector, separated by a generous 48–64px vertical gap to reinforce that it is a distinct step in the user’s mental model. It uses the same centered, card-style treatment: spanning the same 8-of-12-column width, with 24–32px internal padding and a clean white background. A left-aligned section title such as “Site & Project Context” appears at the top, followed by grouped fields for location, subsurface conditions, timeline, and any other contextual details. On desktop, closely related fields (e.g., city/state, approximate start date) can be arranged in two-column rows to create mild horizontal rhythm, while more complex or long-text fields occupy the full card width. Vertical spacing between rows of fields follows the 16–24px scale, avoiding clutter. The bottom of this section aligns visually with the section above, and there is again a 48px–64px gap before the budget area begins, marking a clear transition in content.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, stacked section within the vertical flow of the central column, responsive in width and height. It maintains alignment with the other major card sections to keep a consistent column edge.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The budget input is presented as a focused, slightly more compact section immediately following the project context form. It is vertically separated from the project context card by a 32–48px gap—slightly tighter than the gaps between larger sections to suggest that budget is still part of the same decision cluster. The budget block spans the same central column width (about 60–70% of the page width on desktop) and can either be its own smaller card or the first row of a budget & service-level card. The label (e.g., “Estimated Budget”) is left-aligned at the top, with a single, prominent input field directly below, potentially accompanied by a short helper line (“This helps us tailor our scope and recommendations”). The input itself is horizontally centered within the content column but left-aligned within the field row, with 16px padding around it and sufficient width (50–70% of the central column) to feel substantial and clear. There is 24–32px of vertical space below the budget field before the service-level options list begins, visually pairing these two elements as part of a single decision area.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the central column. On smaller screens, the budget field stretches nearly full width within the column; on larger screens, it maintains a comfortable maximum width to avoid an overly long input line.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The service-level options list is positioned directly beneath the budget input, with a 24–32px gap to imply strong conceptual linkage. It occupies the full width of the central content column and serves as a visually prominent band of selectable options. On desktop, service levels (e.g., “Baseline Assessment,” “Standard Study,” “Comprehensive Consulting”) are displayed as 2–4 cards arranged horizontally in a single row, each card taking roughly 25–33% of the central column width with 16–24px of horizontal spacing between them. Each card has internal padding of 16–24px, a subtle border or shadow, and a clear title, short description, and maybe a small tag for typical budget range, all left-aligned inside the card. On hover/selection, the chosen card uses the brand teal as a border or background accent, while maintaining readability. On smaller screens, these option cards stack vertically with 16–24px vertical spacing, preserving the same card width and padding. The entire service-level area is visually cohesive with the budget section above and ends with a 48–64px gap before the additional notes field below, marking the end of the “configuration” portion of the form.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static grid-like layout within the central column that responds to screen width: horizontal card row on larger screens, single-column stacked cards on small screens. Cards expand vertically based on content but align to the same top baseline where possible.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The review & submit section anchors the bottom of the main form and appears after the additional notes section, separated from it by 48–64px of vertical space to signal it as a final step. This section is a visually distinct, full-width card within the central column, spanning the same 8-of-12-column width but using a slightly tinted background (e.g., very light teal or gray) to stand out from the other white cards. Internal padding is generous (32px or more), and the content is organized into a clear hierarchy: a left-aligned section heading like “Review & Submit,” followed by a concise summary of selected service, project context highlights, budget, and chosen service level. On desktop, this summary can occupy the left two-thirds of the card, with the primary call-to-action button (e.g., “Submit Quote Request”) aligned to the right in a vertical stack or centered vertically in the right third, creating a subtle Z-shaped reading flow from summary text to action. On smaller screens, the summary stacks above the button, both centered horizontally with 16–24px spacing. The bottom of this card has 24–32px of breathing room before its edge, and there is a 32–48px gap below it to the consultation link, allowing it to function as the main visual endpoint of the form experience.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the central column, but acts as a visual anchor at the bottom of the form. On long forms, it may not be immediately visible until the user scrolls, reinforcing a top-to-bottom completion flow.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The additional notes field sits below the service-level options list and above the review & submit section, separated from the options list by a 48–64px gap. It appears as a full-width card within the central column, matching the width and card styling of the other form sections. At the top of the card, a left-aligned heading such as “Additional Notes or Attachments” introduces the section, with 16px of spacing above a large textarea area. The textarea spans most of the card’s width (about 80–90% of the column) and has a comfortable height (equivalent to 4–6 lines of text by default) to invite thoughtful input. Helper text below the field explains what kind of information is useful, separated by 8px of spacing. Internal padding is 24–32px, and the card ends with 24px of space before its bottom. Visually, this section feels slightly less dense than earlier sections because it typically contains a single large field, allowing more whitespace. A 48–64px gap below the card clearly separates it from the review & submit card, marking the transition from data input to confirmation.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, responsive card within the vertical flow, expanding primarily in height as users add text. On smaller screens, the textarea still respects internal padding and stretches to fill the central column width.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The quote submission status appears as a horizontal banner that spans nearly the full width of the page just below the fixed header, ensuring immediate visibility after a submit action. It overlays the top of the content area and may slightly overlap the upper portion of the hero background, while still respecting the header’s fixed position above it. The banner’s height is modest (around 48–64px), with 16px of vertical padding and 24–32px of horizontal padding on each side. On large screens, the banner aligns with the main grid, centering its content so that the message text is aligned with the central column below, even though the background color extends edge-to-edge. Success messages might use a subtle green-tinted background, while errors use a red-tinted one, both with high-contrast text. The message itself is left-aligned within the banner, with an optional icon and a close/dismiss button aligned to the right, following a left-to-right scanning pattern. When visible, the rest of the page content (hero, then central form column) shifts slightly downward so the banner does not obscure interactive elements.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Behaves as a sticky/fixed banner pinned directly beneath the fixed header while active, spanning the full viewport width. When dismissed or not needed, it collapses to zero height, allowing the hero to move up directly under the header.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The quote-to-consultation link is positioned at the very bottom of the main form column, directly below the review & submit section, with a 32–48px vertical gap to maintain visual separation. It is center-aligned within the same central column width, appearing as a single line or two of smaller, lighter-weight text. The copy might read, for example, “Prefer to discuss your project first? Schedule a consultation,” with the actual link portion styled in the brand teal and underlined or otherwise distinguished. The surrounding space is intentionally open, with no enclosing card, reinforcing it as an alternative, secondary path rather than part of the main form. Below this link, additional whitespace (at least 48–64px) leads into the page footer, allowing the link to feel like a soft, graceful endpoint to the page.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static inline element within the central column. It does not stick or float, and remains at the bottom of the content flow, visible as the user reaches the end of the page.

name: Projects & Case Studies
title: Projects & Case Studies
description: Search, filter, and sort project case studies by sector, foundation type, and project value, and navigate to detailed case study and related quote request pages.

core_features: Load available filter and sort options for sectors, foundation types, project value ranges, and sorting from getProjectsFilterOptions.
Display a list of project case studies returned by getProjects, with support for filtering, sorting, and pagination via URL parameters.
Allow users to filter projects by sector, foundation type, project value range, and minimum project value in USD.
Allow users to sort projects using sort options provided by getProjectsFilterOptions (e.g., by project value).
Provide a text-based search input that passes a search term into the getProjects filters.
Enable navigation from each project card to its detailed case study on project_detail.html using the project id.
For eligible transportation/highway-type projects, provide a "Request similar project quote" action that opens quote_request.html with the appropriate projectType parameter.
Support pagination via a page URL parameter mapped to getProjects limit and offset.
user_workflows:
Workflow 1: Initial load with optional pre-applied filters
1. User navigates to projects.html, possibly via a link that includes one or more URL parameters (sector, foundationType, minValueUsd, valueRangeId, sortBy, page).
2. On page load, the state-manager component reads the current URL parameters.
3. The page calls getProjectsFilterOptions with no parameters to retrieve available sectors, foundation types, project value ranges, and sort options.
4. Filter controls (sector, foundation type, project value range, minimum value) and sort controls are populated using the data from getProjectsFilterOptions and the current URL parameter values.
5. The state-manager computes limit and offset based on the current page and configured page size, then calls getProjects with a filters object (including any sector, foundationType, minValueUsd, valueRangeId, and optional search term), sortBy (from URL if present), limit, and offset.
6. The project list displays the returned ProjectCaseStudy items as cards, each showing dynamic data such as title, summary, location, project value display, and completion date when available.

Workflow 2: User refines results with filters and sorting
1. User changes a filter control (e.g., selects a new sector or foundation type) in the filter panel.
2. The filter panel notifies the state-manager of the updated filter values.
3. The state-manager updates the URL parameters to reflect the new filters (e.g., updating sector and foundationType, removing parameters that are cleared). The page parameter is reset to the first page.
4. The state-manager recomputes offset from the updated page, then calls getProjects with the updated filters and existing sortBy.
5. The project list re-renders with the new filtered results.
6. When the user changes the sort option, the state-manager updates the sortBy URL parameter, resets page to the first page, and re-calls getProjects with the same filters and new sortBy.

Workflow 3: User searches projects
1. User enters a search term in the search input and submits the search form.
2. The search form notifies the state-manager of the new search term.
3. The state-manager stores the search term in its in-memory state and resets the page parameter to the first page in the URL.
4. The state-manager calls getProjects with a filters object that includes the current filters (sector, foundationType, minValueUsd, valueRangeId) plus the search term.
5. The project list updates to show only the projects matching the search term and current filters.

Workflow 4: Pagination through project results
1. After viewing the first page of results, the user interacts with a pagination control (e.g., next page or a specific page number) or a load-more-style control.
2. The pagination component calculates the new page number and notifies the state-manager.
3. The state-manager updates the page URL parameter to the new value and recomputes offset: offset = (page - 1) * currentPageSize (where currentPageSize is obtained from configuration, not hardcoded in this design).
4. The state-manager calls getProjects with the same filters and sortBy, but with the new limit and offset.
5. The project list displays the results for the selected page. If a load-more pattern is used, the new results are appended to the existing list instead of replacing it.

Workflow 5: Viewing a project in detail
1. User clicks the title or main image of a project card in the project list.
2. The project list component triggers navigation to project_detail.html with a URL parameter id set to the id of the clicked ProjectCaseStudy.
3. The browser loads project_detail.html?id=<projectId>, where that page is responsible for displaying full case study details.

Workflow 6: Requesting a similar project quote for a transportation/highway project
1. User identifies a transportation/highway-type project in the list.
2. The project card includes a "Request similar project quote" action when business rules indicate that the project corresponds to a highway/roadway type quote.
3. When the user clicks this action, the project list component triggers navigation to quote_request.html with the URL parameter projectType set to the appropriate value (for a highway/roadway-type project, projectType=highway_roadway).
4. The browser loads quote_request.html?projectType=highway_roadway, where the quote request form can pre-select the relevant project type.

Workflow 7: Sharing or bookmarking filtered views
1. After applying filters and sorting, the user copies the current URL from the browser address bar.
2. The copied URL contains the active filter and sortBy parameters (e.g., sector, foundationType, minValueUsd, valueRangeId, sortBy, page).
3. When another user opens this URL, or the original user revisits it, the page initialization workflow (Workflow 1) runs, re-applying the same filters and sorting and loading the corresponding projects with getProjects.
interactions:
Interaction: Change sector filter
- Trigger: User selects or clears a sector option in the sector filter control.
- Behavior: The filter panel sends the new sector value to the state-manager. The state-manager updates the sector URL parameter (removing it if cleared), resets the page parameter to the first page, recomputes offset, and calls getProjects with the updated filters.

Interaction: Change foundation type filter
- Trigger: User selects or clears a foundation type option.
- Behavior: The filter panel sends the new foundationType value to the state-manager. The state-manager updates the foundationType URL parameter (removing it if cleared), resets page, recomputes offset, and calls getProjects.

Interaction: Select project value range
- Trigger: User selects a predefined project value range from a dropdown populated from getProjectsFilterOptions.projectValueRanges.
- Behavior: The filter panel sends the selected valueRangeId to the state-manager. The state-manager updates the valueRangeId URL parameter and removes or retains minValueUsd according to configured precedence (for example, the page can be configured so that selecting a valueRangeId clears minValueUsd). The state-manager resets page, recomputes offset, and calls getProjects with filters including valueRangeId and, if applicable, minValueUsd.

Interaction: Enter minimum project value
- Trigger: User enters a numeric minimum project value in USD and confirms the input (e.g., blur or apply button).
- Behavior: The filter panel validates that the value is numeric, then sends it to the state-manager. The state-manager updates the minValueUsd URL parameter or removes it if the input is cleared, resets page, recomputes offset, and calls getProjects with filters including minValueUsd.

Interaction: Submit search query
- Trigger: User types into the search input and submits the form (e.g., presses Enter or clicks a search button).
- Behavior: The search form sends the current search term to the state-manager. The state-manager stores it in its in-memory filter state, resets page to the first page in the URL, recomputes offset, and calls getProjects with filters including the search term.

Interaction: Change sort option
- Trigger: User selects a different sort option from a dropdown populated from getProjectsFilterOptions.sortOptions.
- Behavior: The sort control sends the selected sortBy value to the state-manager. The state-manager updates the sortBy URL parameter, resets page to the first page, recomputes offset, and calls getProjects with the current filters and new sortBy.

Interaction: Navigate to next/previous page (or load more)
- Trigger: User clicks a pagination control (next, previous, specific page) or a load-more button.
- Behavior: The pagination control computes the new page number and sends it to the state-manager. The state-manager updates the page URL parameter, recomputes offset based on the new page, and calls getProjects with the current filters and sortBy. The project list updates or appends results depending on the pagination pattern.

Interaction: Open case study detail
- Trigger: User clicks on a project card title or main image.
- Behavior: The project list component navigates the browser to project_detail.html?id=<id of the clicked ProjectCaseStudy>.

Interaction: Request similar project quote
- Trigger: On a project card that meets configured criteria for a highway/roadway-type project, user clicks the "Request similar project quote" action.
- Behavior: The project list component navigates to quote_request.html with projectType set to highway_roadway (or another appropriate QuoteRequest.projectType value according to business rules).

Interaction: Reset all filters
- Trigger: User clicks a "Reset filters" action in the filter panel.
- Behavior: The filter panel instructs the state-manager to clear all filter-related URL parameters (sector, foundationType, minValueUsd, valueRangeId) and reset sortBy and page to their default states. The state-manager recomputes offset and calls getProjects with an empty filters object (and default sortBy). The project list then shows the default set of projects.
page_functionality.state_logic:
URL parameters are the primary source of truth for filters, sorting, and pagination. The page reads and writes the following parameters:

- sector (string):
  - On load: If present, included in the filters object as filters.sector when calling getProjects.
  - On change: Updated when the user changes the sector filter; removed when cleared. Changing this parameter resets page to the first page.

- foundationType (string):
  - On load: If present, included in filters.foundationType for getProjects.
  - On change: Updated or removed based on user interaction; changing this parameter resets page.

- minValueUsd (number):
  - On load: Parsed to a number; if valid, included in filters.minValueUsd.
  - On change: When the user edits the minimum value, the parameter is updated or removed. This change resets page.

- valueRangeId (string):
  - On load: If present, included in filters.valueRangeId.
  - On change: Updated when the user selects a project value range; removed when cleared. If configured, selecting a valueRangeId can clear minValueUsd from the URL to avoid conflicting filters. This change resets page.

- sortBy (string):
  - On load: If present, passed directly as the sortBy argument in getProjects. If absent, a default sort option is determined from the sortOptions in getProjectsFilterOptions or other configuration, without hardcoding specific values in this design.
  - On change: When the user selects a new sort option, the sortBy parameter is updated. This change resets page.

- page (number):
  - On load: Parsed as an integer; if invalid or absent, the page defaults to a configured first page value (for example, 1) managed by the state-manager, not hardcoded here.
  - The state-manager computes offset for getProjects as offset = (page - 1) * currentPageSize, where currentPageSize is obtained from configuration.
  - On change: When the user changes the page through the pagination control, the page parameter is updated and offset recalculated.

Internal state (not stored in URL) includes:
- searchTerm (string): Maintained by the state-manager based on the search form. It is included in filters (e.g., filters.searchTerm) when calling getProjects but is not reflected in the URL in this design.

Data fetch order and synchronization:
1. On initial load or whenever URL parameters change (e.g., via browser navigation), the state-manager parses the current URL parameters into an internal state object representing filters, sortBy, and page.
2. The state-manager calls getProjectsFilterOptions to retrieve all selectable options. Controls are initialized using these options and the current internal state derived from URL parameters.
3. The state-manager calculates limit and offset based on currentPageSize and page. It then composes a filters object (including sector, foundationType, minValueUsd, valueRangeId, and searchTerm if present) and calls getProjects(filters, sortBy, limit, offset).
4. The result of getProjects (an array of ProjectCaseStudy) is bound to the project list component. Any subsequent UI interactions that change filters, sorting, or page first update URL parameters (for URL-controlled state) or internal state (for searchTerm), then trigger a fresh call to getProjects with the derived arguments.

components.functionality: Central controller for this page that parses URL parameters, holds the current filter/sort/pagination state, calls getProjectsFilterOptions and getProjects, and distributes data and updates to child components. It ensures that any change in filters, sortBy, or page updates the URL and triggers the appropriate data reload.

Displays and manages filter controls for sector, foundation type, project value range, and minimum project value. It is populated using options from getProjectsFilterOptions and initial values derived from URL parameters via the state-manager. User changes are propagated back to the state-manager.

Provides a text input for users to enter a search term to refine project listings. On submit, it sends the current search term to the state-manager, which then includes it in the filters object passed to getProjects.

Displays available sort options for project listings using the sortOptions from getProjectsFilterOptions and highlights the currently active sort (from URL/state-manager). User selection updates sortBy in the state-manager, which updates the URL and re-fetches projects.

Renders the array of ProjectCaseStudy records returned from getProjects as a list of project cards. Each card displays dynamic fields (e.g., title, summary, location, projectValueDisplay, completionDate, mainImage if available) and provides actions to open the project detail page and, when applicable, to request a similar project quote.

Provides controls for navigating between pages of results (or loading more results). It uses the current page and page size from the state-manager to know which page is active. When the user changes page, it notifies the state-manager, which updates the page URL parameter and calls getProjects with the corresponding limit and offset.

Displays a concise summary of the currently applied filters and sort option (e.g., selected sector, foundation type, project value range, minimum value, and sort label) based on state-manager data and filter options from getProjectsFilterOptions. It helps users understand the active view and can provide quick actions to clear individual filters.

layout.grid_system: Implied 12‑column responsive grid (typical WordPress/corporate layout) with a full-width container for the hero and a centered content column.
layout.layout_pattern: The page follows the mockup’s linear top‑to‑bottom flow with a fixed-height teal header at the very top, then a full-width hero section for the page title and introductory context. Below the hero, the content transitions into a two-column layout within a centered max-width container: a left sidebar for filters and a right main column for search, filter summary, sort control, list of projects, and pagination. Elements in the hero are stacked and center-aligned; elements in the main content area are left-aligned within their columns for scannability.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page follows the mockup’s linear top‑to‑bottom flow with a fixed-height teal header at the very top, then a full-width hero section for the page title and introductory context. Below the hero, the content transitions into a two-column layout within a centered max-width container: a left sidebar for filters and a right main column for search, filter summary, sort control, list of projects, and pagination. Elements in the hero are stacked and center-aligned; elements in the main content area are left-aligned within their columns for scannability.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The header occupies roughly 10–12% of the viewport height. The hero beneath it takes up the remaining initial viewport (about 80–90% of the height), with the title and supporting text centered within the middle 40–60% of that height over a full-bleed background image. The main content area below the hero uses a 12-column grid, with the filter sidebar consuming about 3–4 columns (25–30% width) and the project list area using the remaining 8–9 columns (70–75% width). Vertical spacing between major sections (hero → filters/search band → list → pagination) is generous (48–64px), reflecting the spacious corporate feel.
layout_strategies.content_density: The hero remains visually simple, with a bold headline and minimal supporting information, letting the background image provide visual richness. Below, controls (filters, search, sort) are grouped into clear bands with comfortable padding (16–24px), separated by white space so each function is easy to identify. The project list is moderately dense: cards or rows are clearly separated with 16–24px vertical gaps, but not so sparse that the page feels empty, maintaining a professional, information-focused rhythm.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The user’s eye first lands on the logo and navigation in the fixed header, sweeps to the right across the navigation, then drops down into the centered hero: first the small category pill (e.g., “Projects & Case Studies”), then the bold multi-line page headline, then a lean meta row (e.g., project count and a subtle link to request a quote). As the user scrolls, the flow continues into the main content band: left to the filter sidebar, then across to the prominent search bar at the top of the project list, down to the filter summary and sort control row, and finally into the vertically ordered list of project cards and the pagination controls at the bottom.

layout.overall_description: The page opens with a fixed teal header spanning the full width of the browser, containing a left-aligned logo and right-aligned navigation that sit within a centered content container. Immediately below, a full-bleed hero image fills the remaining viewport height, featuring subtle geotechnical imagery (e.g., drill rigs, soil layers) with a dark overlay. In the vertical center of this hero, a compact pill-shaped label reading something like “Projects & Case Studies” appears above a large, multi-line white headline describing the case studies library. Beneath the headline, a single subtle meta line might show the total number of case studies and a compact link such as “Request a project consultation,” all center-aligned and constrained within a central column that leaves generous left and right margins. Scrolling down reveals the main content area: a wide, white-backed band centered on the page, with the layout splitting into two columns. On the left, a filter sidebar occupies roughly one quarter of the width, running from just below the hero down along the content, with individual filter groups separated by soft dividers and ample vertical spacing. On the right, occupying the remaining width, the search bar sits at the top with strong visual prominence, followed by a slim row combining the active filter summary on the left and the sort control aligned to the right. Below this control band, a vertical list or grid of project case study cards fills the page, each card using clear typographic hierarchy and consistent spacing. At the very bottom of the list, centered pagination controls sit with generous breathing room above and below, providing a clear conclusion to the content. The overall effect mirrors the mockup: a structured, modern corporate layout with a strong hero and a clear, top-to-bottom visual path from global overview down into detailed project exploration.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The state manager is a non-visual component that underpins the entire Projects & Case Studies page. Conceptually, it spans the full page and sits behind the visual layout, tracking URL parameters, filter selections, search queries, sort order, and pagination state. It does not occupy any visible space on the screen, but structurally it is associated with the main content region beneath the hero, orchestrating data flow between the filter sidebar on the left and the list, search, sort, and pagination controls on the right.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual global controller; no rendered spatial footprint, logically attached to the central content container for state management.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The filter panel appears immediately below the hero, anchored on the left side of the main content area within a centered max-width container. On desktop, it occupies roughly 3–4 columns in a 12-column grid (about 25–30% of the page width), starting near the same vertical baseline as the search bar on the right. The panel stretches downward as needed to accommodate all filter sections (e.g., Sector, Foundation Type, Project Value), with the top of the panel sitting about 48px below the bottom edge of the hero to preserve spacious separation. Internally, it uses 16–24px padding on all sides, with each filter group separated by 16–24px of vertical space and a light divider rule. The panel’s background is solid white (or a very light neutral) to contrast against the page background and the busy hero above, and it feels like a stable, left-anchored column. On larger screens, the filter panel remains visually aligned so that its left edge matches the left edge of the hero’s inner content column, reinforcing the grid. On mobile and small tablets, this panel collapses into a full-width accordion or slide-down panel positioned above the search form, spanning nearly 100% of the content width with 16px side margins, while retaining similar internal padding.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the main content flow but uses sticky behavior on desktop so that the top of the panel pins approximately 16–24px below the fixed header as the user scrolls, keeping filters accessible while the project list scrolls independently on the right.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The search form is the first and most prominent element in the right-hand main content column below the hero, directly opposite the top of the filter panel. It sits in the upper-right portion of the page within the same centered content container, with its left edge aligned to the internal grid column adjacent to the filter panel. On desktop, the search bar spans roughly 70–80% of the width of the right column, leaving subtle space on the far right for air and alignment with the sort control beneath. It is positioned about 48px below the bottom of the hero, matching the vertical offset of the filter panel to create a clean horizontal visual band. The search field itself is a single, horizontally oriented input with rounded or softly squared corners, 48px–56px tall, and generous internal padding (12–16px), with a search icon on the left and placeholder text such as “Search projects by keyword, location, or client.” Above the input, an optional compact label or heading like “Search projects” may appear, left-aligned, with 8–12px of space to the field. On mobile, the search form becomes full-width (minus 16px horizontal margins), and shifts to sit directly below the collapsed filter toggle, maintaining a top-of-main-content prominence.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static element in the main content flow, anchored to the top of the right column; it moves naturally with page scroll and does not overlap with other controls.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The filter summary appears directly beneath the search form within the right-hand column, forming the left side of a thin control bar that spans the full width of the right column. It starts about 16px below the bottom of the search field, in line with the mockup’s preference for modest but clear separation. The summary is left-aligned and typically consists of a short status line (e.g., “Showing 24 projects”) followed by small pill-shaped tokens representing active filters (e.g., “Infrastructure,” “Pile Foundations,” “> $10M”), each with consistent 4–8px horizontal spacing between them. The entire filter summary area occupies roughly the left two-thirds of this horizontal control band, leaving visual room on the right for the sort control. The background remains white, so the summary reads as text and pills floating in the content area rather than inside a heavy container. On smaller screens, the summary wraps to multiple lines as needed, maintaining its position above the project list but stacking above the sort control if horizontal space is constrained.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static inline element within a horizontal control row; on desktop it shares the row with the sort control and adjusts its width based on content, wrapping gracefully; on mobile, it stacks above the sort control when space is limited.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The sort control sits within the same horizontal band as the filter summary, aligned to the far right edge of the right-hand content column. Vertically, it is aligned with the center of the filter summary text, positioned approximately 16px below the search form to share the same baseline. The control itself is compact and concise: typically a label like “Sort by” followed by a dropdown or segmented control (e.g., “Most recent,” “Project value (high to low),” “A–Z”), with a total width of about 20–30% of the right column. There is a small gap (8–16px) between the filter summary area and the sort control, making the two clearly distinct but part of the same control band. The sort control does not use a heavy container; instead, it appears as a lightweight dropdown or selector aligned to the right, following the corporate, uncluttered pattern. On tablet and mobile, when the right column narrows, the sort control drops to its own row directly below the filter summary, left-aligned but still visually separated by 8–12px of vertical space.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static element in the main content flow; right-aligned within the horizontal control band on wider viewports and automatically wraps to a new line beneath the filter summary on narrower viewports.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The projects list occupies the majority of the right-hand column and is the main focus of the content area. It begins approximately 24px below the filter summary/sort control band, maintaining the page’s moderate vertical rhythm. On desktop, it spans the full width of the right column (about 70–75% of the page width), with each project represented as either a full-width row or a two-column grid of cards, depending on design choice; a common approach is a two-column grid where each card uses about 48% of the right column width with a 4% horizontal gutter. Each card or row has 16–24px of internal padding and 16–24px of vertical spacing between adjacent items, ensuring clarity and readability. Cards follow a typography hierarchy similar to the hero: project title is prominent, with smaller lines for sector, foundation type, location, and a brief description. A subtle thumbnail image or technical graphic may be placed at the top or left of each card, but the text remains the primary anchor. The list extends downward and can span multiple viewport heights; as the user scrolls, the list scrolls beneath the fixed header while the filter panel on the left may remain sticky. On tablet and mobile, the list shifts to a single-column layout where each card spans nearly 100% of the content width, with consistent vertical spacing preserved.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static scrolling content; it forms the main scrolling area of the page, extending downward as projects are loaded, while adjacent elements like the header stay fixed and the filter panel may remain sticky on taller screens.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The pagination control is positioned at the bottom of the projects list, centered horizontally within the right-hand column. It appears after a comfortable vertical gap of about 32–48px from the last project card, signaling the end of the list. The control itself is a compact, horizontally arranged set of elements: previous/next arrows and page numbers or a “Load more” button, all placed within a narrow horizontal band that spans about 40–60% of the right column’s width. It is visually light—no heavy bars—using subtle outlines or filled buttons aligning with the brand color for the active state. Below the pagination control, there is an additional 48–64px of whitespace before the bottom of the page or footer content, mirroring the generous spacing between major sections seen in the hero and top controls. On mobile, the pagination remains centered but may expand closer to full width to accommodate larger tap targets.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static element anchored to the end of the project list; it becomes visible only once the user scrolls through the current set of projects and remains at the bottom of the content stream.

name: Project / Case Study Detail
title: Project / Case Study Detail
description: Displays full details of a selected geotechnical project case study, including narrative, key metrics, related projects, related services, and calls to action for similar work.

core_features: Load a specific project case study based on the URL parameter `id` by calling `getProjectCaseStudyDetail`.
Display core project information including title, sector, foundation type, location, project value, and completion date.
Show a project summary with an expandable 'Read full case study' control that reveals or jumps to the full narrative content.
Present the full case study narrative content returned by the interface.
Display a main project image when available.
List related projects from `relatedProjects`, each linking to its own Project / Case Study Detail page via `id` in the URL.
List related consulting services from `relatedServices`, each linking to its own service detail page using the service's `pageFilename`.
Provide navigation back to the projects listing page (`projects.html`).
Provide a direct link to view more projects filtered by sector and foundation type (e.g. commercial buildings with pile foundations) via URL parameters.
Provide a call-to-action to request a quote for a similar foundation design, navigating to `quote_request.html` with the `serviceType` URL parameter set to `foundation_design`.
Provide a call-to-action to book a consultation for a similar project, navigating to `consultation.html` with the `specialty` URL parameter set to `foundation_design`.
Handle loading, error, and 'project not found' states when retrieving project data.
user_workflows: 1) Open case study from project listing: User clicks a project item on `projects.html` → browser navigates to `project_detail.html?id={projectId}` → page reads `id` from URL → page calls `getProjectCaseStudyDetail` with `projectCaseStudyId = id` → on success, project header, summary, key metrics, main image, full content, related projects, and related services are displayed.

2) Read the full case study narrative: User lands on `project_detail.html?id={projectId}` → sees project summary and an initial section of content → user clicks the 'Read full case study' control → page toggles state to expanded or scrolls to the full content section → entire `project.content` field becomes visible or is brought into view.

3) Navigate between related projects: User scrolls to the related projects section → sees a list populated from `relatedProjects` returned by `getProjectCaseStudyDetail` → user clicks a related project item → browser navigates to `project_detail.html?id={relatedProjectId}` → new page instance repeats the loading workflow, now showing the newly selected project.

4) Explore related services: User scrolls to the related services section → sees services populated from `relatedServices` (each mapped to a consulting service detail page via `pageFilename`) → user clicks a service associated with slope stability or subsurface investigation → browser navigates to the corresponding service page (for example `service_slope_stability.html` or `service_subsurface_investigation.html`).
5) Go back to all projects: User clicks the 'Back to all projects' control → browser navigates to `projects.html` with no extra URL parameters, where the full project list is shown.

6) View more projects in a similar category: User scrolls to a section offering a link to view more projects for the same sector and foundation type (e.g. commercial buildings with pile foundations) → user clicks this link → browser navigates to `projects.html?sector=commercial_buildings&foundationType=pile_foundations` to show a filtered listing.

7) Request a quote for a similar foundation design: User reads the case study and decides to request similar services → user clicks the 'Request a similar foundation design quote' call-to-action → browser navigates to `quote_request.html?serviceType=foundation_design`, where the quote request form is preconfigured for foundation design services.

8) Schedule a consultation for a similar project: User reads the case study and wants to discuss a similar project → user clicks the 'Discuss a similar project' consultation call-to-action → browser navigates to `consultation.html?specialty=foundation_design`, where available consultation slots for foundation design can be selected.

9) Handle error or missing project: User navigates to `project_detail.html` without an `id` or with an invalid `id` → page attempts to read `id` from URL → if absent or if `getProjectCaseStudyDetail` returns an error or no `project`, the page shows an error/not-found message and provides a link back to `projects.html`.
interactions: On page load, the page controller reads the `id` URL parameter and triggers `getProjectCaseStudyDetail` with `projectCaseStudyId` set to that value.
If `getProjectCaseStudyDetail` resolves successfully, the project, related projects, and related services data are propagated to child components via bindings.
If `getProjectCaseStudyDetail` fails or returns no `project`, the page enters an error/not-found state and offers a navigation link back to `projects.html`.
User clicks 'Back to all projects' → the page navigates to `projects.html`.

User clicks 'View more commercial pile foundation projects' (or an equivalent link for similar sector/foundation combinations if implemented generically) → the page navigates to `projects.html` with `sector=commercial_buildings` and `foundationType=pile_foundations` URL parameters.

User clicks the 'Read full case study' toggle → a component-level state flag is updated to indicate expanded/collapsed view → the component either reveals the remainder of the `content` field or scrolls the page to the full content section.
User clicks the 'Collapse case study' control (if provided) → the expanded state flag is reset → the content component reverts to a summary/partial view.
User clicks a related project item → browser navigates to `project_detail.html?id={clickedRelatedProjectId}`.

User clicks a related service item → browser navigates to that service’s page using its `pageFilename` field as the target (e.g. `service_slope_stability.html`, `service_subsurface_investigation.html`, or other service pages).
User clicks the 'Request a similar foundation design quote' CTA → browser navigates to `quote_request.html?serviceType=foundation_design`.
User clicks the 'Discuss a similar project' consultation CTA → browser navigates to `consultation.html?specialty=foundation_design`.
page_functionality.state_logic: The page state is driven entirely by the URL parameter `id` and the result of the `getProjectCaseStudyDetail` interface. When `project_detail.html` loads, it reads `id` from the URL query string. If `id` is present, the page calls `getProjectCaseStudyDetail` with `projectCaseStudyId` equal to this value. While waiting for the response, the page remains in a 'loading' state. Once the response arrives: if a `project` object is returned, the page switches to a 'loaded' state and binds the `project`, `relatedProjects`, and `relatedServices` data to their respective components; if the response indicates an error or lacks a `project`, the page enters an 'error/not-found' state. Navigating to a related project updates the `id` in the URL, which effectively reloads the page and repeats this process for the new `projectCaseStudyId`. The 'Read full case study' behavior is handled via client-side UI state (e.g. a boolean such as `isContentExpanded`) within the content component and does not modify the URL; however, anchors (such as `#full-case-study`) may be appended to the URL for scrolling without affecting data loading. No localStorage is used; all navigational state is represented either in the URL parameters (`id`, `sector`, `foundationType`, `serviceType`, `specialty`) or in transient in-memory component state for UI presentation (such as toggling collapsed/expanded content).

components.functionality: Coordinates reading of URL parameters, triggers data loading via `getProjectCaseStudyDetail`, manages top-level loading/error state, and passes loaded data to child components.
event_handlers: onPageLoad: Read `id` from URL query string and call `getProjectCaseStudyDetail({ projectCaseStudyId: id })`.
onDataLoaded: When `getProjectCaseStudyDetail` returns successfully, store `project`, `relatedProjects`, and `relatedServices` in page-level state and switch from loading to loaded state.
onDataLoadError: If the interface call fails or returns no `project`, set state to error/not-found and notify the error display component.
onUrlChange: If the URL `id` parameter changes (e.g., via navigation to a related project), re-trigger `getProjectCaseStudyDetail` with the new `projectCaseStudyId`.

components.functionality: Displays the main title and high-level summary information for the current project case study.
Shows key project metrics such as location, project value, completion date, and other core attributes for quick scanning.
Displays the main image for the project if provided, or hides the image area when `mainImage` is not present.
Shows a short, easily scannable version of the case study narrative and provides a 'Read full case study' control that toggles or scrolls to the full content.
event_handlers: onToggleExpand: Toggles internal state between collapsed and expanded views of the narrative content.
onClickReadFullCaseStudy: Optionally scrolls the viewport to the full content section (e.g., an anchor where the complete `content` is rendered).

components.functionality: Renders the full text narrative of the case study returned in `project.content` and serves as the destination when the user chooses to read the full case study.
Provides a navigational link back to the complete projects listing.
event_handlers: onClick: Navigate browser to `projects.html` (no additional URL parameters).

components.functionality: Provides a link for the user to view more projects with a specific combination of sector and foundation type, such as commercial buildings with pile foundations.
Displays a list of related project case studies supplied by `getProjectCaseStudyDetail`, each item linking to its own project detail page.
event_handlers: onClickRelatedProjectItem: For the clicked related project, navigate browser to `project_detail.html?id={relatedProject.id}`.

components.functionality: Displays consulting services related to the current project, using the `relatedServices` array, and links out to each service's page.
event_handlers: onClickRelatedServiceItem: For the clicked service, navigate browser to the service detail page using its `pageFilename` (e.g., `service_slope_stability.html`, `service_subsurface_investigation.html`, or other filenames).

components.functionality: Provides call-to-action controls to request a quote or book a consultation for a similar foundation design project.
Indicates loading status while data is being fetched and displays an error or not-found message with recovery navigation when the project cannot be loaded.
event_handlers: onClickBackToProjectsFromError: Navigate browser to `projects.html` when the user chooses to return from an error state.

layout.grid_system: Implied 12‑column responsive grid (typical WordPress/corporate layout) with a full-width container for the hero and a centered content column.
layout.layout_pattern: Use a linear, top-to-bottom structure anchored by a full-bleed hero. At the very top is the global fixed header (logo left, nav right, implied from the mockup but handled outside these components). Directly beneath it, the project hero combines the project-main-image as a background with the project-header content centered over it (category pill, headline, metadata). Immediately below the hero, the main content column begins: first a slim strip with navigation links (back and similar category), then the key metrics panel, then the summary/read-more control leading into the full project content. Below the narrative content, two related-items sections (projects and services) appear in sequence, followed by a full-width CTA panel near the page bottom.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: Use a linear, top-to-bottom structure anchored by a full-bleed hero. At the very top is the global fixed header (logo left, nav right, implied from the mockup but handled outside these components). Directly beneath it, the project hero combines the project-main-image as a background with the project-header content centered over it (category pill, headline, metadata). Immediately below the hero, the main content column begins: first a slim strip with navigation links (back and similar category), then the key metrics panel, then the summary/read-more control leading into the full project content. Below the narrative content, two related-items sections (projects and services) appear in sequence, followed by a full-width CTA panel near the page bottom.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The global header occupies roughly 10–15% of the viewport height. The hero (project-main-image + project-header) takes up the remainder of the first viewport, with the text block centered vertically within the middle 40–60% of the hero height.
