Below the hero, all content sits in a centered column spanning about 70–80% of the viewport width on desktop (max-width around a typical 12-column layout). Section spacing follows the 8px base with 48–64px vertical gaps between major bands (metrics, content, related items, CTA). Cards and panels use 16–24px internal padding. The CTA panel and hero behave as full-bleed bands, while other content respects the centered column.
layout_strategies.content_density: Maintain a moderately spacious layout: few elements per band, generous white space, and clear separation between sections. The hero has large typography with only a category pill, title, and metadata row. Key metrics appear as 3–4 concise data points in a single well-spaced panel. The main content is set with comfortable line lengths and ample line-height. Related lists use a small grid but avoid crowding by using consistent card spacing. No area feels overloaded; whitespace around and between modules keeps the corporate, premium tone.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: Follow an F/Z hybrid flow. On page load, the user’s eye first hits the global logo area at the top-left, then scans across the top nav. The gaze then drops to the centered category pill in the hero, follows down to the bold multi-line project title, and then to the metadata row below it. Scrolling continues downward into the key metrics panel and summary/read-more, naturally leading into the full content. From there, attention moves through related projects and services, ending on the highly contrasted CTA band at the bottom. Secondary navigation links (back, similar category) are aligned near the top of the main content area as subtle entry/exit aids, not as primary focal points.

layout.overall_description: The page presents as a polished, corporate case study view with a strong full-bleed hero at the top. A fixed-height teal global header sits across the top edge, with the brand logo on the left and navigation on the right. Immediately below, a large edge-to-edge project image fills the screen width; this image forms the hero background. Over the center of this image, a compact category pill appears near the upper-middle, followed by a bold, multi-line white project title centered on the page, and a single-line metadata row beneath it showing author/avatar, date, and comment or project detail counts. The hero occupies most of the initial viewport, giving the project headline dominance. Beneath the hero, the layout transitions into a centered content column that floats within generous side margins. At the top of this column, a slim row holds a left-aligned back link and a right-aligned similar-category link. Below this, a clean key-metrics panel stretches across the content width, presenting 3–4 key project stats in a horizontal row. The project summary and its read-more toggle sits next, opening into the longer narrative section directly below. The full content reads like a structured article with clear paragraphs and ample spacing. Past the main story, two related sections stack vertically: related projects laid out as a few cards in a responsive grid, followed by related services arranged similarly but with fewer cards. The page concludes with a full-width CTA panel that returns to a hero-like treatment: a strong brand-colored band spanning edge-to-edge, a concise call-to-action title and body text centered within the main column, and a prominent button. Loading and error states appear centered over the main hero or content area as overlays or in-place messages, temporarily replacing the normal content while preserving overall structure.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This invisible controller component spans the full page, sitting behind all visible sections and orchestrating their order. Conceptually, it sequences the global header (outside this component list), then the hero (project-main-image with the project-header overlaid), followed by the main content column (navigation links row → key metrics → summary/read-more → full content), then the related items lists, and finally the CTA panel. It also determines whether the status indicator is shown instead of the normal content. Spatially, it does not occupy visible space; it simply defines that all components align to a central vertical axis and respect the 12-column grid and 48–64px vertical section spacing.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual structural controller; defines stacking order from top to bottom and toggles visibility of loading and error overlays across the main content area.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The project header content is centered both horizontally and vertically within the hero area, overlaid on top of the project-main-image. It occupies approximately the central 50–60% of the hero width on desktop, aligned to the main content column so that the text aligns with content below. At the top of this block, a small category pill sits centered, with 16–24px of space above and below. Directly beneath, the main project title spans two to three lines, set in large, high-contrast type and center-aligned. It occupies roughly 60–70% of the column width, with generous line spacing. Below the title, a single horizontal row displays metadata (avatar or initials on the left, then author name, date, and comments or project indicators) with the row also centered and limited in width. Vertical spacing within this stack uses multiples of 8px, with approximately 16px between category and title, and 12–16px between title and metadata. The header text block is positioned so that it appears roughly in the vertical middle of the hero image, leaving enough image visible above and below to frame it.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Sits as an absolutely-positioned or flex-centered layer over the project-main-image, staying centered within the hero area on all screen sizes; text wraps responsively while maintaining central alignment.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The key metrics panel appears immediately below the hero, inset within the main centered content column. It spans about 80–100% of the column width (depending on breakpoint), with 24px internal padding on all sides. Visually it reads as a low-profile horizontal band with 3–4 metrics arranged side-by-side in equal-width columns on desktop. Each metric has a compact label above a bold numeric value or short phrase, aligned either left within each metric cell or center-aligned, depending on overall typography. There is 16–24px of vertical space above the panel (separating it from the hero) and 32–48px below it before the summary area. On narrower screens, the metrics stack into two columns or a single column, but the panel maintains generous vertical spacing between items to preserve a clean, corporate look.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Flows as a full-width block within the main content column; responsive behavior shifts metrics from a horizontal row on desktop to stacked groups on tablet/mobile, preserving consistent padding and section spacing.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The project-main-image functions as the full-width hero background. It sits directly beneath the global header and spans the entire viewport width, bleeding edge-to-edge with no horizontal margins. Its height is calibrated so that, together with the header, it fills roughly the first viewport height on desktop (typically 60–80% of viewport height for the image area alone). The image is set to cover the area (no distortion), with important focal content centered to sit behind the project-header text. The project-header block is overlaid on top of this image in the central region. On scroll, the image remains fixed in position within its section, with 0px margin at the top and 48–64px of margin below before the next section. On small screens, the height may reduce slightly but still maintain a dominant hero presence.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Behaves as a full-bleed hero background. Typically uses background-size: cover and centers the focal point. Stays static relative to the page (no parallax by default) and does not scroll independently of the content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Located directly below the key metrics panel, the summary and read-more block sits within the same centered content column. It aligns to the left edge of the column, occupying about 70–80% of the width for optimal line length, with the right margin left as whitespace on larger screens. At the top of this block, a subtle subheading like “Project Overview” may appear (if present), followed by a 2–4 sentence summary paragraph. Beneath the summary, aligned left, is a read-more link or button with 16px top margin, visually distinct but not as strong as the main CTA. When collapsed, only this summary portion is visible; when expanded, the full-content section immediately below feels like a natural continuation of the story. Vertical spacing above and below the block is comfortable: 32–40px between it and the metrics panel, and 24–32px before the full content begins.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline block within the main content column. The read-more control toggles the perceived length of nearby content but does not change its own position; it simply reveals or hides the continuation in the full content section below.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The full content section sits immediately below the summary/read-more block as the main narrative body. It aligns with the same left edge and width as the summary, ensuring a consistent column. The content flows in a single vertical column with paragraphs, subheadings, and possibly small inline images or diagrams, all respecting a 16–24px vertical rhythm between text elements. The section often occupies 1–2 screen heights of content, with a max-width designed for readability (roughly 60–75 characters per line). There is a clear 32–48px gap above this section (from the summary) and a 48–64px gap below before the related-projects-list begins. If the read-more toggle is used, this section may be partially or fully hidden until expanded, but it always occupies the central content column when visible.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard scrolling content area within the main column; expands vertically as needed. On smaller screens it becomes full-width with consistent padding on both sides and maintains the same typographic rhythm.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This back link appears as part of a slim navigation row at the very top of the main content column, just beneath the hero (and typically just above or flush with the top of the key metrics panel). It is left-aligned with the column, occupying minimal horizontal space—a simple text link with an optional left-pointing icon. There is 24–32px of vertical space separating it from the bottom of the hero, and about 8–12px of space below it before any dividing rule or the next element. It sits on the same horizontal line as the similar-category link, creating a light top utility bar that frames the content below.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline in a horizontal flex row with the similar-category link, anchored to the main content column. On narrow screens, it may stack above the similar-category link while remaining left-aligned.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This link is positioned in the same slim navigation row as the back link, at the very top of the main content column. It is right-aligned on the same horizontal baseline as the back link, typically styled as a subtle pill or text link indicating the broader category (e.g., “More in Slope Stabilization”). The row spans the width of the content column, leaving ample whitespace above and below, and the right-aligned placement provides a light counterbalance to the left-aligned back link, forming a simple F-pattern line before the eye drops into the key metrics. On narrow viewports where horizontal space is limited, this link may move below the back link with a small 8–16px vertical gap.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Shares a horizontal flex container with the back link, aligning itself to the right on wider screens. It responds to viewport changes by potentially stacking below the back link while maintaining comfortable spacing.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The related projects list appears beneath the full-content section, separated by 48–64px of vertical space. It sits in the main centered column, with a section heading (e.g., “Related Projects”) left-aligned at the top. Below the heading, a grid of 3–4 project cards is laid out in one or two rows depending on the number of items and screen width. On desktop, cards typically appear in 3 columns across, each card occupying about one-third of the column width with 16–24px gaps between cards horizontally and vertically. The section spans the full width of the content column but maintains generous margins to the viewport edges. The cards themselves are kept simple: project title, small thumbnail, and maybe a short tag or date, following the clean, spacious aesthetic. There is a 40–56px gap below this section before the related-services-list begins.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Behaves as a responsive grid within the main content column. Cards wrap to fewer columns or a single column as the viewport narrows, while the section’s top and bottom spacing stays aligned with the overall vertical rhythm.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The related services list sits directly below the related projects section, separated by a clear 40–56px vertical gap. It uses the same centered content column and alignment. At the top is a left-aligned section heading such as “Services Used” or “Related Services.” The list itself is slightly more compact than the projects grid—typically 2–3 service cards per row on desktop, with each card containing a service name, a brief one-line descriptor, and possibly a small icon. Cards are arranged in a responsive grid with 16–24px spacing. The entire section visually reads as a supporting band, not as heavy as the hero or CTA, but clearly distinguished from the narrative content above with its own grouping and spacing. There is 48–64px of space beneath this section leading into the project-cta-panel.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Responsive grid block inside the main column; cards wrap to fewer columns on smaller screens. Maintains consistent section margins above and below, integrating smoothly into the vertical scroll flow.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The CTA panel anchors the bottom of the page, appearing after the related services with a generous 48–64px gap. It spans the full viewport width as a full-bleed band, similar in presence to the hero but shorter in height (roughly 25–35% of the viewport on desktop). The background uses the strong brand color (e.g., teal or a variant) to create a high-contrast block. Within this band, content is centered in the same main column used above. The CTA content is vertically and horizontally centered within the panel, forming a compact stack: a short, bold heading, a brief supporting line or two of copy, and a primary button aligned left or center. The internal padding is generous (32–48px top and bottom), and the CTA is visually dominant in this region without feeling heavy, serving as the final key action prompt before any global footer.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Full-width section anchored near the bottom of the page. Content is centered within the standard column. On smaller screens, it maintains its full-bleed behavior, with text and button stacking and remaining centered or left-aligned depending on the brand style.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The loading and error display overlays or replaces the main content area depending on state. On initial load, a loading indicator appears centered vertically in the viewport and horizontally within the page, overlapping the hero region where the project-main-image and project-header would normally appear. The surrounding background may use a subtle neutral fill to keep focus on the indicator while still showing hints of the page structure. For error states, a concise error message box sits in the same central position, roughly the width of the main content column, with a clear title, brief explanation, and possibly a retry link or button. When active, this component minimizes visual clutter by hiding or dimming underlying project content, keeping the user’s attention centered. It respects the overall spacing system but is allowed to float above other elements for clarity.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Behaves as a modal-like overlay or stateful replacement centered in the viewport. It temporarily sits on top of other page elements with a higher z-index, then disappears or collapses back into the invisible state once loading completes or errors are resolved.

name: Resources & Knowledge Center
title: Resources & Knowledge Center
description: Search, filter, sort, and browse geotechnical resources, then open each resource’s detail page to view and download.

core_features: Initialize filter and sort controls by calling getResourceFilterOptions and applying any URL parameters as the initial state.
List resources returned by getResources with metadata such as title, type, topic, level, and published date.
Provide filter controls for resource type, topic/discipline, user level, and minimum published date.
Provide a free-text search input (q) that is passed to getResources via its filters parameter.
Provide sort controls (sortBy) driven by sortOptions returned from getResourceFilterOptions.
Support pagination using URL parameter page mapped to getResources limit and offset.
Maintain all filter, search, sort, and pagination state in the URL query parameters so the list is shareable and bookmarkable.
Allow navigation to a resource detail page (resource_detail.html) by clicking a resource row or dedicated link, passing the resource id as a URL parameter.
user_workflows: Browse with default or pre-applied filters: 1) User arrives on resources.html from navigation or a filtered link; 2) Page reads URL parameters (resource_type, topic, level, publishedAfter, sortBy, q, page); 3) Page calls getResourceFilterOptions and builds filter controls; 4) Page maps URL parameters to the available filter options and sort options; 5) Page calls getResources with filters derived from URL state and displays the resulting ResourceItem list; 6) User scrolls and optionally changes page via pagination controls.

Filter by type, topic, level, and date: 1) User adjusts filter controls (e.g., selects a resource type, topic, level, or sets a published-after date); 2) Filter component updates the corresponding URL parameters and resets page to 1; 3) Controller observes the URL change and calls getResources with updated filters; 4) List updates to show only resources matching the selected filters.

Search within resources: 1) User enters a query string into the search input and submits; 2) Search form updates the q URL parameter and resets page to 1; 3) Controller calls getResources with filters including the q value; 4) Results list updates to display matching resources.

Sort results: 1) User changes the sort option using the sort control; 2) Sort control updates the sortBy URL parameter and resets page to 1; 3) Controller calls getResources with the selected sortBy; 4) Resources are re-rendered in the new order.

Paginate through results: 1) User clicks next or previous page, or directly selects a page number; 2) Pagination control updates the page URL parameter; 3) Controller recalculates offset from the page parameter and calls getResources with the same filters/sort but new offset; 4) Resource list updates to show the new page.

Open resource details: 1) User clicks on a resource title or a 'View details' link in a resource row; 2) Resource list component triggers navigation to resource_detail.html with ?id=<ResourceItem.id>; 3) User is taken to the resource detail page to view or download the resource.
interactions: On initial page load, the controller reads URL parameters, calls getResourceFilterOptions, applies any matching filter/sort values to controls, then calls getResources with the derived filters and sortBy.

When a filter (resource type, topic, level, published-after date) changes, the filter panel updates the URL parameters, removes parameters for cleared filters, and triggers a refresh via getResources.
When the search form is submitted, it updates the q parameter in the URL, removes q when cleared, and triggers getResources with the new query.
When the sort option changes, the sort control updates sortBy in the URL and triggers getResources with the new sort order.
When the user navigates via pagination controls, the pagination component updates the page parameter and triggers getResources with adjusted offset.

If no resources are returned from getResources for the current filters, an empty-state component displays a message and a control to clear filters (which resets related URL parameters and reloads data).
Clicking any resource row or its explicit details link triggers navigation to resource_detail.html?id=<ResourceItem.id> using standard link navigation.
page_functionality.state_logic: The page’s primary state is entirely represented by URL query parameters: resource_type maps to a filter on ResourceItem.resourceType, topic maps to ResourceItem.topic, level maps to ResourceItem.level, publishedAfter maps to a minimum bound on ResourceItem.publishedDate, sortBy maps to one of the values returned in getResourceFilterOptions.sortOptions, q is passed through in the filters object to getResources as a free-text search term, and page controls pagination. On page load or whenever the URL changes, the controller parses these parameters. For each filter parameter, if its value matches an available option from getResourceFilterOptions, it is applied to both the UI control state and the filters parameter for getResources; if not, it is ignored. The page parameter is parsed as an integer; invalid or missing values default logically to the first page. A pageSize value (defined outside this specification) is used to compute limit and offset where limit = pageSize and offset = (page - 1) * pageSize. Whenever the user changes a filter, sort, or search term, the corresponding URL parameter is updated (added or removed); page is reset to 1; then getResources is called again with filters built from the updated URL. Pagination updates only the page parameter, preserving resource_type, topic, level, publishedAfter, sortBy, and q. This ensures that reloading the page or sharing the URL reconstructs the same view using the same sequence: read URL -> call getResourceFilterOptions -> apply URL to controls -> call getResources with derived parameters.

components.functionality: Coordinates initial data loading, URL parameter parsing, and subsequent updates to resource listings when URL state changes.
Displays the page title and a brief functional description bound to the page configuration, without additional marketing content.
Provides a text input for free-text search over resources, synchronized with the q URL parameter.
Displays and manages structured filters for resource type, topic, level, and published-after date, mapped to URL parameters and used in getResources filters.
Displays available sort options from getResourceFilterOptions and updates the sortBy URL parameter when the user selects a different sort order.
Optionally groups search, filter reset, and sort controls, and shows the current applied filter summary computed from URL parameters and active filters.
Renders the list of resources returned from getResources, showing key metadata and providing clickable elements to open each resource’s detail page.
Displays a functional message and a control to clear filters when getResources returns an empty array for the current URL state.
Provides next/previous and page selection controls, synchronized with the page URL parameter and mapped to getResources limit and offset.

layout.grid_system: Implied 12‑column responsive grid (typical WordPress/corporate layout) with a full-width container for the hero and a centered content column.
layout.layout_pattern: Keep a linear top-to-bottom structure anchored by a full-width fixed site header (logo left, nav right), followed by a full-bleed hero-style page header for the Resources & Knowledge Center. Below the hero, stack functional controls in a clear sequence: first a prominent, centered search form; then a horizontal toolbar that balances filter and sort controls; followed by the resource list (or empty state) and, finally, centered pagination. Filters should feel like the 'category pill' analog from the mockup, while the resource list plays the role of the article content that lives under a large hero.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: Keep a linear top-to-bottom structure anchored by a full-width fixed site header (logo left, nav right), followed by a full-bleed hero-style page header for the Resources & Knowledge Center. Below the hero, stack functional controls in a clear sequence: first a prominent, centered search form; then a horizontal toolbar that balances filter and sort controls; followed by the resource list (or empty state) and, finally, centered pagination. Filters should feel like the 'category pill' analog from the mockup, while the resource list plays the role of the article content that lives under a large hero.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: Reserve the top 10–15% of the viewport for the global header and 30–40% of the viewport for a full-bleed hero-like page header that introduces the Resources section. The remaining vertical space is for search, filters, and the list. Controls (search, filter, sort, toolbar) sit within a centered content column (about 70–80% of page width, max-width ~1140–1200px) with comfortable vertical spacing (48–64px) between major bands. The resource list occupies the majority of the mid-lower page area in a scrollable column, with pagination and any empty-state messaging centered in the lower portion of that same column.
layout_strategies.content_density: Maintain a moderately spacious density: few elements per band, generous padding (16–24px) inside containers, and clear breathing room between sections. The hero header is visually bold but clean with large typography and minimal surrounding elements. The search and toolbar region is visually tidy, with controls not crammed together; filters and sort are grouped but separated by 16–24px spacing. The resource list uses simple, repeatable rows/cards with consistent vertical rhythm, ensuring easy scanning and a corporate, uncluttered feel.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: Preserve the F/Z hybrid: attention starts at the top-left logo, sweeps across navigation, then drops into the centered page header title in the hero. Within the hero, the flow is top-down from a compact category label (e.g., 'Resources') to a large, multi-line page title and supporting line of copy. Below, the eye moves down to the search at center, then sweeps horizontally across the filter/sort toolbar (filters starting left, sort aligned right), and finally flows vertically down through the list of resources to the pagination at the bottom. High contrast between text and background plus alignment to a centered content column keeps the flow organized despite functional complexity.

layout.overall_description: The Resources & Knowledge Center page sits under a full-width, fixed teal site header with the geotechnical firm’s logo pinned to the left and a horizontal navigation to the right. Directly beneath, a full-bleed hero-style page header spans edge-to-edge with a subtle technical/geological background image. In the center of this hero, a narrow content column (about 70–80% of the page width) is vertically and horizontally centered: a small, pill-shaped label for the section sits at the top, a large multi-line page title appears beneath it, and a one-line description anchors the bottom of this cluster. As you scroll down, the hero gives way to a clean, centered search bar, followed by a horizontal toolbar where filters line up on the left and sorting sits on the right, all within the same centered column. Below this band, the main resource list runs as stacked rows or cards, each aligned to the 12‑column grid and maintaining consistent padding. If there are no results, a calm, centered empty-state message replaces the list. The pagination sits at the very bottom of the content column, centered and with generous spacing above, visually closing the page. The entire layout feels like a corporate marketing hero adapted for a functional library, with strong central alignment, clear hierarchy, and generous whitespace around each block.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The resources-page-controller functions as the structural wrapper for the page content beneath the global site header. It defines a full-width container that respects the fixed header at the top (offset margin-top equal to header height) and houses all subsequent sections in a vertically stacked column. Within this controller, all interior content (page header hero, search form, toolbar, list, empty state, pagination) is constrained to a centered content column of approximately 70–80% of the viewport width, with a max-width around 1140–1200px to mirror a 12-column corporate grid. Each section inside the controller is separated by consistent vertical spacing (48–64px) to maintain the spacious, marketing-style rhythm.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static structural wrapper, spanning full width, scrolling with the page and providing top padding equal to the fixed header height.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The resources-page-header appears immediately below the fixed global header as a full-width, full-bleed hero section with a subtle geotechnical or technical imagery background. The hero height initially approximates 35–45% of the viewport height on desktop, ensuring it dominates the upper fold without overwhelming functionality. Within this hero, a centered content column (aligned directly under the header’s logo/nav centerline) spans about 70–80% of the viewport width. Inside this column, content is vertically and horizontally centered in the hero: at the top, a small, pill-shaped label (e.g., 'Resources & Knowledge') sits at roughly the upper edge of the central cluster; beneath it, a large multi-line page title occupies the majority of the width (up to 8–10 grid columns) with high-contrast text; below that, a single line of supporting copy or brief description sits in smaller type, forming a tight vertical stack with 8–16px spacing between elements. The entire block is padded from the hero edges (at least 32–48px top/bottom, 24–32px side padding within the content column) to protect readability over the image background.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static section that sits beneath a fixed global header; hero background image scales responsively and remains full-bleed while text stays within the centered content column.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The resource-search-form is placed directly below the hero page header, separated by a comfortable vertical gap of about 48px. It is horizontally centered within the main content column and spans roughly 60–70% of that column’s width on desktop, resulting in a visually prominent but not edge-to-edge input. The search input itself is a single, long field with rounded or subtly squared corners, with 16–20px internal padding, and a search icon or button aligned to the right end. Above the input, an optional small label or helper text can be left-aligned with the field, following the column’s left edge. The area above and below the search form has generous whitespace (24–32px padding) to maintain the spacious feel and to clearly separate it from both the hero above and the filter/sort toolbar below. On smaller screens, the search form gracefully expands to use a greater portion of the width (up to nearly 100% of the content column) while maintaining the vertical stacking order.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static element within the flow, always positioned as the first functional control immediately after the hero.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The resource-filter-panel is visually integrated into a horizontal control band beneath the search form, but aligned to the left side of the centered content column. It sits in the same horizontal row as the sort control on larger screens. The panel occupies approximately the left half to two-thirds of the content column, using a series of compact filter chips, dropdowns, or segmented controls that echo the mockup’s category pill style (small, compact, pill- or capsule-shaped elements). Internally, filters are arranged in a left-to-right sequence with 8–16px gaps between them, and the entire panel has around 16px vertical internal padding. Vertically, the filter panel is positioned about 24px below the search form, forming a tidy band at the top of the list area. On smaller screens, the filter panel wraps onto its own row above the sort control, remaining left-aligned within the content column and using multi-line wrapping as needed while preserving comfortable spacing between filter elements.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static; part of a shared toolbar band with the sort control, reflowing responsively between one and two lines depending on screen width.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The resource-sort-control is positioned in the same horizontal band as the filter panel, but right-aligned within the centered content column to form a balanced F-pattern scanning path. On desktop, it occupies roughly the rightmost 20–30% of the content column. The control appears as a concise label plus dropdown (e.g., 'Sort by: Most Recent'), aligned so that its right edge aligns with the right edge of the content column. Vertical alignment is centered relative to the filter panel controls on the left, forming a single, visually cohesive toolbar line. There is a horizontal gap of 16–24px between the last filter control and the sort control to prevent crowding. On smaller screens, when horizontal space is limited, the sort control drops below the filter panel in the visual stack, spanning close to full width but staying right-aligned text-wise to retain a sense of hierarchy and echo the mockup’s right-biased nav pattern.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static; docked within the toolbar band, aligning to the right and reflowing under filters on narrow viewports.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The resource-list-toolbar serves as the framing band that contains both the filter panel and the sort control, visually separating controls from the resource results. It stretches across the full width of the centered content column and sits approximately 24–32px below the search form. The toolbar has a subtle background tint or faint bottom border, with 12–16px vertical padding and 16–24px horizontal padding inside, giving it a lightly boxed, structured feel without heavy framing. Within this band, filters are aligned to the left and sort to the right, following a horizontal flex layout with space-between alignment. The toolbar’s bottom edge sits directly above the resource list, with a moderate gap (16–24px) providing a clear but close relationship between controls and results, mirroring the tight yet readable meta-row under the hero text in the mockup.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static horizontal band that anchors filters and sort controls; scrolls with content and stays just above the resource list.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The resource-list occupies the main content area beneath the toolbar, stretching across the full width of the centered content column but respecting internal gutters (e.g., content within each row/card remains within about 10–11 of 12 grid columns to maintain side padding). It begins roughly 16–24px below the toolbar and extends downward as a vertical stack of resource items. Each resource item is designed as a horizontal row or card with 16–24px internal padding, a clear title at the top-left, a brief description beneath, and metadata (such as type, date, tags) aligned in a single row beneath or to the right, analogous to the hero’s meta row but more compact. Vertical spacing of 16–24px separates each item, creating a rhythmic but not crowded list. On larger screens, items may optionally align thumbnail or type icons on the left with text content on the right, following the 12-column grid (e.g., 3 columns for a small visual, 9 for text). On smaller screens, each item collapses into a single-column stack while maintaining the same vertical relationships. The list area forms the central, scrollable portion of the page and visually dominates the mid-to-lower viewport once the user scrolls past the hero.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, scrollable content region; height is determined by the number of resources, and it pushes pagination downward accordingly.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The resource-empty-state occupies the same spatial area as the resource list and is centered within the content column when no results are found. It appears approximately 48–64px below the toolbar to visually indicate that the system has responded to filters/search but found nothing. The empty-state content (icon/illustration, headline, and supporting text) is stacked vertically and center-aligned: a simple line icon or abstract geological graphic sits at the top center, followed by a short, clear headline, then a brief explanatory sentence and a call-to-action (e.g., 'Clear filters') below. The entire empty-state block is constrained to about 50–60% of the content column width to avoid stretching text too wide and is vertically padded (24–32px inside a subtle box, or just generous top/bottom margin) to maintain the calm, corporate aesthetic. Pagination typically hides while this state is active, ensuring the empty-state is the clear focal point of the result region.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Conditionally rendered; replaces the resource list content area and centers itself within the same horizontal column when no results match the current controls.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The resource-pagination sits at the bottom of the main content column, directly under the resource list (or, when applicable, completely omitted under the empty state). It is horizontally centered within the content column and typically begins about 32–48px below the last resource item, giving a clear visual separation between content and navigation controls. The pagination controls (previous/next arrows and page numbers or a 'Load more' button) form a compact horizontal cluster, with 8–12px spacing between buttons or page pills. The overall pagination block has 16–24px vertical padding around it to prevent it from feeling cramped against the page’s lower edge. This element visually caps the page content, leveraging the same central alignment as the hero’s headline, creating a vertically symmetric feel to the whole layout.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static element at the bottom of the content flow; it scrolls naturally with the page and remains anchored below the list content.

name: Resource Detail
title: Resource Detail
description: Displays a single resource item with metadata, preview information, PDF download controls, and links to related resources, based on a resource ID provided in the URL.

core_features: Load and display the details of a single resource item using the 'getResourceDetail' interface, based on the 'id' URL parameter.
Show resource metadata including title, summary, publication date, level, topic, resource type, checklist status, file size, and download count.
Provide a preview section so users can assess resource relevance before downloading (using available summary and metadata fields).
Allow users to download the resource PDF via a 'Download PDF' control that uses the 'downloadResourcePdf' interface and handles success/error states.
Display a list of related resources returned from 'getResourceDetail' and allow navigation to their detail pages via URL parameter updates.
Provide navigation back to a topic-specific resource listing by linking to the resources listing page with the topic as a URL parameter.
user_workflows:
View a specific resource:
1. User arrives at resource_detail.html with an 'id' URL parameter (e.g., from resources.html or another page).
2. The page parses the 'id' from the URL.
3. The page calls getResourceDetail(resourceId = id).
4. While loading, the page shows a loading state instead of the resource content.
5. On success, the page displays the resource title, summary, metadata, preview section, and related resources list.
6. If getResourceDetail fails or returns no resource, the page shows an error state and does not show download or related items.

Download the resource PDF:
1. User reviews the resource details and decides to download.
2. User clicks the 'Download PDF' control.
3. The page calls downloadResourcePdf(resourceId = current resource.id).
4. While waiting for the response, the download control shows a pending/disabled state to prevent duplicate clicks.
5. If the call succeeds and returns success = true, the client uses the returned pdfUrl to initiate the browser download and may update displayed file size using fileSizeMb.
6. If the call fails or success = false, the control shows an error message using the returned message and re-enables the download button.

Navigate to a related resource:
1. User scrolls to the related resources section.
2. User clicks on a related resource item.
3. The browser navigates to resource_detail.html?id={relatedResourceId} (via normal link navigation) or updates the URL accordingly.
4. The page lifecycle repeats: the new 'id' is parsed, getResourceDetail is called again, and the content updates for the new resource.

Return to topic-specific resources listing:
1. After viewing the resource, the user wants to see other resources on the same topic.
2. User clicks the 'Back to [topic] resources' link.
3. The browser navigates to resources.html?topic={resource.topic}, using the topic from the currently loaded resource.
4. The resources listing page uses its own logic (outside this page) to filter by the topic parameter.
interactions:
Page load / ID change:
- Trigger: Initial page load or navigation that changes the 'id' URL parameter.
- Behavior: The page parses 'id'. If present and non-empty, it calls getResourceDetail(id). If missing or invalid, it displays an error state and does not call the interfaces.

Download control click:
- Trigger: User clicks the 'Download PDF' button or link.
- Behavior: If the resource is loaded and has a valid id, the page calls downloadResourcePdf(resourceId). The control enters a loading/disabled state until the call resolves. On success, the client initiates the file download using pdfUrl. On failure, an error message is displayed and the control is re-enabled.

Related resource item click:
- Trigger: User clicks any related resource item in the related resources component.
- Behavior: The click navigates to resource_detail.html?id={relatedResource.id}. The entire page reloads or the router updates the URL, which triggers a new getResourceDetail call for that id.

Back-to-topic link click:
- Trigger: User clicks the 'Back to [topic] resources' link.
- Behavior: The click navigates to resources.html?topic={resource.topic}. The link is only rendered once a resource is successfully loaded and its topic is available.

Error and retry (optional interaction):
- Trigger: getResourceDetail or downloadResourcePdf call fails.
- Behavior: The corresponding component shows an error message. For getResourceDetail, a 'Retry' control (button/link) may be shown, which re-calls getResourceDetail with the current id when clicked; for downloadResourcePdf, the user may simply click the download control again after the error is shown.
page_functionality.state_logic: The primary state driver is the 'id' URL parameter (resource_detail.html?id={resourceId}). On initial load, the page reads 'id'. If 'id' is not present or is empty, the page enters a 'missing-id' error state and does not invoke getResourceDetail. If 'id' is present, the page enters a 'loading' state and calls getResourceDetail(resourceId = id). When getResourceDetail resolves successfully, the page transitions to a 'loaded' state with in-memory state containing 'resource' and 'relatedResources'. All dependent components (header, metadata, preview, download control, related list, back-to-topic link) read from this in-memory state. If getResourceDetail fails (network or server error, or no resource found), the page transitions to an 'error' state and hides content sections that require a valid resource. Navigating to another resource via related resources changes the 'id' in the URL; the page reacts by discarding the current in-memory resource state, returning to 'loading', and re-fetching with the new id. The downloadResourcePdf flow does not change the URL; it uses the currently loaded 'resource.id' and manages its own ephemeral 'idle' → 'downloading' → 'download-success' / 'download-error' sub-states inside the download control component. No state is persisted in localStorage or other client storage; all state is URL-driven and maintained in memory only for the lifetime of the page.

components.functionality: Parses the 'id' URL parameter, calls getResourceDetail with that ID, manages loading and error states, and provides the loaded 'resource' and 'relatedResources' to child components.
event_handlers: onInit: Read 'id' from URL; if present, call getResourceDetail(resourceId = id); else set missing-id error state.
onIdChange: When the 'id' URL parameter changes (via navigation), clear existing resource state, enter loading state, and call getResourceDetail(resourceId = newId).
onGetResourceDetailSuccess: Store returned resource and relatedResources in memory and expose them to child components; clear error state.
onGetResourceDetailError: Store error information, clear resource and relatedResources, and expose error state to child components.
onRetryClick: Re-invoke getResourceDetail(resourceId = currentId) when the user triggers a retry from an error view.

components.functionality: Displays the resource title and top-level summary once the resource data is loaded.
event_handlers: onResourceChange: When the bound resource object updates from the loader, re-render the title and summary.

components.functionality: Shows key metadata for the resource, such as publication date, level, topic, resource type, checklist flag, file size, and download count, to help users assess relevance and size before downloading.
event_handlers: onResourceChange: When the resource object updates, re-render metadata values.
onFormatMetadata: Internal handler to format values (e.g., turning publishedDate into display text) without introducing static domain content.

components.functionality: Provides a preview of the resource so users can confirm its relevance before downloading, using the available summary and metadata fields.
event_handlers: onResourceChange: When the resource updates, update the preview text and any derived labels based on the resource fields.

components.functionality: Renders a 'Download PDF' control that initiates the PDF download via downloadResourcePdf using the current resource ID, manages loading/disabled states, and responds to success or error.
event_handlers: onClick: If a valid resource.id is available and the control is not in a loading state, call downloadResourcePdf(resourceId = resource.id) and set internal state to 'downloading'.

onDownloadSuccess: When downloadResourcePdf returns success = true, use pdfUrl to initiate the browser download and update any displayed file size from fileSizeMb; transition state to 'idle' or 'download-success'.
onDownloadError: When downloadResourcePdf fails or returns success = false, display the returned message as an error indicator and reset state to 'idle' so the user can retry.
onResourceChange: If the resource.id changes (e.g., navigation to a new resource), reset any previous download success/error indicators for a clean state.

components.functionality: Provides navigation back to the resources listing page filtered by the current resource's topic.
event_handlers: onResourceChange: When the resource updates, compute the link href as resources.html?topic={resource.topic}. If topic is not available, hide or disable the link.
onClick: Navigate to resources.html?topic={resource.topic} using normal browser navigation.

components.functionality: Displays a list of related resources provided by getResourceDetail and allows users to navigate to another resource detail by clicking an item.
event_handlers: onRelatedResourcesChange: When the relatedResources array updates, re-render the list of items, each with title and key metadata as needed.

onRelatedItemClick: For a clicked related resource, navigate to resource_detail.html?id={relatedResource.id}, which triggers a full page reload or router navigation and causes the 'resource-detail-loader' component to fetch the new resource.

components.functionality: Shows error messages for missing 'id' or failed getResourceDetail calls and optionally provides a retry action.
event_handlers: onMissingId: When no 'id' is present in the URL, display a message indicating that the resource could not be identified.
onGetResourceDetailError: When an error occurs while fetching the resource, display a generic error message and a 'Retry' control.
onRetryClick: Invoke the 'onRetryClick' of the 'resource-detail-loader' component to re-run getResourceDetail with the current 'id'.

layout.grid_system: Implied 12‑column responsive grid (typical WordPress/corporate layout) with a full-width container for the hero and a centered content column.
layout.layout_pattern: The page follows a linear, top-to-bottom flow anchored by a full-width fixed global header (logo left, nav right), followed by a full-bleed hero that serves as the resource header. Within the hero, the resource category, title, and key meta information are stacked and center-aligned in a single, vertical content column. Below the hero, the page transitions into a centered content area that uses a 12-column grid: a two-column layout where the left 7–8 columns host the resource preview and detailed metadata stacked vertically, and the right 4–5 columns host the primary download control at the top and a related resources list below. A back-to-topic link sits just above this main content block, aligned to the left of the content column.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page follows a linear, top-to-bottom flow anchored by a full-width fixed global header (logo left, nav right), followed by a full-bleed hero that serves as the resource header. Within the hero, the resource category, title, and key meta information are stacked and center-aligned in a single, vertical content column. Below the hero, the page transitions into a centered content area that uses a 12-column grid: a two-column layout where the left 7–8 columns host the resource preview and detailed metadata stacked vertically, and the right 4–5 columns host the primary download control at the top and a related resources list below. A back-to-topic link sits just above this main content block, aligned to the left of the content column.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The fixed global header occupies roughly 10–15% of the viewport height. Directly beneath, the hero (resource header) fills most of the remaining initial viewport, with a minimum height of 70–80% of the viewport to create a dominant first impression. Hero text is vertically centered within the middle 40–60% of the hero height and horizontally constrained to about 60% of the page width, centered. Below the hero, the main content area sits within a max-width centered container (~1000–1200px) with generous top margin (48–64px) from the hero. Within this container, the left column takes about two-thirds of the width and the right column one-third. Vertical spacing between stacked sections (preview, metadata, related resources) follows the 48–64px range, while internal padding within cards and blocks uses 16–24px.
layout_strategies.content_density: The hero remains visually sparse but impactful: a small category pill, a large multi-line title, and a single horizontal row of meta information over a visually rich geotechnical background image. Below the hero, density increases slightly to convey more detailed information (preview, metadata, related resources) but preserves ample whitespace around and between components, using clear card-like groupings with 16–24px internal padding and 32–48px gaps. Navigation and action elements are not crowded; each has breathing room to maintain a corporate, high-legibility look.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The eye begins at the global logo in the top-left, sweeps across the header navigation, then drops into the center of the hero where it encounters the category pill, then the large title, and finally the metadata row. From there, the natural downward flow leads to the left-aligned back-to-topic link, then into the main content: first the resource preview at the upper-left of the content area, then the prominent download control at the upper-right. As the user scrolls, the left column drives deeper engagement with the preview and metadata, while the right column offers quick access to downloading and scanning related resources. The layout loosely follows an F/Z pattern: top horizontal across the header and hero, then a vertical scan down the main left column with side glances to the right-hand sidebar.

layout.overall_description: The page presents as a polished corporate resource detail view under a fixed, full-width site header. Immediately below the header, a full-bleed hero with a geotechnical-inspired background image fills most of the first screen. In the middle of this hero, a semi-transparent, centered text column floats over the image: a compact category pill at the top, a bold multi-line resource title beneath, and a single-line row of meta details (author avatar, publication date, and comment or view count) arranged horizontally beneath the title. The hero is visually dominant but minimal in content, with wide margins left and right so text never reaches the screen edges. As you scroll below the hero, the layout tightens into a centered content container on a clean, light background, with a subtle back-to-topic link aligned left above the main content. Inside the container, a two-column grid appears: the left, wider column starts with a spacious resource preview panel and continues with a metadata panel below, while the right, narrower column begins with a visually prominent download card and continues with a related resources list. Generous vertical spacing separates each block, maintaining a calm, professional rhythm. When loading or error states occur, the primary resource content area is replaced or overlaid by a simple, centered feedback panel, but the overall structure of header, hero space, and centered content column remains consistent with the modern marketing aesthetic.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The loader appears as an overlay on top of the hero and main content region, starting directly below the fixed global header and extending down to the bottom of the viewport. It spans the full width of the page and uses a subtle, semi-transparent dark or light scrim so the underlying hero background or layout is hinted at but visually subdued. At the center of this overlay, both vertically and horizontally, a compact loading indicator (spinner or progress ring) is displayed with a short loading label beneath it. The loader respects the hero’s central focus by occupying roughly a 200–240px square area in the middle of the screen, surrounded by generous empty space (48–64px or more on all sides). As soon as resource data is available, the loader disappears entirely, revealing the hero and the rest of the content beneath it.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Overlay positioned fixed relative to the viewport, covering the content area beneath the global header while active; removed from the DOM or hidden once loading completes.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The resource header occupies the hero section directly below the fixed global site header. It is full-width and full-bleed, with a geotechnical engineering themed background image stretching edge-to-edge horizontally and reaching a minimum of 70–80% of the viewport height. Over this image, a centered content column sits within a maximum width of about 60% of the page (roughly 7–8 of 12 columns), horizontally centered. This text block is vertically centered within the hero, so it appears in the middle band of the screen rather than hugging the top or bottom. At the top of the text block is a small, pill-shaped category label (e.g., “Technical Resource” or topic name) with about 8–12px vertical and 16–24px horizontal padding and a subtle background using the brand teal or an accent color. Below the pill, with 16–24px of vertical spacing, the main resource title appears in large, bold, high-contrast type covering two to three lines, occupying roughly 60–70% of the width of the text column and centered. Another 16–24px below the title, a single horizontal row of metadata is displayed: a small circular avatar icon on the left, followed by the author name, publication date, and comment or view count spaced out in a single line, all center-aligned as a group. A translucent dark overlay behind the text (covering the central 40–50% of the hero’s height) helps maintain legibility against the busy background image. Generous top and bottom padding (48–64px) within the hero ensures the text never feels cramped.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static section anchored immediately below the fixed global header, occupying the initial viewport and pushing subsequent content downward.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The resource metadata panel is positioned in the main content area just below the hero, inside the left-hand column of a centered two-column grid. After the user scrolls beyond the hero, they encounter a max-width content container with a back-to-topic link at its top. Beneath that, the grid begins: in the left column, the resource preview sits first, and the metadata panel is placed directly below it with a vertical gap of about 32–48px. The metadata panel itself spans roughly two-thirds of the overall content width (about 7–8 of 12 columns) and reads as a card: a light, solid background, with 24px internal padding and slightly rounded corners. It extends horizontally from the left margin of the content container inward, leaving comfortable white space (at least 32px) between it and the right sidebar column. Within this panel, high-level metadata fields (e.g., project type, location, discipline, keywords, document length) are organized into 2–3 columns on desktop, with each label/value pair in stacked rows and 16–24px vertical spacing between rows. Section headings within the panel (e.g., “Resource Details”, “Technical Specs”) sit at the top of their groups with 16px spacing above and below, reinforcing a tidy, scannable structure that echoes the orderly nature of the hero’s content.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static element within the left column of the main content grid, growing vertically as metadata content requires, always maintaining its position below the preview section.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The resource preview section is the first piece of content in the main area beneath the hero and back-to-topic link. It sits in the left column of a centered, max-width container that begins roughly 48–64px below the bottom edge of the hero. This left column occupies about two-thirds of the container width (around 7–8 of the 12 layout columns), starting flush with the container’s left margin. The preview is presented as a prominent card with a subtle shadow or border and 24–32px of internal padding. It has a fixed visual height on initial view (e.g., 400–600px) to showcase either a PDF thumbnail, embedded viewer, or key page image, with the content centered horizontally and vertically within that area. A concise label such as “Preview” is placed at the top-left inside the card, with 8–16px spacing from the top edge, and the preview content (thumbnail or viewer) sits beneath with 24px of vertical separation. The card maintains at least 32px of clear space to its right, separating it from the sidebar column, and about 32–48px of whitespace below, where the metadata panel begins. This arrangement mirrors the hero’s central dominance by making the preview the primary focal object of the main content area.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block at the top of the left column, anchoring the main content; its height may be adjustable based on viewport but remains visually prominent on first scroll.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The download PDF control lives in the right-hand sidebar column of the main content container, aligned horizontally with the top of the resource preview section. When the user scrolls below the hero into the main content area, the download control appears in the upper-right quadrant of the screen. The sidebar column spans approximately one-third of the container width (about 4–5 of 12 columns) and is separated from the left column by at least 32px of gutter space. The download control is styled as a compact, high-contrast card with 16–24px padding and a clear, full-width primary button inside. At the top of the card, a short label such as “Download PDF” or a brief summary (file size, pages) appears, with 8–16px of spacing below it. The main button spans nearly the full width of the card, with 12–16px vertical padding, using the brand’s primary teal color and white text, making it immediately visible in peripheral vision when viewing the preview. Below the button, optional secondary info (e.g., ‘Opens in new tab’, ‘Requires registration’) is shown in smaller text with 8–12px spacing. The card is positioned so its top edge roughly aligns with the top edge of the preview card, reinforcing a clean horizontal rhythm.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Positioned in the right column of the main grid; on taller viewports it may remain in view by using a sticky behavior, remaining fixed to the top of the viewport within the sidebar column as the user scrolls the longer preview and metadata content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The back-to-topic link is placed just below the hero and above the main two-column content area, acting as a subtle navigational anchor. It sits inside the same max-width content container that holds the preview and sidebar, aligned to the left edge of that container. Vertically, it appears about 48–64px below the bottom edge of the hero, giving a clear visual break between the large hero and the detailed content area. The link is relatively small and low-key—presented as a simple text link with a left-pointing chevron icon—so it does not compete with the hero or primary actions. It occupies only a single line and spans no more than 20–25% of the container width, with ample white space (24–32px) above the next element (the top of the preview and sidebar grid). Its position at the top left of the content container fits the F-pattern reading flow and makes it easy for users to step back to a listing or topic overview without overwhelming the main content.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, inline navigational element anchored at the top of the main content container, scrolling away naturally with the page content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The related resources list is located within the same right-hand sidebar column as the download control, positioned directly below it. A vertical gap of about 32–48px separates the bottom of the download card from the top of the related resources block, giving each function a clear area. The related list spans the full width of the sidebar column (about one-third of the overall content container) and is presented as a vertically stacked list within a light, card-like block with 16–24px internal padding. A small section heading such as “Related Resources” or “You May Also Like” appears at the top, left-aligned, with 16px spacing beneath. Each related item is displayed as a concise row: resource title on one or two lines, optionally with a tiny category tag or date beneath, and 16–24px vertical spacing between items. The overall list height is visually balanced with the left column content; if there are many items, only the top few (3–5) show here to maintain a clean, scanning-friendly sidebar. On scroll, the related resources move with the sidebar; if the download control is sticky, the related list sits just beneath it and scrolls normally once it reaches the top of the viewport.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the right sidebar column, positioned under the download control; scrolls with the page content and adapts to available vertical space.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The error state occupies the same visual territory as the hero and main content when a resource fails to load or is not found. Instead of showing the hero, preview, and metadata, a single, centered error panel is displayed starting just below the fixed global header. The panel is contained within a max-width centered column (about 6–8 of 12 columns) on a neutral background that may either replace the hero background entirely or sit over a lightly dimmed version of it, depending on implementation. Vertically, the error content is centered within the viewport’s remaining height so it appears roughly in the middle of the screen. The main error message (e.g., “Resource Not Found”) is set in a larger, bold heading style, with 16–24px spacing below it, followed by a short explanatory text block and one or two recommended actions (such as a button to go back to the resource list or home). Ample whitespace surrounds this message (at least 48–64px padding within the block and generous margins to neighboring elements), keeping the layout calm and in line with the corporate aesthetic even when something has gone wrong.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Conditionally replaces the hero and main content region; positioned statically under the global header and centered vertically within the available viewport height.

name: Book a Consultation
title: Book a Consultation
description: Page for configuring and scheduling a geotechnical consultation by selecting meeting type, consultant specialty, date, time slot, and providing contact and project details.

core_features: Load available meeting types, specialties, and project stages from getConsultationFormOptions and initialize the selection form, optionally prefilled from URL parameters.
Allow users to select or change meeting type, specialty, and project stage, and keep these selections reflected in the URL parameters for sharable state.
Provide a date selector that can be initialized from the URL parameter `date` and triggers retrieval of available consultation slots via getConsultationAvailability.
Display available consultation time slots for the selected date, meeting type, and specialty using ConsultationAvailabilitySlot data and allow the user to select one slot.
Capture user details (name, email) and optional project name along with the selected project stage and time slot.
Submit a booking using bookConsultation with the selected options and show a confirmation panel with the returned ConsultationBooking details.
Handle error states from getConsultationFormOptions, getConsultationAvailability, and bookConsultation with clear, functional error messaging and retry options.
user_workflows:
Workflow 1: Direct navigation with no pre-selected parameters
1. User navigates to consultation.html without URL parameters.
2. On page load, getConsultationFormOptions is called.
3. Page initializes meeting type, specialty, and project stage using the defaults returned by getConsultationFormOptions (or empty selections if no defaults are provided).
4. Date picker initializes to today’s date (or a neutral initial date determined by the implementation, not hardcoded in the design).
5. When both a meeting type and specialty are selected, the page calls getConsultationAvailability with the selected meetingType, specialty, and date.
6. Available time slots are displayed; the user selects one slot.
7. User fills in name, email, and optional project name and submits the form.
8. bookConsultation is called with meetingType, specialty, projectStage, availabilitySlotId, name, email, and projectName.
9. On success, the page shows a confirmation summary of the booking and updates the URL with a bookingId parameter for reference.

Workflow 2: Navigation from a service page with pre-selected meeting type and specialty
1. User clicks a 'Book a Consultation' link from a service page that includes URL parameters such as consultation.html?meetingType=...&specialty=....
2. On page load, the URL parameters meetingType and specialty are read.
3. getConsultationFormOptions is called and returns lists of meetingTypes, specialties, and projectStages.
4. If the URL-provided meetingType and specialty match entries from getConsultationFormOptions, those values are pre-selected in the configuration form; otherwise, defaults from the API are used.
5. The projectStage parameter, if present, is handled similarly: if it matches an option from projectStages, it is pre-selected.
6. The date picker is initialized from the `date` URL parameter if present and valid; otherwise, it falls back to an implementation-defined default.
7. With meetingType, specialty, and date determined, getConsultationAvailability is called and available slots are shown.
8. User selects a time slot, completes contact details, and submits the form to bookConsultation.
9. On success, the booking confirmation is displayed, and URL parameters are updated to include bookingId and optionally status=confirmed.

Workflow 3: User adjusts options after seeing no availability
1. User arrives with certain meetingType, specialty, and date already selected (via URL or prior choices).
2. getConsultationAvailability returns an empty slots array for that combination.
3. The availability component displays a functional notice that no time slots are available and prompts the user to change the date, meeting type, or specialty.
4. User changes the date using the date picker; the date parameter in the URL is updated.
5. The change triggers a new call to getConsultationAvailability with the updated date and existing meetingType and specialty.
6. If new slots are available, they are displayed for selection; the rest of the workflow continues as in Workflow 1 from the time-slot selection step.

Workflow 4: User reloads or shares a confirmation URL
1. After a successful booking, the application appends bookingId and status=confirmed to the URL (e.g., consultation.html?meetingType=...&specialty=...&date=...&bookingId=...&status=confirmed).
2. When the page is reloaded or accessed by another user via this URL, the page reads the parameters.
3. The configuration form and date picker are initialized from meetingType, specialty, projectStage, and date exactly as in previous workflows.
4. Since the booking state is already known from URL parameters (status=confirmed and bookingId present), the confirmation component can display an informational message that a booking exists, using the booking data retained in current page state (if still available) or a minimal generic message referencing the bookingId.
5. The user can either keep the existing booking (no further action) or change selections and create a new booking by following the standard workflow.
interactions: User selects a meeting type in a dropdown or equivalent selector; this updates the internal selectedMeetingType state, syncs the value to the meetingType URL parameter, and triggers a refresh of availability (if date and specialty are already selected).
User selects a consultant specialty; this updates selectedSpecialty, syncs the value to the specialty URL parameter, and triggers getConsultationAvailability if meetingType and date are set.
User selects a project stage; this updates selectedProjectStage and syncs the value to the projectStage URL parameter but does not trigger availability changes.
User changes the date from the date picker; this updates selectedDate, syncs to the date URL parameter, and triggers getConsultationAvailability with the current meetingType and specialty.
User clicks on an available time slot; the slot’s id is stored as selectedAvailabilitySlotId, and any previously selected slot is deselected.
User fills in text inputs for name, email, and projectName; each keystroke updates the corresponding field in the consultation details form state.

User clicks the 'Confirm Booking' (or similar) button; the form validates required fields and, if valid, calls bookConsultation with meetingType, specialty, projectStage, availabilitySlotId, name, email, and projectName.
If bookConsultation returns success=false, the page displays the provided message and may allow the user to retry submitting or reselect a slot.
If bookConsultation returns success=true, the page displays a confirmation view of the booking details and updates the URL to include bookingId and a confirmed status indicator.

User can change selections after a booking has been made; changing meetingType, specialty, date, or selected slot clears any transient confirmation display and prepares a new booking while leaving the prior bookingId in the URL only if desired by implementation.
page_functionality.state_logic: On initial load, the page reads URL parameters meetingType, specialty, projectStage, date, bookingId, and status (if present). The page then calls getConsultationFormOptions to fetch valid option sets and defaults. For each parameter, the page attempts to match the provided value to a returned option value; if matched, that value becomes the initial selection; if not matched or not provided, the page uses the defaultMeetingType and defaultProjectStage values from getConsultationFormOptions for meetingType and projectStage respectively, and an implementation-defined default for specialty and date. Whenever the user changes meetingType, specialty, projectStage, or date, the corresponding URL parameter is updated to keep the URL in sync with the current state; this allows bookmarking or sharing the page state. After meetingType, specialty, and date are all defined, getConsultationAvailability is called with these values. The returned timeZone and slots are stored in page state to render the availability list. Selecting a slot stores its id (availabilitySlotId) in state but does not modify URL parameters directly unless desired by the implementation. When the user submits the booking form, bookConsultation is invoked with the current state values. On a successful response, the booking object is stored in page state for display, and the URL is updated to include bookingId=booking.id and status=booking.status, without removing the existing configuration parameters. On subsequent reloads, the presence of bookingId and status informs the page to render any confirmation messaging in addition to the normal configuration and availability UI, but availability and configuration are still recomputed based on the other URL parameters and current API responses.

components.functionality: Fetches meeting type, specialty, and project stage options from getConsultationFormOptions on page initialization and provides them to dependent components.
Allows the user to select meeting type, specialty, and project stage using dynamic options from getConsultationFormOptions and initializes selections from URL parameters when possible.

Provides a date selection control for the consultation and keeps the selected date synchronized with the URL parameter `date`. Once a date is selected (and meetingType and specialty are known), it triggers a refresh of available time slots using getConsultationAvailability.

Displays the list of available time slots returned from getConsultationAvailability for the selected date, meeting type, and specialty. Handles slot selection and communicates the selected availabilitySlotId to the booking form.
Captures the user's name, email, and optional project name, along with the selected project stage and availabilitySlotId, and submits a booking via bookConsultation when the user confirms.

Displays the outcome of the booking request, including key fields from the ConsultationBooking returned by bookConsultation (such as date, time range, meeting type, specialty, project stage, name, email, projectName, and status). It may also read bookingId and status from URL parameters to conditionally show confirmation messaging on reload.

Synchronizes user selections (meetingType, specialty, projectStage, date, and optionally bookingId and status) with the URL query parameters whenever they change, and initializes component state from URL parameters on load. Ensures that configuration state is restorable on reload or via shared URLs.

Centralizes loading and error state handling for getConsultationFormOptions, getConsultationAvailability, and bookConsultation calls, enabling dependent components to display functional messages and retry actions without embedding API-specific logic.
Shows a live summary of the currently selected meeting type, specialty, project stage, date, and selected time slot so the user can review their configuration before confirming the booking.

layout.grid_system: Implied 12‑column responsive grid (typical WordPress/corporate layout) with a full-width container for the hero and a centered content column.
layout.layout_pattern: The page follows the mockup’s linear, top‑to‑bottom structure: a fixed-height full-width header with logo left and navigation right, a full-bleed hero band directly beneath it with centered booking messaging, and then a vertically stacked main booking area. Within the main booking area, content is organized into a two-column 12‑column grid: the primary booking steps (configuration, date & time, details) form a left content column, while a live consultation summary occupies a narrower right sidebar. The functional components appear in a logical sequence down the left column, with system/state components layered invisibly over or above this flow as needed (loaders, errors, URL sync).

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page follows the mockup’s linear, top‑to‑bottom structure: a fixed-height full-width header with logo left and navigation right, a full-bleed hero band directly beneath it with centered booking messaging, and then a vertically stacked main booking area. Within the main booking area, content is organized into a two-column 12‑column grid: the primary booking steps (configuration, date & time, details) form a left content column, while a live consultation summary occupies a narrower right sidebar. The functional components appear in a logical sequence down the left column, with system/state components layered invisibly over or above this flow as needed (loaders, errors, URL sync).
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The header occupies approximately 10–12% of the viewport height with a solid brand-colored background.
