The hero section fills the remaining initial viewport height (roughly 80–90% minus header), with centered text over a full-bleed technical background image. Below the hero, the main booking content rests on a white background with a max-width centered container (about 1,120–1,280px) subdivided into an 8/12 left column and a 4/12 right column on desktop. Vertical gaps between major blocks (each form card, hero-to-form transition, confirmation) are around 48–64px, with internal padding of 16–24px per card, following the 8px base spacing scale.
layout_strategies.content_density: The layout preserves the moderately spacious feel from the mockup: relatively few elements per screen, generous breathing room around text and interactive controls, and clear grouping into card-like sections. Each form panel uses consistent padding and line height, with no more than 3–6 related fields per visible cluster to keep scanning easy. The hero remains visually dominant but minimal in content, and the main booking area balances the density of fields with ample whitespace and clear section headings, avoiding a cramped, transactional look.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The visual flow mirrors the mockup’s F/Z pattern. The eye starts at the logo in the top-left of the fixed header, scans right across the navigation, then drops into the center of the hero where a category pill and large headline about booking a consultation are vertically stacked and center-aligned. From there, the eye moves downward into the main booking container, landing first on any notification/error strip, then the primary configuration form at the top-left of the content area. As the user progresses down the left column (configuration → date picker & availability → details), the right-hand summary sidebar remains in peripheral view, capturing the secondary horizontal scan across the page. After submission, a full-width confirmation panel near the top of the main content area becomes the new focal point, with the hero and summary receding in visual priority.

layout.overall_description: The page presents as a polished corporate booking experience that mirrors a marketing hero above a structured, application-style form. A fixed teal header spans the full width of the screen at the very top, with the consulting firm’s logo anchored on the left and a compact horizontal navigation on the right, centered vertically within the bar. Directly beneath, a full-bleed hero covers nearly the entire first viewport: a technical, geotechnical-themed background image washed with a translucent teal overlay. In the vertical and horizontal center of this hero, a small pill-shaped label introduces the section (e.g., “Consultations”), followed by a bold, multi-line headline like “Book a Geotechnical Engineering Consultation,” and a short one-line descriptor, all center-aligned and set in high-contrast white text. Below the hero, the background shifts to solid white and a wide, centered content container appears, respecting generous left and right margins. At the very top of this container sits any cross-page error or status messages, spanning its full width. Just below that, the main booking experience is divided into two columns: an 8-column left rail where the user steps through configuration, date/time selection, and project details, each in its own lightly elevated white card with 16–24px padding; and a 4-column right sidebar summarizing their selections in a sticky, card-like panel. The form panels are stacked vertically down the left with comfortable 32–48px gaps between them, while the summary on the right aligns to the top of the first form and scrolls within view as the user moves down the page. A subtle loader may occasionally wash over the booking panels during data fetches, and URL/state management happens invisibly in the background. After successful booking, a prominent confirmation panel appears near the top of the main content container, visually bridging the hero and the rest of the content. Overall, the design feels calm, structured, and professional, with the hero establishing brand presence and the main area providing a clear, stepwise booking flow anchored by a persistent summary on the right.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The options loader is a non-intrusive overlay that appears over the main booking content area (the white section directly below the hero) whenever consultation configuration data is being fetched or recalculated. It does not affect the fixed header or the hero. Spatially, it is positioned absolutely within the main centered container, stretching from the top edge of the primary form card to just above the page footer, spanning nearly the full 12-column width of the content container (approximately 80–90% of the viewport width on desktop, constrained by the max container width). The overlay uses a very light translucent white or teal-tinted wash so that underlying forms and the summary sidebar remain faintly visible but clearly inactive. At the center of this overlay—both horizontally and vertically relative to the visible form stack—a simple spinner and short status message appear, aligned on the central vertical axis of the container. On smaller screens, the overlay still covers the entire stacked form content, ensuring that the user understands the booking UI is temporarily busy while retaining the overall spacious feel.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual when idle; when loading, behaves as a full-width, centered overlay anchored to the main booking container, floating above all form and summary panels without affecting their layout.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The configuration form is the first interactive block in the main booking area and sits directly below any global error/status strip and a narrow 48–64px vertical gap beneath the hero. It is positioned in the left portion of the 12-column grid, occupying roughly 8 columns (about two-thirds of the content container width) and aligned flush with the container’s left margin. Visually, it appears as a white card with a subtle shadow or border, featuring internal padding of 16–24px on all sides. A section title (e.g., “Consultation Setup”) sits at the top-left inside the card, with 16–24px spacing below it before the first row of fields. Fields for meeting type, consultant specialty, and other initial configuration options are arranged in 1–2 columns within this card depending on viewport width, but the overall card width remains consistent with the 8-column allocation. There is 24–32px of vertical space between field groups to maintain the mockup’s moderate density. The bottom of the card has slightly more padding (24–32px) to visually separate it from the next section. To its right, there is a clear 24–32px gap before the summary sidebar begins, reinforcing the two-column layout. On tablet and mobile, this form expands to take up 100% of the content width and appears above the summary (which stacks below).
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the document flow, anchored at the top of the left column of the main content container; it scrolls normally with the page.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The date picker is positioned immediately below the configuration form in the left 8-column content area, separated by a vertical gap of approximately 32–48px to keep sections visually distinct. It shares a horizontal band with the availability list on larger screens, forming a cohesive time-selection module. Within the 8-column width, the date picker occupies about 5 of the 8 columns (roughly 60–65% of the left column), aligned to the left edge of that column. It appears as a card with similar styling to the configuration form: white background, subtle elevation, and 16–24px internal padding. At the top-left of the card, a concise section heading like “Select a Date” is aligned to the left, with about 16px spacing before the date grid. The calendar grid itself fills most of the card, with sufficient padding around the edges so days aren’t cramped against the borders. The right-hand side of this same horizontal band is visually reserved for the availability list card, leaving a 16–24px horizontal gap between the date picker and availability list. On smaller viewports (tablet/mobile), the date picker card becomes full width and sits above the availability list card, maintaining the same vertical rhythm of padding and gaps.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static and aligned within the left column; participates in a split horizontal section with the availability list on wider screens, stacking vertically when space is constrained.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The availability list is tightly coupled spatially with the date picker, forming the right side of the time-selection band. On desktop within the 8-column left content area, it occupies the remaining 3 of the 8 columns (roughly 35–40% of the left column width), aligned to the right edge of that column. Its top edge is aligned with the top of the date picker card so the two panels form a unified horizontal strip. A 16–24px horizontal gap separates it from the date picker, keeping consistency with the 8px-based spacing system. The availability list is also styled as a white card with 16–24px internal padding, but slightly taller than its content to give breathing room around the list of time slots. A section label like “Available Time Slots” appears at the top-left inside the card, with 8–16px spacing before the first slot row. Each time slot is listed in a single column, with 8–12px vertical spacing between items to avoid visual clutter. If no times are available, a centered, concise message occupies the middle of this card. On tablet and mobile, this card stacks directly below the date picker card, spanning the full content width, with 24–32px vertical spacing between the two to maintain clear grouping.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, paired horizontally with the date picker on wide screens and stacked underneath it on narrow screens; scrolls with the left-column content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The details form is placed directly below the combined date-picker/availability band in the left 8-column area, separated by a vertical gap of approximately 32–48px. It spans the full width of the left column (all 8 columns) to accommodate more extensive fields such as contact information and project details. The card matches the other panels’ styling: white background, light elevation, and 16–24px padding. A section header like “Project & Contact Details” is aligned to the top-left with 16–24px spacing before any input fields. Fields are arranged in 1–2 columns depending on screen width, but the layout never feels cramped; multi-line inputs (e.g., project description) occupy full-width rows with extra vertical spacing (16–24px) above and below. The bottom of the form includes the primary “Confirm Booking” or similar CTA button aligned to the left or right within the card, with 16–24px padding separating it from the last field. The card’s bottom edge leaves an additional 48–64px margin before any subsequent content or the page footer, preserving the spacious corporate feel. On smaller devices, the card remains full-width and appears after the summary sidebar card, since the sidebar stacks below all left-column content on mobile.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the left column, forming the final main step in the booking flow; scrolls normally and aligns with the grid’s left edge.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The booking confirmation panel appears only after a successful submission and is designed to become a clear new focal point while respecting the established layout structure. When visible, it is inserted at the top of the main content container, just below any error/status component and the bottom edge of the hero, pushing the configuration form and other sections downward. It spans the full 12-column width of the centered content container, visually bridging both the left content column and the right summary sidebar. The panel is styled as a prominent band with either a soft brand-colored background (e.g., a diluted teal) or a slightly elevated white card with a bold teal accent bar along the top edge. Internal padding is generous (24–32px) and typography is larger than in the forms, echoing the hero’s emphasis. A success icon and headline (e.g., “Your Consultation Is Scheduled”) are aligned left within the container, with supporting details (date, time, consultant name) laid out in a multi-column row beneath on larger screens and stacked on smaller devices. There is 24–32px of spacing below this panel before the configuration form, in case the user wants to review or modify their booking. On mobile, it remains the first element beneath the hero and spans the full width of the screen within the content margins.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Appears conditionally and occupies a fixed position at the top of the main content container, pushing other content downward; scrolls with the page once displayed.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The state URL sync component has no direct visual representation but conceptually sits at the page level overseeing the entire booking area. It tracks selections across the configuration form, date picker, availability list, details form, and summary sidebar, and keeps the URL query parameters or hash in sync. There are no visible panels or controls; any impact is perceived only indirectly—for example, when the user refreshes and lands back on the same configuration. Spatially, you can think of it as layered beneath or behind the main content container, affecting behavior but not layout.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Purely behavioral, non-visual; it does not consume layout space and has no fixed or floating behavior, instead observing and updating application state in the background.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The error and loading handler surfaces information in a way that aligns with the mockup’s clear visual flow. When showing global errors or important status messages (e.g., failed to load availability), it renders a full-width notification strip at the very top of the main content container, just below the hero and above the configuration form. This strip spans the entire 12-column width and uses a slightly more saturated version of the brand palette (e.g., soft red for errors) with white or dark contrasting text. Internal vertical padding is around 12–16px, with text left-aligned and possibly a small icon on the left for quick scanning. A 16–24px vertical gap separates this strip from the top of the configuration form so it doesn’t feel cramped. For more localized form errors, it supports inline messages directly under the respective fields, but these messages remain contained within their parent form cards, using smaller text and 4–8px spacing. If a full-page loading state is required (beyond the options loader), this component can temporarily replace the main booking area with a central message within the same content container position. On mobile, the notification strip still appears immediately beneath the hero, full-width within the container margins.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Conditionally appears as an inline, full-width banner anchored to the top of the main content container; otherwise operates in the background managing error and loading states without occupying space.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The summary sidebar is the primary secondary column on the page and sits to the right of the main booking forms within the central content container. On desktop, it occupies approximately 4 of the 12 grid columns (about one-third of the content width), aligned flush to the container’s right margin. Its top edge aligns vertically with the top of the configuration form, creating a clean horizontal baseline across the content area. The sidebar appears as a tall white card with 16–24px internal padding, subtle elevation, and perhaps a thin accent line or colored header to link it visually to the brand and hero. Inside, key booking details (selected meeting type, consultant specialty, date, time, and any estimated fees or notes) are laid out in a vertical stack with clear labels and values, separated by 8–16px spacing. A compact CTA or reassurance note (e.g., “No payment required until confirmation”) can sit at the bottom, with extra padding to give it breathing room. As the user scrolls through the left-column forms, the sidebar remains visible for as long as possible: it behaves as a sticky element that anchors a few dozen pixels below the fixed header, maintaining a 16–24px gap from the header when stuck. There is at least a 24–32px horizontal gap between this sidebar and the left-column forms. On tablet and mobile, the summary sidebar reflows to sit below all main booking steps, spanning the full width of the container with the same card styling and vertical spacing between sections.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Right-column, sticky panel: fixed within the scrolling context of the main content container so that it remains in view as the user scrolls, while respecting top and bottom margins; stacks below the main forms on smaller screens.

name: Tools Overview
title: Tools & Calculators
description: Functional overview page that lists all geotechnical tools and calculators, explains their intended use and limitations based on provided metadata, supports tool highlighting via URL parameters, and links users to each tool’s dedicated page.

core_features: Load and display all available tools and calculators using the getToolsOverview interface.
Optionally highlight and auto-focus a specific tool based on the highlightToolSlug URL parameter.
Provide basic client-side filtering and sorting of tools (e.g., by tool type, featured status, name) using the data returned by getToolsOverview.
Display per-tool details (name, type, description) to communicate typical use cases and limitations, based on the Tool.description field.
Provide navigation from each tool entry to the tool’s dedicated page using the Tool.url field (including the Bearing Capacity Calculator page).
Present a concise advisory section that encourages users to combine tool usage with professional consultation when needed.
user_workflows:
Browse all tools from navigation:
1. User navigates to tools.html from the header or footer.
2. Page initializes and calls getToolsOverview with no parameters.
3. While loading, a loading state is shown in place of the tool list.
4. On success, the full list of Tool records is stored in page state and passed to the listing and filter components.
5. Tools are displayed in a list/grid, initially sorted by Tool.displayOrder (if present) or by Tool.name as a fallback.
6. User can scroll through the list, optionally apply filters/sorting, and expand individual tools to read more about their use and limitations.
7. User clicks a tool’s primary action to navigate to the tool’s dedicated page (using Tool.url).

Open a highlighted tool from another page:
1. User clicks a link on index.html or resources.html that points to tools.html with a highlightToolSlug query parameter (e.g., tools.html?highlightToolSlug=some-slug).
2. Tools Overview page parses highlightToolSlug from the URL during initialization and stores it in state as the requested highlighted slug.
3. Page calls getToolsOverview and waits for the Tool array.
4. After tools are loaded, the page searches for the tool whose Tool.slug matches highlightToolSlug.
5. If a matching tool is found, that tool is marked as selected/highlighted in state.
6. The list component scrolls to the matching tool and visually flags it as highlighted.
7. The detail panel component automatically displays the selected tool’s detailed information (including description-based use cases/limitations).
8. If no tool matches highlightToolSlug, the page simply shows the full list with no selection.

Filter and sort tools:
1. With tools loaded, the user interacts with the filter toolbar (e.g., selects one or more tool types, toggles a "show featured" filter, or enters a search text).
2. The filter toolbar updates filter state in the page root (e.g., selectedToolTypes, searchQuery, showFeaturedOnly, sortBy).
3. The tools list receives the full Tool array plus the filter state and applies filtering and sorting on the client:
   - Filter by Tool.toolType matching selected types if any are selected.
   - Optionally filter to tools where Tool.isFeatured is true if showFeaturedOnly is enabled.
   - Apply case-insensitive text match on Tool.name and/or Tool.description using searchQuery.
   - Sort using the chosen sort field (e.g., displayOrder, name).
4. The filtered, sorted list is rendered.
5. The count of currently visible tools is displayed to the user.
6. Filters do not trigger additional API calls; they only operate on the in-memory result of getToolsOverview.

Open tool details and navigate to the tool page:
1. User identifies a tool in the list and clicks a "Details" control (e.g., button or row action) associated with that tool.
2. The click handler sets the selectedToolSlug in page state and updates the URL’s highlightToolSlug parameter using history.replaceState or history.pushState.
3. The detail panel component receives the selectedToolSlug and the full Tool array, resolves the selected Tool, and renders detailed information (name, type, description).
4. The description field is shown in full so that any use-case and limitation information provided in Tool.description is visible.
5. From within the detail panel, the user can click a primary action (e.g., "Open Tool") that navigates to Tool.url (e.g., bearing_capacity_calculator.html for the bearing capacity tool).
6. Browser transitions to the tool’s dedicated page.

Revisit or share a specific tool highlight:
1. A user who has selected a tool (which updated highlightToolSlug in the URL) copies the browser URL or bookmarks the page.
2. When this URL is revisited or shared, the Tools Overview page is loaded with highlightToolSlug already set.
3. The workflow for "Open a highlighted tool from another page" repeats, auto-selecting and focusing the same tool, providing a shareable deep link into the overview.

Consultation encouragement flow:
1. While reviewing tools or using filters, the user notices a static advisory panel suggesting that design decisions should be validated by a qualified geotechnical engineer.
2. The panel may include one or more navigation actions (e.g., a link/button to a generic consultations or contact page) that are standard navigational links not dependent on dynamic data.
3. User clicks the link, which navigates to the appropriate page (e.g., consultations.html) in the same or a new tab, without additional API interactions from this page.
interactions:
Page load:
- onPageLoad: Parse URL for highlightToolSlug, initialize state, and invoke getToolsOverview().

Data loading:
- onToolsOverviewRequest: Triggered by the data-loader component to call getToolsOverview.
- onToolsOverviewSuccess: Store the returned Tool array in page state; compute derived values (unique tool types present, featured flags, default sort order).
- onToolsOverviewError: Store error state and display a non-visual error message component (e.g., text) instead of the tool list.

Filtering and sorting interactions:
- onToolTypeFilterChange: User selects or deselects tool types (derived from Tool.toolType values present in the data). Updates filter state and re-renders filtered list.
- onSearchQueryChange: User types into a search input; updates searchQuery state (e.g., with debounced input) and re-renders the filtered list.
- onShowFeaturedToggle: User toggles a control to show only tools with Tool.isFeatured = true; update filter state and re-render.
- onSortOptionChange: User changes sort option (e.g., "Recommended" / "Name A–Z"); updates sortBy state and re-renders.

Tool selection & highlighting:
- onToolCardClick (details action): When a user clicks a dedicated "Details" or similar control on a tool card, the page sets selectedToolSlug in state to Tool.slug for that card and updates the URL parameter highlightToolSlug accordingly. The detail panel then renders the selected tool’s information.
- onListRenderWithHighlight: After the list first renders, if highlightToolSlug is set and matches a tool, the list component scrolls that tool into view and visually indicates it as highlighted.

Navigation to tool pages:
- onOpenToolClick: When a user clicks the primary action (e.g., the card itself or an "Open Tool" button), the page navigates the browser to Tool.url using a standard link or a programmatic navigation call. For the bearing capacity tool, Tool.url corresponds to bearing_capacity_calculator.html.

Consultation panel interactions:
- onConsultationLinkClick: User clicks a link or button within the advisory panel; browser navigates to a separate consultations/contact page (e.g., consultations.html) without invoking any additional API call on this page.
page_functionality.state_logic:
The page uses a combination of in-memory state and URL parameters to control behavior, without relying on localStorage.

URL parameters:
- highlightToolSlug (string, optional):
  - On initial page load, the page reads the highlightToolSlug query parameter from the URL (e.g., tools.html?highlightToolSlug=bearing-capacity-calculator).
  - The value is stored in state as requestedHighlightToolSlug.
  - After getToolsOverview returns, the page searches the Tool array for a tool whose Tool.slug equals requestedHighlightToolSlug.
  - If found, that slug is set as selectedToolSlug, which drives:
    - Which tool card is visually highlighted in the list.
    - Which tool is displayed in the detail panel by default.
    - The scroll behavior that brings the selected tool into view.
  - If no matching Tool.slug exists, selectedToolSlug remains null and the list is shown without a selected tool.
  - When a user selects a different tool via the UI (e.g., clicking a "Details" action on a card), the selectedToolSlug is updated, and the URL query parameter highlightToolSlug is also updated to match using history.pushState or history.replaceState. This synchronizes page state with the URL, enabling deep linking and bookmarking.

In-memory state (examples):
- tools: Array of Tool objects returned by getToolsOverview.
- isLoading: Boolean flag to indicate whether getToolsOverview is in progress.
- loadError: Error object or message if getToolsOverview fails.
- selectedToolSlug: The slug of the currently selected/highlighted tool (may be derived from highlightToolSlug or user interaction).
- filters: Object containing current filters, such as:
  - selectedToolTypes: Array of toolType values selected (derived from Tool.toolType values present in tools).
  - searchQuery: String for text search across Tool.name/Tool.description.
  - showFeaturedOnly: Boolean to restrict the list to tools where Tool.isFeatured is true.
- sortBy: Current sort preference (e.g., 'displayOrder', 'name').
- derivedFilteredTools: Computed subset of tools based on filters and sorting; passed down to the list component for rendering.

State transitions:
- Initial: highlightToolSlug parsed, isLoading = true, tools = [].
- After getToolsOverview success: isLoading = false, tools populated, filters initialized (e.g., selectedToolTypes = all or empty), derivedFilteredTools computed.
- After matching highlightToolSlug: selectedToolSlug is set and the list + detail components update accordingly.
- On filter/sort changes: filters or sortBy updated; derivedFilteredTools recomputed; list re-renders with no additional API calls.
- On URL change due to user selection: highlightToolSlug in the URL mirrors selectedToolSlug, enabling direct access to the same state on reload.

components.functionality: Acts as the main controller for the Tools & Calculators page: parses URL parameters, orchestrates data loading via getToolsOverview, maintains global page state (tools, filters, selection), and wires child components together.
Invokes the getToolsOverview interface once on initialization to retrieve the list of Tool records, manages loading and error states, and passes the results to the root container.

Provides client-side filtering and sorting controls for the tool list, such as selecting tool types derived from Tool.toolType values, toggling display of featured tools, and entering a search query. Updates filter and sort state in the root component.

Displays the filtered and sorted collection of tools as a list or grid of tool entries. Highlights the selected tool if selectedToolSlug is set, and exposes actions for viewing details and opening the tool’s dedicated page.

Represents a single tool within the tools list. Shows the Tool.name, Tool.toolType, the first part of Tool.description (or full description if desired), and provides two primary actions: view details (select/highlight this tool in the overview) and open the tool’s dedicated page by navigating to Tool.url.

Displays detailed information for the currently selected tool identified by selectedToolSlug, derived from the Tool array. Shows the full Tool.name, Tool.toolType, and Tool.description so that any use-case and limitation information embedded in the description is clearly communicated. Includes an action to open the tool’s dedicated page (Tool.url).

Synchronizes the selected tool highlight between URL and in-memory state. On load, reads highlightToolSlug from the URL and notifies the root component; when selectedToolSlug changes due to user interaction, updates highlightToolSlug in the URL using the History API without reloading the page.
Shows a loading indicator while getToolsOverview is in progress and an error message when the data request fails. Hides itself when tools data is successfully loaded.

Displays a concise summary above the list (e.g., total number of tools and number of tools currently visible after filtering) using the Tool array and derivedFilteredTools, helping users understand the scope of available tools.

Provides static guidance encouraging users to validate important design decisions with a qualified geotechnical engineer and to use the tools as decision-support aids rather than definitive designs. May include a navigation link to a consultations or contact page.

layout.grid_system: Implied 12‑column responsive grid (typical WordPress/corporate layout) with a full-width container for the hero and a centered content column.
layout.layout_pattern: The page mirrors the mockup’s header–hero–content stack. Below the fixed global header, a full-width tools-summary-header acts as the hero for this page, with centrally aligned text over a technical/geotechnical background. Directly beneath it sits a horizontal tools-filter-toolbar spanning the main content column. Under the toolbar, the layout shifts into a two-column content area on larger screens: the tools-list occupying the left 2/3 of the width and the tool-detail-panel occupying the right 1/3. Supporting systems (data-loader, highlight-state-manager, loading/error indicator) are layered functionally over or within these components, while the consultation-advisory-panel appears as a full-width strip toward the bottom of the page.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page mirrors the mockup’s header–hero–content stack. Below the fixed global header, a full-width tools-summary-header acts as the hero for this page, with centrally aligned text over a technical/geotechnical background. Directly beneath it sits a horizontal tools-filter-toolbar spanning the main content column. Under the toolbar, the layout shifts into a two-column content area on larger screens: the tools-list occupying the left 2/3 of the width and the tool-detail-panel occupying the right 1/3. Supporting systems (data-loader, highlight-state-manager, loading/error indicator) are layered functionally over or within these components, while the consultation-advisory-panel appears as a full-width strip toward the bottom of the page.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The summary header occupies roughly the upper half of the initial viewport on desktop, echoing the mockup’s dominant hero. The header content is vertically centered within this band, with lateral padding consistent with a centered 12-column grid (text confined to about 8 columns). The filter toolbar consumes a compact horizontal band below the hero (about 64–80px tall), and the main content region below is divided ~66% width for the tools-list and ~34% for the tool-detail-panel. Vertical spacing between major sections (hero, toolbar, main content, advisory panel) uses generous 48–64px margins to preserve a spacious, corporate feel based on the 8px spacing scale.
layout_strategies.content_density: Content remains moderately sparse at the top: the summary header uses a large headline, a short description, and a small metadata row, surrounded by ample padding to stand off from the busy background. The filter toolbar is denser but still orderly, with well-spaced controls in a single horizontal row. The tools-list region has a medium density: stacked tool-cards with clear separation (16–24px gaps) and concise descriptions. The tool-detail-panel presents more text (usage and limitations) but within a constrained width and clear hierarchy to prevent visual overload. The consultation-advisory-panel uses minimal but important copy with strong spacing to feel like a distinct, digestible info block rather than clutter.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The visual flow continues the mockup’s F/Z pattern: the eye starts at the global logo and navigation (outside this scope), then drops into the centered category pill and bold title in the tools-summary-header. From there, the gaze moves down to the summary metadata row, then to the filter toolbar immediately below. The flow then zigzags through the left-hand tools-list where highlighted tool-cards stand out, and across to the right-hand tool-detail-panel for deeper information. The loading and error states interrupt this flow only when necessary by centering status feedback, while the consultation-advisory-panel at the bottom provides a clear endpoint and call to consider professional consultation, reinforcing responsible tool usage.

layout.overall_description: Below the site’s fixed teal header, the Tools Overview page opens with a full-width hero-style summary header showing a geotechnical-themed background that spans edge to edge. In the middle of this band, a centered content column holds a small pill label, a large multi-line page title, and a concise description, with a thin row of metadata just beneath. As users scroll, the hero gives way to a slim, horizontally aligned filter toolbar that stretches across the same centered column, housing search and filter controls in a clean row. Below this, the page divides into two columns on desktop: the left two-thirds is a vertical list of tool-cards with comfortable spacing, while the right third is a detail panel that reinforces whichever tool is selected or highlighted from the list. When loading or errors occur, a subdued overlay appears over the main content, centering feedback without obscuring the overall structure. At the bottom of the main content, a full-width advisory panel with a lightly tinted background offers guidance on the tools’ limitations and a clear path to request expert consultation, giving the page a responsible, professional conclusion that matches the corporate engineering aesthetic.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The tools-overview-root is the main page container sitting immediately below the fixed global site header. It spans the full browser width but constrains primary content to a centered max-width grid (approximately 1120–1200px) with 24px side padding on smaller screens. Vertically, it arranges sections in a clean top-to-bottom flow: first the tools-summary-header hero band, then the tools-filter-toolbar, followed by the split layout containing the tools-list and tool-detail-panel, and finally the consultation-advisory-panel near the bottom. Section-to-section spacing uses 48–64px vertical margins to keep each block clearly separated while maintaining continuity. The background is neutral (light grey or white), allowing the hero background image and teal brand accents to carry most of the visual weight.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Acts as a standard, scrollable container; its top starts directly below the fixed global header and it defines the 12-column responsive grid for all inner components.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The tools-data-loader is a non-visual structural component that sits logically at the top of the tools-overview-root’s content stack, just above the visual tools-summary-header. It does not occupy visible layout space when idle, but its state drives the appearance and timing of the tools-loading-and-error-state overlay that covers the hero and main content region. Conceptually, it spans the same horizontal boundaries as the main content column so that any loading or error feedback feels anchored to the page rather than floating arbitrarily.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-rendering in normal operation; controls global loading and error overlays that center within the viewport and dim the underlying layout without shifting other components.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The tools-filter-toolbar is positioned directly beneath the tools-summary-header, separated by about 32px of vertical space to signal a new functional band. It sits within the same centered content column as the hero text, spanning approximately 80–90% of the viewport width up to the page max-width, with 16–24px horizontal padding inside the bar. The toolbar height is compact (around 64–80px), forming a clean horizontal strip that holds search and filtering controls arranged from left to right, with generous 16px spacing between elements. The background is slightly lifted from the page (e.g., a subtle light tint or faint border) so it reads as a control band but does not visually compete with the hero above or the content beneath.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Initially in normal document flow below the hero; on larger screens it can optionally become sticky just beneath the global header when the user scrolls past the summary header, maintaining full width of the content column while keeping filters accessible.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The tools-list occupies the left portion of the main content area beneath the filter toolbar. On desktop and large tablets, it uses roughly 8 of 12 grid columns (around 66% of the content width), aligned to the left edge of the centered content column, with a 32px horizontal gap separating it from the adjacent tool-detail-panel. On mobile, this list expands to full width and stacks above the tool-detail-panel. Vertically, the list is a scrollable stack of tool-cards, each separated by 16–24px of vertical spacing, with the overall top of the list about 32px below the filter toolbar for breathing room. The list forms the primary scanning region, with a relatively consistent rhythm of cards from top to bottom and the first one appearing within the upper part of the viewport on initial scroll.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Participates in normal document flow; scrolls vertically with the page, while the layout around it (notably the tool-detail-panel) may exhibit sticky behavior on larger screens.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Each tool-card fills the full width of the tools-list column, forming a rectangular card with 16–24px internal padding and slightly rounded corners or a subtle border to separate it from the background. At the top-left of the card, a compact pill indicating category or tool type aligns with the hero’s category pill styling, creating continuity with the page’s hero. Below the pill, the tool’s name appears as a bold, multi-line headline spanning most of the card width, followed by a short description paragraph in smaller, high-contrast body text. Along the bottom edge of the card, a single horizontal row presents key metadata (e.g., difficulty, typical use, last updated) and possibly an inline link or icon indicating navigation to the dedicated tool page. Highlighted cards (via the highlight-state-manager) are visually emphasized with a stronger teal border, slight shadow, or subtle glow, and are positioned so that the highlighted card appears within the central region of the viewport when auto-scrolled.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Lives entirely within the vertical flow of the tools-list; its size grows only with content but is kept visually compact. When highlighted, it may animate slightly (e.g., micro-scale or border fade) without affecting the overall list layout.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The tool-detail-panel is positioned to the right of the tools-list on wider screens, starting at the same vertical level as the top of the list. It occupies about 4 of 12 grid columns (roughly one-third of the main content width), with a 32px gap between it and the list, and matches the list’s vertical padding and alignment to feel like part of the same section. Inside the panel, content is vertically stacked: the selected tool’s name as a readable sub-headline, a short descriptive summary, followed by clearly labeled sections for intended use, key assumptions, limitations, and a prominent button or link to view the full tool page. On mobile, this panel drops below the tools-list, expanding to full width with 32–48px vertical separation, effectively becoming a secondary section users reach after scanning the list. The panel uses a subtle elevated treatment (light background, faint shadow) to signal that it is a focused detail area related to the selection in the list.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: On larger screens, the panel is sticky relative to the viewport: as the user scrolls through the list, the panel remains pinned within the right column, staying fully visible from approximately the top navigation boundary down to a comfortable bottom margin. On small screens, it reverts to normal flow, scrolling like any other section after the list.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The highlight-state-manager is a non-visual logic component that coordinates which tool-card and corresponding detail content are emphasized based on URL parameters or user selection. It does not occupy spatial real estate itself, but its effects are visible: the relevant tool-card is brought into view within the central portion of the tools-list column, and the tool-detail-panel is populated or updated to match. Highlighted cards receive enhanced styling (e.g., a thicker teal border, subtle background tint, or left-edge accent bar), making them stand out from the vertically stacked neighbors without altering the overall list spacing or alignment.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-rendering by default; influences layout behavior by triggering smooth scroll positioning of the selected card toward the vertical center of the viewport and synchronizing the sticky detail panel’s content. It does not change the structural flow of any container.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The tools-loading-and-error-state appears as an overlay centered over the main content area when data is being fetched or when an error prevents normal rendering. During full-page loading, a semi-transparent layer extends from just below the global header to the bottom of the viewport, dimming the tools-summary-header, filter toolbar, list, and detail panel while leaving their positions intact. In the center of the viewport, a compact card (about 320–400px wide) displays a spinner and short message for loading, or a clear error icon and brief description with a retry action. For localized issues (e.g., reloading just the list), a slimmer inline status bar appears at the top of the tools-list column, occupying full column width with 16px vertical padding and not shifting major sections above or below.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Temporarily overlays existing content without reflowing it; fixed relative to the viewport for full-page states, and inline at the top of the tools-list for partial states. It appears and disappears dynamically based on data-loader state.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The tools-summary-header functions as the hero section at the top of the page’s content. It spans the full viewport width with a geotechnical or technical background image that extends edge-to-edge, overlaid with a subtle dark or teal gradient to ensure text legibility. On desktop, the hero height occupies roughly 45–60% of the viewport height, with a minimum height around 360–420px; content is vertically centered within this band. Within a centered column (about 8 of 12 grid columns wide), a small pill-shaped label sits at the top of the text stack, followed by a large multi-line headline describing the tools overview, then a brief supporting paragraph. Beneath this, a horizontal metadata row—showing the total number of tools, last updated date, and perhaps a brief note about online vs. offline tools—aligns centrally. The text is center-aligned, echoing the mockup’s hero composition, with ample top and bottom padding (32–48px) to separate the text cluster from the hero’s edges.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Sits at the top of the tools-overview-root in normal flow; scrolls away as the user moves down the page. On smaller screens, the height compresses slightly, retaining the centered text and margins but allowing more vertical space for content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The consultation-advisory-panel is a full-width informational strip located near the bottom of the page, directly following the combined tools-list and tool-detail-panel section with about 48px of vertical separation. It spans the entire viewport width but keeps its textual content within the same centered max-width column as the rest of the page. The panel uses a light teal-tinted background to distinguish it from the main white content areas, with generous vertical padding (32–48px) to feel open and important rather than dense. Inside, content is arranged in a left-to-right row on desktop: a small icon or graphic on the far left, a short but strong headline about seeking expert review in the center, and a prominent CTA button or link on the right. On mobile, these elements stack top-to-bottom, center-aligned, maintaining spacing between each. The panel visually anchors the bottom of the page and reinforces responsible usage of the tools by clearly communicating limitations and offering a clear path to contact the consulting team.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Participates in normal document flow and sits as the concluding section of the page content. It does not stick or float, but its contrasting background and generous spacing make it feel like a distinct, final call-to-action band.

name: Bearing Capacity Calculator
title: Bearing Capacity Calculator
description: Page to configure, run, review, and save shallow foundation bearing capacity calculations, with state controlled via URL parameters.

core_features: Load calculator configuration (soil types, units, methods, default values) via getBearingCapacityCalculatorConfig and initialize the input form.
Support URL-driven preconfiguration using soilType, footingWidth, footingDepth, factorOfSafety, projectLocation, method, and optional calculationId parameters to set initial form and result state.
Provide an input form to select soil type, footing width and unit, footing depth and unit, factor of safety, optional project location, and calculation method, with validation and unit controls.
Execute bearing capacity calculations through calculateBearingCapacity when the user submits the form, and display ultimate and allowable bearing capacities, the method used, and result notes.
Maintain the latest calculationId from calculateBearingCapacity in page state so that it can be used in subsequent save operations.
Enable saving of a previously computed result to My Plans by opening a dialog that collects a projectName and calling saveBearingCapacityResult.
After a successful save, allow navigation to my_projects.html with tab and highlightResultId URL parameters pointing to the saved result so it can be emphasized in the My Plans view.
user_workflows:
Workflow 1 – Run a new calculation from a direct visit:
1) User navigates to bearing_capacity_calculator.html without URL parameters.
2) On page load, the page calls getBearingCapacityCalculatorConfig and initializes the form with defaultValues from the response.
3) User selects a soil type and chooses units for footing width and depth.
4) User enters footingWidth, footingDepth, factorOfSafety, and optionally projectLocation, and confirms or changes the method.
5) User clicks the Calculate action.
6) Page validates inputs and, if valid, calls calculateBearingCapacity with the current form values.
7) On success, the page stores the returned calculationId and displays ultimateBearingCapacity, allowableBearingCapacity, methodUsed, and resultNotes in the result panel.
8) URL is updated to reflect the current form state (soilType, footingWidth, footingDepth, factorOfSafety, projectLocation, method) and the returned calculationId so the state can be restored on reload or sharing.

Workflow 2 – Use preconfigured values from Tools or Resources:
1) User clicks a link on tools.html or resources.html that points to bearing_capacity_calculator.html with one or more of soilType, footingWidth, footingDepth, factorOfSafety, projectLocation, or method as URL parameters.
2) On page load, the page calls getBearingCapacityCalculatorConfig and merges the URL parameters into the form’s initial state, overriding any defaultValues where provided.
3) Pre-filled values appear in the form; user optionally adjusts them.
4) User clicks Calculate.
5) Page calls calculateBearingCapacity with the current form state and shows the results as in Workflow 1.
6) URL parameters are updated to match any changed form values and the returned calculationId.

Workflow 3 – Review or refine a calculation linked from My Plans:
1) User navigates from my_projects.html to bearing_capacity_calculator.html with calculationId and optionally other input parameters in the URL.
2) On load, the page calls getBearingCapacityCalculatorConfig, initializes default form values, then applies any provided URL parameters on top of those defaults so the form and method match the previously saved or linked calculation.
3) If the host environment has pre-rendered result data corresponding to calculationId, the page simply displays that result; otherwise, the user can click Calculate to re-run the calculation with the pre-filled inputs.
4) User modifies any inputs if needed and clicks Calculate to obtain updated results.
5) URL parameters are updated so the refined state, including potentially a new calculationId, is shareable and reloadable.

Workflow 4 – Save a calculation to My Plans and navigate there:
1) After a successful calculation, the Save Result / Save to Project action becomes enabled.
2) User clicks Save Result, opening a dialog that shows key, non-editable input and output summaries and a required projectName field.
3) User enters projectName and confirms save.
4) Page calls saveBearingCapacityResult with the latest calculationId from the last successful calculateBearingCapacity call and the user-entered projectName.
5) On success, the page receives savedResult with its id and related fields, displays a confirmation state, and stores savedResult.id in local component state.
6) The dialog shows a button or link to View in My Plans.
7) User clicks View in My Plans, and the page navigates to my_projects.html with URL parameters tab=calculations and highlightResultId set to savedResult.id so that the My Plans page can highlight that specific saved calculation.
interactions: On initial page load, trigger getBearingCapacityCalculatorConfig, then apply URL parameters on top of the returned default values to build the initial form state and any initial method selection.
When the soil type select control changes, update the internal form state and mark the form as dirty, and optionally update the URL’s soilType parameter when the user runs a calculation.
When the footing width or depth value inputs change, update the internal form state and clear any field-level validation errors for those fields.
When the footing width or depth unit selectors change, update form state, and ensure any displayed dimensional hints or labels reflect the selected units.
When the factor of safety input changes, update form state and validate that the input is present and within the acceptable numeric range before allowing calculation.
When the project location text input changes, update form state and ensure the projectLocation URL parameter will be updated on the next successful calculation.
When the bearing capacity method select control changes, update form state and ensure the method URL parameter is updated on the next successful calculation.

When the user clicks the Calculate button, validate all required fields (soilType, footingWidth, footingWidthUnit, footingDepth, footingDepthUnit, factorOfSafety, method) and, if valid, call calculateBearingCapacity with the current form state.

After calculateBearingCapacity returns success, store the returned calculationId in component state, update the result panel with ultimateBearingCapacity, allowableBearingCapacity, methodUsed, and resultNotes, and update URL parameters for soilType, footingWidth, footingDepth, factorOfSafety, projectLocation, method, and calculationId.
If calculateBearingCapacity returns a non-success state, display an error message in the result panel area and do not update calculationId or URL parameters.

When the user clicks Save Result / Save to Project, open the save dialog only if a calculationId from a successful calculation is available; otherwise, show an inline message prompting the user to calculate first.
Inside the save dialog, when the projectName input changes, update dialog state and clear any validation error for projectName.
When the user submits the save dialog, validate that projectName is non-empty, then call saveBearingCapacityResult with the current calculationId and projectName.
On successful saveBearingCapacityResult, store savedResult in state, update any confirmation messaging, and enable the View in My Plans navigation control.
When the user clicks View in My Plans, navigate to my_projects.html while adding or updating URL parameters tab=calculations and highlightResultId=<savedResult.id>.

When the user modifies any form fields after a calculation, mark the result as stale so that the interface can indicate that the displayed results no longer match the current inputs until Calculate is clicked again.

When the page detects URL parameter changes via navigation (e.g., browser back/forward), re-derive form state from URL and config, and, if needed, clear or recompute the displayed results based on whether the calculationId matches the current inputs.
page_functionality.state_logic: On initial load, the page calls getBearingCapacityCalculatorConfig to obtain soilTypes, units, methods, and defaultValues. It then reads URL parameters soilType, footingWidth, footingDepth, factorOfSafety, projectLocation, method, and calculationId. For each input field, if a corresponding URL parameter is present, that value overrides the defaultValue from the config; otherwise, the config default is used. The method selection is similarly determined. The calculationId parameter, if present, is stored in a dedicated piece of state representing the current calculation reference and can be used for display or by the host environment to pre-render results; it does not trigger any additional interface calls from this page. After a successful calculateBearingCapacity call, the returned calculationId overwrites any previous calculation reference, and the page updates the URL parameters so they reflect the exact input values and the new calculationId. This ensures that reloading the page or sharing the URL restores the same input configuration and calculation reference. When the user changes any form field after a calculation, the page marks the calculation as stale in state but does not immediately change URL parameters; parameters are updated only after the next successful calculation to avoid mismatches between URL state and displayed results. When a save operation completes successfully, the savedResult.id is not written back into the bearing_capacity_calculator.html URL but is instead passed to my_projects.html as highlightResultId when the user navigates there; the calculator page continues to use calculationId for its internal reference. No localStorage or other client-side persistence is used; all rehydration and sharing of state relies solely on URL parameters and the assigned interfaces.

components.functionality: On page initialization, calls getBearingCapacityCalculatorConfig, stores the returned soilTypes, footingWidthUnits, footingDepthUnits, methods, and defaultValues, then merges them with any URL parameters to derive the initial form state and selected method.

Displays and manages the calculator input fields: soil type selector, footing width and unit, footing depth and unit, factor of safety, optional project location, and method selector. Handles validation, tracks dirty state, and exposes a submit event to trigger calculation.

Listens for form submission from the input form, performs input validation, calls calculateBearingCapacity with the current form values, updates the calculation state (including calculationId and latest results), and coordinates URL parameter updates after successful calculations.

Displays the current calculation outputs including ultimateBearingCapacity, allowableBearingCapacity, methodUsed, and resultNotes from the latest successful calculateBearingCapacity call. Also indicates whether the current results are stale relative to unsaved form changes and whether a calculation is in progress or has failed.

Provides the Save Result / Save to Project action. It becomes enabled only when a valid calculationId from a successful calculation is present and results are not stale. When clicked, it opens the save-result-dialog.

Collects projectName for saving the most recent calculation. Shows a summary of the current input and output values (read-only) for user confirmation. On submit, calls saveBearingCapacityResult with the stored calculationId and entered projectName, then exposes savedResult for downstream use.

After a successful save, uses savedResult.id from saveBearingCapacityResult to navigate the user to my_projects.html with URL parameters tab=calculations and highlightResultId set to the saved result ID.

Responsible for reading URL parameters on load and whenever the browser navigation changes, and for writing updated parameters after successful calculations so that the calculator state can be restored by reloading or sharing the URL.

layout.grid_system: Implied 12‑column responsive grid (typical WordPress/corporate layout) with a full-width container for the hero and a centered content column.
layout.layout_pattern: Use a linear, top-to-bottom structure matching the mockup: a fixed, full-width header with logo on the left and main navigation (including the My Plans link) on the right; a full-bleed hero section occupying the remainder of the initial viewport with centrally aligned title and descriptive copy for the Bearing Capacity Calculator; followed by a white, application-style calculator workspace where the input form appears on the left and the result panel on the right within a centered content column.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: Use a linear, top-to-bottom structure matching the mockup: a fixed, full-width header with logo on the left and main navigation (including the My Plans link) on the right; a full-bleed hero section occupying the remainder of the initial viewport with centrally aligned title and descriptive copy for the Bearing Capacity Calculator; followed by a white, application-style calculator workspace where the input form appears on the left and the result panel on the right within a centered content column.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: Keep the header at roughly 10–15% of the viewport height and the hero at the remaining 85–90% of the first viewport, with the calculator workspace beginning just below the hero. Within the calculator workspace, use a centered max-width (e.g., 1080–1200px) content column that spans about 80–90% of the viewport width, with a two-column layout on desktop (input form ~60% width left, result panel ~40% width right) collapsing to a single column on smaller screens. Apply the 8px-based scale for padding and section spacing: 24–32px internal padding for cards/panels, and 48–64px vertical gaps between the hero and calculator section, and between major subsections.
layout_strategies.content_density: Maintain a moderately spacious feel: few competing elements per section, generous line spacing, and clear separation between functional blocks. The hero remains visually light in terms of components (category label, headline, brief intro) over a more visually complex background image. The calculator workspace feels more utilitarian but still airy, with form fields grouped into logical blocks, ample vertical space (16–24px) between rows, and clear separation between the input form, control row, and result panel.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: Preserve the F/Z hybrid pattern: start at the logo in the fixed header, move right to the navigation including the My Plans link, then drop down into the hero where attention is drawn to the centered category pill and large headline. From there, the eye moves downward into the calculator workspace heading and then naturally left to right across the two columns: first to the input form and calculation controls, then to the result panel. Supporting elements like state loaders and URL sync are invisible, and the save-result dialog temporarily overrides all other content by occupying the center of the viewport when active.

layout.overall_description: The page opens with a fixed-height, teal corporate header stretching edge-to-edge across the top, logo aligned left and a clean horizontal navigation on the right, including a My Plans link. Below, a full-bleed hero image fills the first viewport; in its center, a constrained content column floats over the background, presenting a compact category pill, a bold, multi-line Bearing Capacity Calculator headline, and a short line of descriptive text, all horizontally centered and clustered in the mid-height band of the screen. As users scroll past this hero, the background gives way to a white calculator workspace section, still using a centered max-width column. Here, the left side hosts a spacious card containing the bearing capacity input form with the calculation controller anchored at its bottom, while the right side presents the calculation results in a parallel card, visually aligned with the top of the form. A Save Result button sits within the results panel as a contextual action. Invisible functional components manage configuration loading and URL-based state without disrupting the visual rhythm. When the user saves, the page dims under a centered modal dialog, which rises above everything as a focused form for naming and storing the calculation plan.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This component is non-visual and occupies no discernible area on the page. Conceptually, it is positioned near the top of the calculator workspace section, executing as soon as the page or calculator section loads. It initializes and populates the bearing-capacity-input-form (and any related controls) based on URL parameters, but does not create any visible UI chrome, padding, or spacing of its own. Its presence does not alter the flow or alignment of visible components; the layout of the form and result panel appears continuous and uninterrupted.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-rendered logical component that participates in the page’s lifecycle but does not affect layout; effectively fixed to the page load event rather than a specific on-screen location.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Within the main calculator workspace below the hero, the bearing-capacity-input-form appears as the primary left-hand card in a two-column layout. It is housed in a white, subtly elevated panel that spans roughly 55–60% of the centered content width on desktop and sits flush to the left grid margin. The card has generous internal padding (around 24px on all sides) and uses vertical spacing of 16–24px between form groups, reinforcing the spacious corporate feel. The top edge of this form card aligns horizontally with the top edge of the calculation-result-panel on the right, creating a balanced visual line across the page. On larger screens, the form extends downward and may be taller than the result panel; on smaller tablets and mobile screens, the layout collapses to a single-column stack where this form card becomes full-width (spanning 100% of the content column) and appears above the result panel, with about 32–48px of vertical separation between the two cards.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Participates in normal document flow within the calculator section, with responsive behavior: two-column left card on wider screens, stacking to a full-width top card on narrow viewports.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The calculation-controller is visually integrated with the bearing-capacity-input-form as a horizontal control row at the bottom of the form card. It occupies the full width inside the card’s content area, sitting below the final group of form fields with a vertical gap of about 24px. The primary action (e.g., Calculate) appears aligned to the right edge of the card, with any secondary actions (Reset, Clear, etc.) aligned to its left in a compact row, maintaining at least 8–16px horizontal spacing between buttons. This row is visually lighter than the hero but clearly distinguished within the card through alignment and button styling, acting as the form’s lower anchor. On smaller screens, this controller retains its position below the fields and continues to right-align the main button, but it may allow buttons to wrap to a second line if horizontal space is constrained. The surrounding whitespace above and below the row ensures it does not feel cramped, echoing the mockup’s clean, uncluttered density.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Flows within the input form card and is effectively anchored to the form’s bottom edge; remains non-sticky, scrolling naturally with the form while preserving its relative position at the end of the fields.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Directly opposite the input form, the calculation-result-panel occupies the right-hand side of the calculator workspace in a matching card. On desktop, it spans roughly 40–45% of the content width and aligns its top edge with the top of the bearing-capacity-input-form, creating a clear left–right pairing. The card features similar internal padding (around 24px) and uses a vertical structure: a compact title row at the top, optionally followed by a brief status line, then the main numerical results and secondary details stacked downward with 16–24px spacing. The panel visually contrasts calculations and units using clear typographic hierarchy, but remains less visually heavy than the hero. On smaller screens, the result panel shifts below the input form and calculation controller, becoming a full-width card that maintains about 32–48px vertical separation from the form card. The overall effect is a cohesive two-card workspace: input on the left and output on the right, or top/bottom on mobile, mirroring the orderly, balanced feel of the mockup.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Participates in normal flow as a sibling to the input form card. On large screens it may optionally use a subtle sticky behavior, remaining in view as the taller form scrolls, but its default behavior is as a fixed-height card aligned to the top of the calculator section.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The save-result-trigger is a prominent button placed within the calculation-result-panel, positioned in the upper-right area of the card’s content zone. It typically appears either on the same horizontal line as the result panel’s title (right-aligned) or immediately below the title with a modest vertical gap of 16px. This placement keeps the control visually associated with the results it will save, while not competing with the main Calculate action in the form. The button’s size and padding follow the site’s standard CTA style (e.g., 12–16px vertical padding and 24px horizontal padding), but its prominence is secondary to the primary calculation controls. In terms of spatial relationship, it anchors the upper-right of the result card, balancing any textual heading on the left and helping the eye move from reading results to taking the save action.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline element within the result panel card; it moves and resizes with the card, maintaining a top-right alignment on both desktop and mobile layouts.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: When activated, the save-result-dialog appears as a centered modal overlay that sits on top of the entire page, temporarily overriding the normal visual flow. A semi-transparent dark scrim covers the full viewport, subtly muting the header, hero, and calculator workspace beneath. In the center of the screen, a white dialog card appears, approximately 480–560px wide on desktop and up to 90% of viewport width on mobile, with generous padding of about 24–32px and rounded corners consistent with the site’s style. Content inside the dialog is vertically stacked: a short, bold title at the top, a brief explanatory line, the form fields for naming and describing the calculation, and a bottom-aligned horizontal row of actions (Save / Cancel) right-justified, with 16–24px spacing above and below. The dialog is vertically centered in the viewport, with enough surrounding scrim to evoke focus and a calm, uncluttered feel, echoing the mockup’s emphasis on clarity and central alignment.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Fixed-position overlay covering the full viewport with a centered modal card; it does not scroll with the underlying content, though its interior can scroll if content exceeds available height on smaller screens.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The my-plans-navigation-link appears in the fixed top header’s right-hand navigation cluster. Within the header’s horizontal layout, the logo sits at the far left, and a row of navigation items extends to the right; My Plans is one of these items, aligned along the same baseline with equal horizontal spacing (16–24px) between it and adjacent links. The header’s height is consistent (approximately 10–15% of viewport height on first load, typically 64–80px), and the link is vertically centered within this bar. On wide screens, this link is fully visible as a text-based nav item with clear, high-contrast type; on smaller screens, it may move into a collapsed menu, but remains logically part of the top navigation. Its presence reinforces the F-pattern flow across the header: logo on the left, navigation including My Plans on the right, before the eye drops into the hero.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Resides within a fixed-position header that stays pinned to the top of the viewport as the user scrolls; the link itself is static within that header bar.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This component is invisible and does not occupy any visual space on the page. Functionally, it sits at the page level, monitoring changes to the bearing-capacity-input-form, calculation-controller state, and calculation-result-panel outputs and updating the URL parameters accordingly. It also responds to URL changes by updating those same components. There is no padding, margin, or visual artifact associated with it, so the user perceives a clean interface where the header, hero, and calculator workspace remain visually uninterrupted.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-rendered behavioral layer attached to the page and history/URL, without any impact on physical layout or scroll behavior.

name: Our Team Directory
title: Our Team Directory
description: Searchable directory of team members with filters by region, discipline, experience, and skills, and quick access to the current project team shortlist.

core_features: Display a paginated list of team members using getTeamMembers with filters derived from URL parameters.
Provide filter controls for region, discipline, and minimum years of experience using options from getTeamFilterOptions.
Provide a skills keyword search field that filters team members by matching skills via getTeamMembers.
Provide a sort control that updates the sortBy URL parameter and re-queries getTeamMembers.
Implement pagination using page and a derived offset parameter for getTeamMembers.
Display a shortlist preview panel using getProjectTeamShortlist with navigation to the full project team page (my_projects.html?tab=team).
Allow navigation to individual team member profiles (team_member.html?id=...) by clicking on member cards or 'View profile' actions.
Keep the directory state (filters, search, sorting, pagination) in sync with URL parameters so it is shareable and supports browser navigation.
user_workflows:
Workflow 1: Initial load with optional incoming filters
1. User opens team.html directly, or via 'Our Team' navigation, or via a link from another page that includes region and/or discipline URL parameters.
2. The state manager component reads URL parameters: region, discipline, minYears, skills, sortBy, and page.
3. The page calls getTeamFilterOptions with no parameters to obtain the available regions, disciplines, and minimum years of experience options.
4. The filter controls are initialized with the values from the URL (if present) and bound to the filter options from getTeamFilterOptions.
5. The state manager builds a filters object from the URL parameters and calculates limit and offset for pagination.
6. The page calls getTeamMembers(filters, sortBy, limit, offset) to retrieve the initial list of team members.
7. In parallel, the page calls getProjectTeamShortlist() to fetch the current shortlist for display in the shortlist preview component.
8. The team member list, shortlist preview, and any results summary are rendered based on the returned data.

Workflow 2: Adjusting filters (region, discipline, experience)
1. User changes one or more filter controls in the filter bar (region, discipline, or minimum years of experience).
2. The filter bar emits a filtersChanged event containing the new filter values.
3. The state manager receives the event, updates the URL query parameters (region, discipline, minYears) accordingly, resets page to the first page, and updates sortBy and skills only if they are changed.
4. The state manager recalculates offset from the updated page and limit and calls getTeamMembers with the updated filters and existing sortBy.
5. The team member list component is refreshed with the new results.
6. Browser back/forward navigation restores previous filter states because they are encoded in the URL parameters.

Workflow 3: Skills keyword search
1. User enters a keyword into the skills search field and submits the search.
2. The search form component emits a skillsSearchSubmit event with the keyword string.
3. The state manager updates the skills URL parameter with the submitted keyword and resets page to the first page while preserving other filters and sortBy.
4. The state manager rebuilds the filters object including the skills value and calls getTeamMembers(filters, sortBy, limit, offset).
5. The team member list rerenders based on the updated results.
6. If the user clears the skills field, the search form emits a skillsSearchClear event, the state manager removes the skills parameter from the URL, resets page if needed, and calls getTeamMembers again without a skills filter.

Workflow 4: Sorting results
1. User selects a sort option in the sort control (for example, a sort order supported by the backend for getTeamMembers).
2. The sort control emits a sortChange event with the selected sortBy value.
3. The state manager updates the sortBy URL parameter, resets page to the first page, and preserves existing filters and skills.
4. The state manager recalculates offset for page 1 and calls getTeamMembers(filters, sortBy, limit, offset).
5. The team member list updates to reflect the new sort order.

Workflow 5: Pagination
1. User clicks a pagination control such as next, previous, or a specific page number.
2. The pagination component emits a pageChange event with the target page number.
3. The state manager validates the page number, updates the page URL parameter, and keeps other parameters (region, discipline, minYears, skills, sortBy) unchanged.
4. The state manager derives offset from the updated page and current limit, then calls getTeamMembers(filters, sortBy, limit, offset).
5. The team member list rerenders with the results for the selected page.
6. If browser back/forward navigation changes the page parameter, the state manager re-runs this process so pagination remains consistent.

Workflow 6: Viewing a team member profile
1. User clicks a team member card or an explicit 'View profile' link within a card in the team member list.
2. The team member list component reads the id of the selected TeamMember and constructs a navigation URL team_member.html?id={teamMemberId}.
3. The browser navigates to team_member.html with the id parameter; no additional API calls are made on team.html for this action.
4. When the user navigates back to team.html, the previous state is restored from the URL parameters without needing local storage.

Workflow 7: Accessing the project team shortlist
1. On page load, the state manager calls getProjectTeamShortlist() to retrieve the current shortlist entries.
2. The shortlist preview component displays the shortlist items and the total count based on the returned data.
3. User clicks on an individual shortlist item (optional behavior) to view the corresponding team member profile; the component navigates to team_member.html?id={teamMemberId} using the teamMemberId from the shortlist data.
4. User clicks a 'View project team' or similar action in the shortlist preview.
5. The component navigates the browser to my_projects.html?tab=team to display the full shortlist on the My Plans & Project Team page.

Workflow 8: Handling URL changes directly
1. A user manually edits the URL query string (for example, changing region, discipline, page, skills, or sortBy) or arrives via a saved/shared URL.
2. The state manager detects the URL parameters on load (or via a popstate event for back/forward navigation).
3. The state manager updates its internal representation of the filters, sort, and pagination based solely on the URL, then calls getTeamMembers with the corresponding filters, sortBy, limit, and offset.
4. The filter bar, search input, sort control, and pagination components are updated to reflect the values encoded in the URL, ensuring the interface is consistent with the shareable URL state.
