interactions: Changing region filter: When the user selects a different region from the region dropdown, onRegionChange is triggered, the region query parameter is updated in the URL, page is reset to the first page, and getTeamMembers is called with the updated region filter.
Changing discipline filter: When the user selects a discipline, onDisciplineChange updates the discipline URL parameter, resets page, and refreshes results via getTeamMembers.

Changing minimum years of experience: When the user selects a minimum years option, onMinYearsChange updates the minYears URL parameter, resets page, and triggers a new getTeamMembers call with the updated filter.

Submitting a skills search: When the user submits the skills search form, onSkillsSearchSubmit updates the skills URL parameter, resets page, and calls getTeamMembers with the skills value included in the filters.

Clearing a skills search: When the user clears the skills field, onSkillsSearchClear removes the skills parameter from the URL, resets page if needed, and calls getTeamMembers without a skills filter.

Changing sort order: When the user selects a new sort option, onSortChange updates the sortBy URL parameter and causes getTeamMembers to be called with the new sortBy, with page reset to the first page.

Navigating between pages: When the user clicks next, previous, or a page number, onPageChange updates the page URL parameter, recalculates offset, and calls getTeamMembers with the same filters and sortBy but new pagination settings.
Clicking a team member card: When the user clicks a team member card or 'View profile' link, onTeamMemberClick navigates the browser to team_member.html?id={teamMemberId}.

Clicking a shortlist item: When the user clicks on a team member within the shortlist preview, onShortlistItemClick uses the teamMemberId from getProjectTeamShortlist data and navigates to team_member.html?id={teamMemberId}.
Clicking 'View project team' from shortlist: When the user clicks the shortlist panel's link to view the full project team, onViewProjectTeamClick navigates to my_projects.html?tab=team.

Browser back/forward navigation: When the user uses browser navigation buttons, onUrlChange (popstate listener) re-parses the URL parameters and re-invokes getTeamMembers with the restored filters, sortBy, and page, keeping the directory in sync with the URL.
page_functionality.state_logic:
The page state is fully derived from and synchronized with URL query parameters; no localStorage or other client-side persistence is used. On initial load, the state manager reads the following parameters: region, discipline, minYears, skills, sortBy, and page. Incoming links from about.html or index.html may provide region and/or discipline parameters to pre-filter results. If a parameter is absent or invalid, the component uses neutral defaults (such as 'no filter' and the first page) determined by application-level logic, not hardcoded within this page.

For each interaction that changes filters, search text, sorting, or pagination, the state manager updates the URL query string using navigation APIs so the URL remains the single source of truth. Specifically:
- region in the URL represents the currently selected region filter. Removing it in the URL clears the region filter.
- discipline represents the selected discipline filter.
- minYears represents the selected minimum years of experience.
- skills represents the skills keyword search. An empty or missing skills parameter means no skills keyword is applied.
- sortBy represents the current sort selection used when calling getTeamMembers. The page does not assume any particular sort values beyond those recognized by the backend.
- page represents the current page index used for pagination. The state manager converts page to an offset when calling getTeamMembers using offset = (pageIndex - 1) * limit.

The state manager uses these parameters to construct the filters argument for getTeamMembers. Only parameters that are present and valid are included in the filters object so that getTeamMembers receives a clean set of filters. limit is treated as a page size value provided externally (for example, via configuration) and is kept consistent across calls. The state manager infers whether additional pages may exist from the number of records returned by getTeamMembers relative to limit; if fewer results than limit are returned, the next page controls can be disabled.

Any time the URL changes (either via user interactions on the page or browser back/forward navigation), the state manager re-parses the URL, updates its internal representation of current filters, skills, sortBy, and page, and re-invokes getTeamMembers with these values. Child components (filter bar, search input, sort control, pagination, team list, and shortlist preview) receive their props from this centralized state, ensuring that the UI is consistent with the URL and that links to specific filter/sort/search combinations are shareable and restorable.

components.functionality: Coordinates data fetching and state synchronization between URL parameters, filters, sorting, pagination, the team list, and the shortlist. It parses URL parameters on load, constructs the filters object, manages limit and offset, calls getTeamFilterOptions, getTeamMembers, and getProjectTeamShortlist, and distributes the resulting data and state to child components.

Displays and manages filter controls for region, discipline, minimum years of experience, and the skills search field. It binds to options from getTeamFilterOptions and to the current selection values derived from URL parameters. On user changes, it emits events to request URL and data updates via the state manager.

Provides a control to change the sort order of team members. It reads the current sortBy value from the state manager and lets the user select among backend-supported sort options. When the selection changes, it notifies the state manager, which updates the sortBy URL parameter and re-fetches team members.

Displays a summary of the currently visible team member results (such as the count of items returned by getTeamMembers for the current page) and may also echo the active filters and skills search based on URL-derived state. It updates automatically whenever the team member list or filters change.

Renders the list of team members returned by getTeamMembers for the current filters, sort, and pagination settings. Each list item (card or row) shows relevant TeamMember fields and allows navigation to the detailed profile page when clicked.

Provides controls to navigate between pages of results. It displays the current page (from the page URL parameter) and determines whether next/previous controls should be enabled based on the number of TeamMember records returned relative to the configured limit. When the user chooses a different page, it emits an event that causes the state manager to update the page URL parameter and re-fetch data.

Displays a concise view of the current project team shortlist using data returned by getProjectTeamShortlist. It may show each shortlisted member with basic information and provides a link to open the full project team view on my_projects.html. It can also allow navigation to an individual team member's profile when a shortlist item is clicked.

layout.grid_system: Implied 12‑column responsive grid (typical WordPress/corporate layout) with a full-width container for the hero and a centered content column.
layout.layout_pattern: Below the site’s fixed full-width header (and any global hero), the Team Directory content follows a linear top-to-bottom structure that echoes the mockup’s hero stack: a compact, high-level summary and sort row at the top, followed by a full-width, center-aligned filter bar acting as a functional hero band, then a main results area with the team member list as the primary left column and the project team shortlist preview as a supporting right column. Pagination anchors the bottom of the list. All interactive controls are grouped in the upper third of the content area, mirroring the mockup’s centrally clustered hero content.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: Below the site’s fixed full-width header (and any global hero), the Team Directory content follows a linear top-to-bottom structure that echoes the mockup’s hero stack: a compact, high-level summary and sort row at the top, followed by a full-width, center-aligned filter bar acting as a functional hero band, then a main results area with the team member list as the primary left column and the project team shortlist preview as a supporting right column. Pagination anchors the bottom of the list. All interactive controls are grouped in the upper third of the content area, mirroring the mockup’s centrally clustered hero content.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The header retains roughly 10–15% viewport height. The directory’s control band (summary + sort + filters) visually occupies the upper 25–35% of the remaining viewport, with generous vertical padding (32–48px) to feel like a functional hero. The main results area fills the rest of the page, arranged in a 12‑column grid where the team list uses approximately 8 columns (about two-thirds of the width) and the shortlist preview uses approximately 4 columns (about one-third). Horizontal content is kept within a centered max-width container (e.g., ~1,140–1,280px), with lateral padding of 24–32px to protect text from screen edges, while background treatments and section bands extend full-width.
layout_strategies.content_density: Controls and labels follow a moderately spacious rhythm, with 16–24px internal padding for each band or card and 24–32px vertical gaps between stacked elements. The filter bar and summary row are uncluttered, with clear groupings and breathing room around each control. The team member list is denser vertically (cards stacked with 16–24px gaps) but still respects the corporate spaciousness. The shortlist preview is a compact yet readable panel with comfortable padding and a slightly denser arrangement than the hero band, to signal its utility without overwhelming the main content.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The eye lands first under the global header on the top-left of the directory content: the results summary. It then sweeps horizontally to the right to pick up the sort control, mirroring the mockup’s logo-to-nav scan. From there, attention drops down to the centered, full-width filter bar acting like the hero’s category pill and title cluster. After filters, the gaze follows a Z-shaped path into the left-aligned team member list (primary content), then across to the right column where the project team shortlist preview remains visible and slightly elevated. Pagination at the bottom of the list provides a clear end-stop and next-step in the scanning flow.

layout.overall_description: Beneath the site’s fixed teal header and any global hero banner, the Our Team Directory page opens into a full-width, light background band that behaves like a functional hero: within a centered content column, a concise results summary sits on the top-left with the sort control aligned to the top-right, forming a clear horizontal bar of high-level controls. Directly underneath, a full-width filter bar stretches across the main content column, its controls center-aligned and spaced with generous padding so it reads as the core interaction hub, much like the centrally clustered hero text in the mockup. Below this control band, the layout settles into a two-column main area on desktop: on the left, the team member list occupies about two-thirds of the width, presenting vertically stacked cards with comfortable spacing; on the right, a slimmer project team shortlist panel occupies the remaining third, aligned to the top of the list and staying visible as the user scrolls. Pagination controls sit beneath the list, aligning with the main column and providing a clean, grounded end to the content. The overall feel is clean and corporate, with strong horizontal bands, generous vertical spacing between sections, and a visual flow that naturally leads from global navigation, to filters, to results, and finally to shortlist actions.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The state manager is an invisible structural layer that spans the full width of the main content area beneath the global header (and any global hero). It wraps all other directory components inside a centered max-width container that aligns with the site’s 12-column grid. Vertically, it begins roughly 48–64px below the bottom edge of the hero or page title band and extends down to the end of the pagination controls. All visual components (summary, sort, filters, list, shortlist, pagination) are arranged within this manager in a strict top-to-bottom sequence, maintaining the linear flow observed in the mockup while internally coordinating responsive layouts and interactions.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual structural container that occupies the full content width; scrolls with the page and governs layout breakpoints and state, but does not itself appear as a visible block.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The filter bar sits directly beneath the results summary/sort row, forming the most visually dominant band of the directory content. It occupies the full width of the central content column (approximately 80–90% of the viewport width, capped at the site’s max content width) and is horizontally centered on the page. Internally, filters are arranged left-to-right in a single row on desktop (e.g., Region, Discipline, Experience, Skills), each rendered as a clean, pill-like or outlined control with 16–24px of internal padding and at least 16px horizontal gaps, matching the spacious control rhythm from the mockup. Vertically, the filter bar has 24–32px top and bottom padding, making it feel like a shallow functional hero band. On smaller screens, the controls wrap into two or more rows but remain center-aligned within the same band. The filter bar visually anchors the upper third of the directory and is the main focal point after the user’s initial scan of summary and sort.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Initially positioned below the summary/sort row; becomes sticky to the top of the viewport once the user scrolls past it, docking just under the fixed site header so filters remain persistently accessible while the list scrolls below.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The sort control is positioned in the top-right corner of the directory’s control area, on the same horizontal row as the results summary. It aligns to the right edge of the main content column, roughly 48px below the bottom of the global hero/title area, and sits within a compact pill or button group that echoes the navigation spacing from the mockup: 8–16px internal padding, clear label, and an adjacent icon or chevron. On larger screens, it appears on a single line opposite the summary, creating a balanced horizontal bar stretching from left to right. On tablet and mobile, it drops directly below the summary but remains right-aligned within the content column and uses the same width as its label, preserving the impression of a controlled, ordered layout. It maintains a comfortable 16–24px vertical gap from the filter bar below.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline, non-sticky control that scrolls with the page. It remains horizontally anchored to the right side of the main content column but reflows beneath the summary on narrow viewports.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The results summary is the first visible element of the directory content band and is placed at the top-left of the main content column, directly beneath the global hero or page title area. It spans roughly 60–70% of the row’s width on desktop, leaving space for the sort control on the right. The summary text (e.g., “128 specialists available worldwide”) is set in a medium-weight, high-contrast type, visually reminiscent of the hero’s metadata line: compact, single-line where possible, and aligned baseline with the sort control. There is 24–32px top padding above the summary (separating it from the hero/title) and approximately 8–16px of vertical space below it before the filter bar band. The alignment and spacing guide the eye from this summary toward the sort control, then down to the filters, echoing the F/Z-shaped reading path from the mockup.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, scrolls with the page as part of the top control band. It flexes responsively so that on smaller screens it becomes a full-width block above the sort control, while maintaining left alignment within the content column.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The team member list occupies the primary left column in the main results area, starting approximately 32–48px below the bottom of the filter bar. It uses about 8 of the 12 grid columns on desktop (roughly two-thirds of the content width), left-aligned within the central column. Each team member is displayed as a card spanning the full width of this column, with 16–24px internal padding and 16–24px vertical gaps between cards, creating a clean but information-rich stack. The list column extends downward for as many rows as needed, with the project team shortlist panel aligning to its right. On very wide screens, the list maintains its max-width alignment and does not stretch excessively, keeping text measure comfortable. On tablet and mobile, the list becomes full-width, moving above the shortlist panel when the layout collapses to a single column. The list visually dominates the mid-to-lower portion of the viewport, analogous to how the large headline dominates the center of the hero in the mockup.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Scrolls normally as the main content column. It sits to the left of the shortlist preview on desktop and reflows to stack above it on smaller breakpoints (e.g., below ~992px), always maintaining its role as the primary vertical scroll content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The pagination controls are positioned directly beneath the team member list, separated by 24–32px of vertical space to clearly mark the end of the current result set. They align with the left edge of the list column and, on desktop, may stretch horizontally up to about half of the list width, depending on the number of page controls. The controls are arranged in a single horizontal row: previous/next buttons and page numbers laid out with 8–16px spacing, following the ordered, airy feel of the navigation from the mockup. The entire pagination block sits within the same central content column as the list and shortlist, with an additional 48–64px margin below it to provide breathing room before any footer content, reinforcing it as the visual bottom of the directory content.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block at the bottom of the list column that scrolls into view as the user reaches the end of the list. It remains left-aligned with the list on desktop and becomes full-width centered on mobile while preserving consistent spacing.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The project team shortlist preview sits to the right of the team member list in the main results area, aligning its top edge with the first visible row of the list. On desktop, it occupies about 4 of the 12 grid columns (roughly one-third of the content width) and is inset within the same centered max-width container, creating a clear two-column layout. The panel has 16–24px internal padding and a subtle card-like treatment (e.g., light surface, faint border or shadow) to distinguish it from the background while staying minimal. Content inside is stacked top-to-bottom: a concise title at the top, followed by the current shortlist items, and quick actions (e.g., view full shortlist, export) near the bottom. Vertical spacing inside the panel follows the 8–16–24px rhythm so it feels compact but not cramped. The shortlist column runs parallel to the list and typically does not extend as tall; when shorter than the list, it ends gracefully with extra white space below. On tablet and mobile, this panel reflows beneath the filter bar and above or below the list (recommended: above the list) as a full-width block, preserving its importance without overcrowding the main list area.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: On desktop, the shortlist panel is sticky within the viewport: once its top edge reaches a small offset below the fixed header (e.g., 16–24px), it remains fixed in place while the team member list scrolls independently beside it. On smaller breakpoints where it becomes full-width, it loses stickiness and scrolls normally with the rest of the content to avoid obstructing vertical space.

name: Team Member Profile
title: Team Member Profile
description: Displays detailed information about a selected geotechnical expert, including experience, skills, and related projects, and allows adding the expert to the user's project team shortlist.

core_features: Load and display full team member details based on the URL parameter id using getTeamMemberDetail.
Show key profile information including job title, discipline, region, years of experience, experience summary, and availability status.
Display a structured list of the team member's key technical skills.
Present a detailed biography or narrative description of the team member.
List related project case studies associated with the team member and enable navigation to project_detail.html with the selected project id.
Provide an Add to project team / Shortlist action that calls addTeamMemberToShortlist with optional context and notes.
Display confirmation and basic details of the shortlist result returned from addTeamMemberToShortlist.
Offer navigation back to the team directory (team.html) to continue filtering and building a shortlist, preserving any directory state via URL parameters when available.
Offer navigation to the project team tab of my_projects.html (tab=team) after successfully shortlisting a team member.
user_workflows: Workflow 1 – View a team member from the team directory and add to shortlist: (1) User navigates from team.html to team_member.html with id set to the selected team member's id and may include additional directory state parameters (for example filter or sort values). (2) On page load, the team_member.html page reads id from the URL and calls getTeamMemberDetail with this value. (3) The page shows a loading state until getTeamMemberDetail returns; on success it displays the team member's header information, skills, and bio, as well as a list of relatedProjects. (4) The user reviews the profile and optionally enters context and notes into the shortlist form. (5) The user clicks the Add to project team button, which triggers addTeamMemberToShortlist with teamMemberId taken from the loaded profile and context/notes from the form. (6) On success, the page shows a success message and a summary of the returned shortlistItem and enables a View project team action that navigates to my_projects.html?tab=team. (7) The user can either click Back to team directory to return to team.html, optionally including preserved filter/sort URL parameters, or continue to my_projects.html to review the team shortlist.

Workflow 2 – Review a shortlisted team member from My Projects: (1) User is on my_projects.html, on the team tab, where each ProjectTeamShortlistItem links to team_member.html with id set to the associated teamMemberId. (2) When the user clicks a team member, the browser navigates to team_member.html?id={teamMemberId}. (3) The page reads id and calls getTeamMemberDetail to load the profile and relatedProjects. (4) The user reviews the member details in context of the existing shortlist. (5) If the member should be added again with different notes for a new project context, the user can enter a new context and notes in the shortlist form and click Add to project team, which calls addTeamMemberToShortlist. (6) On success, the page displays the server message and the new shortlistItem details and allows navigation to my_projects.html?tab=team to review combined shortlist entries. (7) The user may use Back to team directory or the browser back button to return to the previous page as needed.

Workflow 3 – Explore related project case studies from a team member profile: (1) User lands on team_member.html with id set and the page calls getTeamMemberDetail. (2) After successful data load, the relatedProjects list is displayed with each project as a clickable item. (3) The user clicks a project entry. (4) The page navigates to project_detail.html?id={projectId} where projectId is taken from the selected ProjectCaseStudy record returned in relatedProjects. (5) The user can review the project details and then use standard navigation (browser back or project links) to return as needed.

Workflow 4 – Handle data loading or API errors: (1) User navigates to team_member.html with id in the URL. (2) The page calls getTeamMemberDetail. (3) If the call fails (for example, invalid id or network error), the page shows an error state with a retry control that re-invokes getTeamMemberDetail using the same id and an option to navigate back to team.html. (4) If addTeamMemberToShortlist fails when the user attempts to add the member, the page shows an inline error message based on the returned message while keeping the existing profile visible, allowing the user to adjust context/notes and retry.
interactions: On initial page load or when the id URL parameter changes, a data-loading interaction occurs where the page triggers getTeamMemberDetail(teamMemberId=id) and displays a loading state until either data or an error is returned.

Clicking the Back to team directory control triggers navigation to team.html; if the current URL includes extra query parameters representing prior filter or search state, those parameters are forwarded to team.html to restore the previous directory view.

Clicking the Add to project team button triggers form validation for the context and notes inputs (for example, checking for required or maximum lengths if defined by business rules) and then calls addTeamMemberToShortlist with teamMemberId from the loaded TeamMember data and context/notes from the form; while the request is pending, the button is disabled and a progress indicator is shown.

On successful response from addTeamMemberToShortlist, the page displays the returned message and details from shortlistItem (such as teamMemberName, jobTitle, region, discipline, yearsOfExperience, and keySkills) in a confirmation area, and reveals or enables a View project team interaction that navigates to my_projects.html?tab=team when clicked.

If addTeamMemberToShortlist returns success=false or an error, the page displays an error message (using the returned message), leaves the form inputs unchanged, and re-enables the Add to project team button so the user can adjust context/notes and retry.
Clicking any related project item in the relatedProjects list triggers navigation to project_detail.html?id={projectId}, where projectId is taken from the selected ProjectCaseStudy entity.

The availability status of the team member (isAvailableForProjects) directly influences interaction behavior: if isAvailableForProjects is false, the Add to project team button is disabled or its label is adjusted to reflect unavailability while still allowing the user to review profile and relatedProjects.

If the user manually changes the id query parameter in the URL and confirms navigation (for example via address bar), the page re-runs the getTeamMemberDetail load process for the new team member, updating all bound components without requiring a full browser reload beyond standard navigation.
page_functionality.state_logic: The page state is determined entirely by URL parameters and API responses. The primary required parameter is id, representing the teamMemberId. On each navigation to team_member.html, the page reads id from the query string and calls getTeamMemberDetail(teamMemberId=id). The result of this call populates the main view state: teamMember (a TeamMember entity) and relatedProjects (an array of ProjectCaseStudy entities). While waiting for this response, the page is in a loading state; if the response fails, an error state is shown. The teamMember.isAvailableForProjects field controls whether the shortlist action is enabled or disabled. When the user submits the shortlist form, the page uses the current teamMember.id as teamMemberId and passes the current values of context and notes into addTeamMemberToShortlist; no additional persistent state is stored on the client. The response from addTeamMemberToShortlist, specifically the success flag, message, and shortlistItem, controls the shortlist confirmation state of the page. If success is true, a confirmation UI is shown and the View project team navigation becomes active; clicking it navigates to my_projects.html?tab=team, where tab=team is encoded in the URL. If success is false, an error state is displayed for the shortlist area only, without changing the loaded profile state. The Back to team directory navigation link always points to team.html; if the incoming URL contains additional query parameters used to represent the previous directory filter or search state (for example, filter, region, discipline, or other parameters as defined elsewhere), these parameters are carried forward to team.html when constructing the back-link URL so that the directory can restore its prior state. For related projects, each item in relatedProjects exposes its id, which is used to construct the navigation URL project_detail.html?id={projectId}. No localStorage or other client-side persistence mechanisms are used; all cross-page continuity relies on query parameters and server-side persistence of shortlist items.

components.functionality: Reads the id parameter from the URL, calls getTeamMemberDetail with this teamMemberId, manages loading and error states, and exposes the loaded teamMember and relatedProjects data to child components.

Displays primary team member information including name, jobTitle, discipline, region, yearsOfExperience, experienceSummary, officeLocation, profileImage, and availability status derived from isAvailableForProjects.
Shows the list of keySkills for the team member, allowing users to quickly scan the expert's main technical capabilities.
Displays the team member's bio text, providing narrative background on experience and focus areas.
Renders the relatedProjects returned by getTeamMemberDetail as clickable items; clicking an item navigates to project_detail.html with the selected project's id as a URL parameter.
Allows the user to add the current team member to the project team shortlist by entering optional context and notes and submitting these via addTeamMemberToShortlist.
Displays the outcome of the most recent addTeamMemberToShortlist call, including success or error messages and, on success, the returned shortlistItem details.
Provides a Back to team directory action that navigates to team.html and, when available, forwards any directory-related query parameters from the current URL to preserve filter/search state.
Provides a View project team action that navigates to my_projects.html?tab=team after a successful shortlist operation, enabling the user to review the full shortlist.

Controls and renders loading and error states for the overall profile load process; it listens to the status of getTeamMemberDetail and shows either a loading indicator, an error with retry, or the loaded content.

layout.grid_system: Implied 12‑column responsive grid (typical WordPress/corporate layout) with a full-width container for the hero and a centered content column.
layout.layout_pattern: The page follows a clean, top-to-bottom hierarchy anchored by a fixed-height teal global header and a full-width hero for the team member. Immediately beneath the fixed header, a hero section presents the expert's identity and core metadata, center-aligned within a constrained content column over a full-bleed technical background image. Below the hero, the layout transitions into a single, centered content column (max-width ~960px) that stacks sections in a logical sequence: local navigation links, shortlist interaction, skills, bio, and related projects. Components are organized in a linear scroll, with occasional horizontal pairing (shortlist form + status) within the 12-column grid while maintaining the overall vertical reading flow.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page follows a clean, top-to-bottom hierarchy anchored by a fixed-height teal global header and a full-width hero for the team member. Immediately beneath the fixed header, a hero section presents the expert's identity and core metadata, center-aligned within a constrained content column over a full-bleed technical background image. Below the hero, the layout transitions into a single, centered content column (max-width ~960px) that stacks sections in a logical sequence: local navigation links, shortlist interaction, skills, bio, and related projects. Components are organized in a linear scroll, with occasional horizontal pairing (shortlist form + status) within the 12-column grid while maintaining the overall vertical reading flow.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The global header occupies roughly 10–15% of the viewport height as a fixed bar. The hero for the team member header visually dominates the initial viewport, with a minimum height of about 60–70% of the viewport so that the user mostly sees the header + hero on first load. The central content column below uses generous vertical spacing between sections (48–64px) and comfortable internal padding (16–24px) within each card/section. Text is kept away from edges with lateral gutters of at least 10–15% of viewport width on large screens, while the hero background image stretches full-bleed from edge to edge.
layout_strategies.content_density: Content density is moderate and marketing-oriented: few elements are shown at once in the hero with large typography and clear hierarchy. Below the hero, each section (skills, bio, related projects, shortlist controls) is grouped into visually distinct blocks with generous padding, clear headings, and ample whitespace. Lists (skills, projects) are compact but not crowded, favoring legibility and scanning over raw density. Buttons and links are spaced so they are easy to tap and visually distinguish, reflecting a corporate, confident feel rather than a data-heavy dashboard.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The visual flow mirrors an F/Z hybrid: attention starts at the logo and global navigation in the fixed header, moves horizontally across the navigation, then drops into the center of the hero where a compact specialization pill and large name headline capture focus. The eye then travels down to the row of meta information (role, experience, location) before continuing downward into the main content column. Immediately after the hero, the eye catches the back-link on the left and the "view project team" link on the right, then flows into the prominent shortlist call-to-action, and finally into skills, deeper bio, and related projects. Typography contrast and section spacing reinforce this top-down narrative, with the hero and shortlist controls acting as the primary focal points.

layout.overall_description: The page presents as a polished corporate profile within a geotechnical engineering brand. A fixed teal header runs across the very top of the viewport, containing the company logo on the left and horizontally centered navigation on the right. Directly beneath, a full-width hero band fills most of the initial screen with a technical, geotechnical background image. Centered within this hero, a semi-transparent content panel holds a small specialization pill, the expert’s name in a large, high-contrast headline, their role and key stats, and a compact line of metadata, all vertically centered in the mid-zone of the hero. Scrolling down reveals a white main content area in a centered column. At the top of this column sits a slim row with a text link back to the team overview on the left and a link to view the user’s project team on the right. Just below, a prominent shortlist card invites the user to add or manage this expert in their project shortlist, with a companion status panel indicating current shortlist state. Further down, the skills section appears as a structured list of capabilities, followed by a more text-heavy bio section, both left-aligned within the centered column. At the bottom, a related projects list presents selected case studies in a structured grid within the same column. The entire layout feels spacious, with consistent vertical rhythms and a clear visual journey from high-level identity at the top to detailed experience and work examples toward the bottom.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The data loader operates logically at the page level, conceptually wrapping the entire main content area beneath the fixed global header. It does not present a dedicated visual block of its own; instead, it controls when the hero, shortlist, skills, bio, and related projects sections appear. When data is still loading, it ensures that placeholder or skeleton content appears in the hero and key sections within the central content column, occupying roughly the same vertical space as the final components so that the page structure feels stable and does not jump as data arrives.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual controller that governs the visibility and loading states of all below-header components; it does not reserve its own space but affects the content that occupies the hero and main column areas.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The team member header forms the main hero section and sits directly beneath the fixed teal site header, spanning the full width of the viewport. It uses a full-bleed geotechnical or technical background image that stretches from left to right and reaches a minimum height of approximately 60–70% of the viewport. In the horizontal center of this hero, a constrained content column (max-width ~960px, aligned with the rest of the page grid) is centered and padded by 16–24px internally. Within this column, content is stacked and center-aligned: at the top, a compact category/specialization pill (e.g., "Slope Stability", "Foundation Design") appears about one base spacing unit (16–24px) below the bottom of the global header. Below the pill, the expert’s name is rendered as a large, multi-line headline occupying up to 60–70% of the column width, centered and set in high-contrast white text. Directly under the name, a smaller line displays their role, credentials, and firm, also center-aligned. Beneath that, a single horizontal meta row runs across the center: on larger screens, the avatar appears as a circular profile image centered above or just to the left of a row listing years of experience, primary location, and a brief availability or license note, all on one line. Vertically, this stack is positioned near the middle of the hero so that there is generous negative space above and below the text within the hero image field.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Behaves as a full-width, scrollable hero section immediately under a fixed header; the background image remains anchored to the section while the page scrolls, with the centered content column remaining responsive and constrained within the main grid.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The skills list appears in the main content column below the shortlist controls and above the bio section. It is centered horizontally on the page within a column of about 8–10 of the 12 grid columns, leaving generous side gutters on desktop. The section begins with a left-aligned heading (e.g., "Key Skills & Expertise") positioned roughly 48–64px below the bottom edge of the shortlist area. Under the heading, skills are arranged in a clean list or tag grid: on desktop, skills may flow in two or three columns of pill-shaped tags or short rows, each with enough padding (8–16px per pill) and spacing (8–16px between items) to avoid crowding; on smaller screens, the skills collapse into a single column list. The entire section is encapsulated by subtle top and bottom padding (24–32px) and separated from adjacent sections by clear vertical whitespace, so it reads as a distinct but airy block of structured information.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard block-level section within the scrollable main column; it expands vertically based on content length while maintaining fixed side margins relative to the central grid.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The bio section is positioned directly below the skills list in the same centered content column, separated by approximately 48–64px of vertical whitespace to maintain the spacious corporate feel. It occupies the same width as the skills section (roughly 8–10 columns in the central grid), left-aligned for comfortable reading. At the top of this section, a clear heading such as "Professional Background" or "Biography" is placed, followed by multi-paragraph body text that spans about 60–70 characters per line for optimal legibility. Internal padding of 24–32px gives the biography card-like breathing room, and paragraphs are spaced using the base spacing unit (16px) to avoid dense text blocks. On larger screens, optional subheadings or bullet lists (e.g., certifications, memberships) can appear within this section, still left-aligned and confined to the same text column, keeping the layout coherent and readable.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard vertically expanding text block in the main column; it scrolls with the page, maintaining consistent lateral margins and text width across breakpoints.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The related projects list anchors the lower portion of the page and is positioned beneath the bio section with a vertical gap of about 48–64px. It shares the same centered column alignment and width as the skills and bio sections. A section heading such as "Related Geotechnical Projects" introduces the content, left-aligned near the top of the section. Below the heading, project items are arranged as a structured list of cards: on desktop, a 2-column grid of project cards is used within the central column (each card occupying roughly 5–6 columns with a gutter between), while on tablet and mobile, the layout collapses to a single column of full-width cards within the central content area. Each project card has 16–24px internal padding and clear separation (24–32px) between cards, with titles, brief descriptions, and key stats presented in a compact but uncrowded format. This section stretches further down the page as needed but maintains consistent top and bottom margins so it feels like the concluding content block of the profile.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Responsive grid section within the main content column; card layout adapts from 2 columns on larger screens to a single stacked column on smaller devices while preserving the central alignment.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The shortlist form sits near the top of the main content column, immediately below the row containing the back-to-team and view-project-team navigation links. It is visually prominent as a card-like block spanning most of the central column (about 8–10 grid columns wide), centered on the page. The card has generous internal padding (24–32px) and a subtle shadow or border to lift it off the background. On larger screens, the form fields and primary call-to-action button are arranged in a single row or compact vertical stack aligned to the left within the card, with ample horizontal spacing between fields (16–24px) to keep the layout uncluttered. On mobile, the form fields stack vertically, filling the width of the card with comfortable vertical spacing. Vertically, the shortlist form appears about 32–40px below the nav links row, making it one of the first interactive elements seen when scrolling slightly past the hero. The form’s prominence is reinforced by the strong brand color used in the primary button, drawing the eye after the hero.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static card within the scrollable main column; adapts responsively from a slightly wider, horizontal layout on desktop to a vertically stacked form on smaller screens while maintaining its centered alignment.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The shortlist status panel is visually paired with the shortlist form and positioned in close proximity so that status and action are understood together. On desktop and larger tablets, it sits to the right of the shortlist form within the same horizontal row, occupying roughly 3–4 of the 12 grid columns, aligned to the top of the form card. There is a consistent horizontal gap (24–32px) between the form and the status panel, and both align within the same central content width. The status panel itself is a compact card with 16–24px padding, displaying the current shortlist state (e.g., "Not yet added", "Added to Project X") and possibly a subtle icon. On smaller screens, the status panel stacks vertically beneath the shortlist form, separated by about 16–24px of vertical space, remaining centered within the same content column. Its visual styling is slightly more subdued than the form (lighter background, smaller typography) to signal supplementary information while still being easily scannable.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Responsive companion card: sits side-by-side with the shortlist form in a shared row on larger screens, then stacks below the form in the same central column on smaller screens.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The "back to team" navigation link is positioned at the very top of the main content area, directly below the bottom edge of the hero section and aligned to the left within the central content column. It appears as a lightweight textual control (e.g., "← Back to all team members") with modest font size and a subtle brand-colored accent, separated from the hero by about 24–32px of vertical spacing. It shares a horizontal line with the "view project team" link, forming a slim utility navigation row that spans the width of the central column. The back link anchors the left side of this row and is aligned with the left edge of the content column used by the shortlist form and subsequent sections.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, inline navigation element at the top of the main content column; remains left-aligned and visible as the user scrolls past it with the rest of the page content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The "view project team" navigation link shares the same horizontal row as the back-to-team link and is aligned to the right edge of the central content column. Positioned at the top of the main content area, it sits directly opposite the back-link, about 24–32px below the hero section. This creates a clear left-right balance: back navigation to the broader team on the left, and deep navigation into the user’s assembled project team on the right. The link appears as a text link or subtle button with brand-colored text and perhaps a small icon indicating a forward or list view. Its styling is understated so it does not compete with the hero or shortlist call-to-action but remains immediately discoverable.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, inline navigation element anchored to the right side of the central content column; scrolls with the page as part of the main content flow.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The loading and error state controller governs how the page appears when data is not yet available or when an error occurs. In a loading state, it replaces the content of key areas (hero, shortlist, skills, bio, projects) with skeleton blocks that follow the same overall geometry: a full-width hero placeholder bar beneath the fixed header with centered grey lines suggesting the name and meta row, followed by rectangular grey placeholders in the central content column roughly matching the heights of the shortlist card and each section. These placeholders maintain the same vertical spacing (48–64px between major groups) so the page feels structurally consistent even while content is loading. In an error state, a prominent but compact message appears near the top of the main content column, just below the hero area, using a full-width alert bar or card (within the central column) that spans the same width as other sections. The rest of the content may be hidden or replaced with a minimal prompt to retry. The hero background can remain visible to keep brand presence, but the central hero text is replaced with a short, centered error note if the profile itself cannot load.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Acts as an overlay/state switcher for existing sections rather than a separate block; during loading it populates section placeholders in-place, and during errors it inserts an alert-style card near the top of the main content column and/or within the hero’s central content area.

name: My Plans & Project Team
title: My Plans & Project Team
description: Single-user workspace where visitors can review and manage their saved investigation plans, bearing capacity calculation results, and shortlisted project team members, with direct navigation back to the tools and profiles used to create them.

core_features: Load a combined overview of all saved investigation plans, bearing capacity calculation results, and project team shortlist items using getMyPlansOverview.
Display three main sections (tabs) – Plans, Calculations, and Project Team – with the active section controlled by the URL parameter `tab`.
List saved investigation package plans with key metadata, link each plan to its corresponding package_detail.html view, and allow inline renaming via updateSavedInvestigationPlanName.
Allow removal of saved investigation plans via removeSavedInvestigationPlan, with immediate UI updates to the plans list.
List saved bearing capacity calculation results with key inputs and outputs, and link each result to bearing_capacity_calculator.html for revisiting the calculation.
Allow removal of saved bearing capacity results via removeSavedCalculationResult, with the calculations list updating accordingly.
List shortlisted project team members with key attributes and a link to team_member.html for more detailed profiles.
Allow removal of shortlisted team members via removeShortlistedTeamMember, updating the shortlist view in place.
Use URL parameters `highlightPlanId` and `highlightResultId` to visually emphasize specific items in the Plans and Calculations lists when the user arrives from related pages.
user_workflows:
Reviewing and renaming a saved investigation plan:
1. User navigates to my_projects.html (via footer or a confirmation link from package_detail.html).
2. Page initializer reads URL parameters (`tab`, `highlightPlanId`, `highlightResultId`). If `tab` is absent, it defaults to the Plans section.
3. The page calls getMyPlansOverview and populates internal state with savedInvestigationPlans, savedBearingCapacityResults, and projectTeamShortlist.
4. The Plans tab component renders the list of saved plans. If `highlightPlanId` is present, the matching plan row is visually emphasized.
5. The user clicks a plan’s rename/edit control. An inline text input appears populated with the current projectName.
6. The user updates the project name and clicks Save. The component calls updateSavedInvestigationPlanName with the planId and newProjectName.
7. On success, the component updates the displayed projectName for that plan and clears any temporary loading or error state. If the request fails, an error message is shown and the name reverts to the last confirmed value.

Removing a saved investigation plan:
1. In the Plans tab, the user clicks a Remove/Delete control on a specific plan row.
2. The component optionally shows a confirmation interaction (e.g., dialog or inline confirmation state).
3. On confirmation, the component calls removeSavedInvestigationPlan with the corresponding planId.
4. If the response indicates success, the plan is removed from the in-memory list used to render the plans table, and the UI re-renders without that item. If the removed planId equals the current URL `highlightPlanId`, the URL is updated to remove or clear `highlightPlanId`.
5. If the response indicates failure, the plan remains visible and an error message is shown.

Reviewing and managing saved bearing capacity calculation results:
1. The user navigates to my_projects.html with `tab=calculations` and optionally `highlightResultId` (for example, from bearing_capacity_calculator.html after saving a result).
2. The page initializer reads the URL parameters and calls getMyPlansOverview.
3. The Calculations tab renders a list based on savedBearingCapacityResults, highlighting the result whose resultId matches `highlightResultId`.
4. The user can click a "View calculation" action on any row, which navigates to bearing_capacity_calculator.html with `calculationId` set to that row’s resultId.
5. To remove a result, the user clicks its Remove/Delete control. The component calls removeSavedCalculationResult with resultId.
6. On success, the corresponding item is removed from the list and the table re-renders. If the removed resultId matches `highlightResultId`, the URL is updated to remove or clear `highlightResultId`.

Managing the project team shortlist:
1. The user navigates to my_projects.html with `tab=team` (for example, from team.html or team_member.html after adding a team member to the shortlist).
2. The page initializer calls getMyPlansOverview and passes projectTeamShortlist to the Team tab component.
3. The component renders each shortlist item with name, jobTitle, region, discipline, yearsOfExperience, and keySkills.
4. The user clicks a team member’s name to navigate to team_member.html with `id` set to that teamMemberId for a detailed profile.
5. To remove someone from the shortlist, the user clicks a Remove/Delete control. The component calls removeShortlistedTeamMember with shortlistItemId.
6. On success, the shortlist entry is removed from the in-memory list and the view updates without that person. On failure, the item remains visible and an error is shown.

Switching between tabs and preserving state via URL:
1. While on my_projects.html, the user clicks on a different tab (Plans, Calculations, or Project Team) using the tab-navigation component.
2. The event handler updates the browser URL `tab` parameter to the newly selected tab (e.g., `?tab=calculations`). Any highlight parameters not relevant to the new tab can optionally be removed from the URL.
3. The tab-navigation component updates the active tab state to match the `tab` parameter and triggers re-rendering so only the chosen section is visible.
4. Because getMyPlansOverview has already loaded data for all sections, switching tabs uses existing in-memory data without additional API calls, unless a refresh is explicitly needed after an add/remove/rename action.
5. If the user refreshes the page, the URL parameters are re-read so the same tab remains active and any applicable highlights are re-applied.
interactions: On page load, read URL parameters (`tab`, `highlightPlanId`, `highlightResultId`) and call getMyPlansOverview to retrieve all My Plans data.
Click on a tab label (Plans, Calculations, Project Team) to change the active section; the handler updates the URL `tab` parameter and toggles which list is displayed.
In the Plans tab, click on a plan’s rename/edit control to toggle an inline editing state for the projectName field.
In the Plans tab, submit the rename form to call updateSavedInvestigationPlanName with the selected planId and entered newProjectName; handle success/failure and exit the editing state.
In the Plans tab, click on a plan’s Remove/Delete control to call removeSavedInvestigationPlan with the corresponding planId; upon success, remove the plan from the displayed list.
In the Plans tab, click on a plan’s "View package details" navigation control to go to package_detail.html with `packageId` set to that plan’s investigationPackageId.
In the Calculations tab, click on a result’s Remove/Delete control to call removeSavedCalculationResult with the selected resultId; upon success, remove that result from the list.
In the Calculations tab, click on a result’s "View calculation" navigation control to go to bearing_capacity_calculator.html with `calculationId` set to that resultId.
In the Calculations tab, if the URL includes `highlightResultId`, apply a highlight state to the matching result row on initial render.
In the Plansτως tab, if the URL includes `highlightPlanId`, apply a highlight state to the matching plan row on initial render.
In the Team tab, click on a team member’s name to navigate to team_member.html with `id` set to that teamMemberId.

In the Team tab, click on a shortlist item’s Remove/Delete control to call removeShortlistedTeamMember with the corresponding shortlistItemId; upon success, remove that item from the displayed shortlist.
Optionally use a manual refresh interaction (e.g., a "Refresh" control) to re-run getMyPlansOverview and re-sync all three lists with the latest server data after several operations.
page_functionality.state_logic: The page relies on URL parameters and in-memory state, without using localStorage or other persistent client-side storage. On every load or reload of my_projects.html, the page initializer reads `tab`, `highlightPlanId`, and `highlightResultId` from the URL query string. If `tab` is not provided or has an unexpected value, the default active tab is the Plans section. The initializer calls getMyPlansOverview once and stores the returned savedInvestigationPlans, savedBearingCapacityResults, and projectTeamShortlist in page state objects that are passed to child components. The active tab state is controlled by the `tab` parameter and is updated whenever the user clicks a tab; the URL is updated via pushState/replaceState so that refreshing or sharing the URL preserves the selected tab. The highlightPlanId and highlightResultId parameters are used only to apply a transient visual emphasis: in the Plans tab, the row whose planId equals highlightPlanId is highlighted; in the Calculations tab, the row whose resultId equals highlightResultId is highlighted. These highlight parameters do not affect which items are loaded, only their visual state. When an item referenced by a highlight parameter is removed, the URL is updated to remove the corresponding highlight parameter to avoid referencing a non-existent item. Within the lifecycle of the page, lists are updated in memory after successful calls to updateSavedInvestigationPlanName, removeSavedInvestigationPlan, removeSavedCalculationResult, or removeShortlistedTeamMember, without additional queries, unless a manual or automatic refresh re-triggers getMyPlansOverview.

components.functionality: Coordinates initial data loading and state setup for My Plans & Project Team. On page load, parses URL parameters (`tab`, `highlightPlanId`, `highlightResultId`), calls getMyPlansOverview to retrieve the combined data sets, stores the results in page-level state, and passes these state objects plus the parsed parameters into the tab-navigation and content components.

Provides three selectable tabs (Plans, Calculations, Project Team) and controls which content panel is active. Reads the current active tab from page state (derived from the `tab` URL parameter) and updates the URL when a different tab is selected. Notifies container components to show or hide associated lists accordingly.

Displays the list of saved investigation plans, along with controls for renaming, removal, and navigation to package_detail.html. Applies a highlighted state to a specific plan when the planId matches the `highlightPlanId` URL parameter.

Renders each saved investigation plan as a row or card, showing the plan’s projectName, linked investigationPackageId and packageName, status, savedAt timestamp, projectType, price, currency, and boolean indicators for package features. Receives a flag for each row indicating whether it matches `highlightPlanId` and applies a highlight state accordingly.

Handles inline editing of a plan’s projectName. When invoked for a specific plan, shows an input pre-filled with the current projectName and Save/Cancel controls. On Save, calls updateSavedInvestigationPlanName and, upon success, updates the plan name in the plans list and exits edit mode.

Encapsulates the API call to updateSavedInvestigationPlanName. Receives planId and newProjectName from the inline form, executes the API call, interprets the `success` and `message` fields, updates the corresponding plan object in page state on success, and surfaces success or error messages to the UI.

Handles removal of a saved investigation plan. When triggered, calls removeSavedInvestigationPlan with the selected planId. If `success` is true, it removes the matching plan from the in-memory collection used by plans-list. If the removed planId equals `URL.highlightPlanId`, it also triggers an update to the URL to clear the highlight parameter.
Generates a link from each saved plan to its associated package detail page. Uses the plan’s investigationPackageId to construct a navigation to package_detail.html with `packageId` set accordingly.

Displays the list of saved bearing capacity calculation results with key inputs and outputs, allows navigation back to the calculator for a specific result, and supports removal of saved results. Applies highlighting to the result whose resultId matches `highlightResultId`.

Renders each bearing capacity result as a row or card, showing projectName, soilType, footingWidth and unit, footingDepth and unit, factorOfSafety, ultimateBearingCapacity, allowableBearingCapacity, projectLocation, and calculatedAt. Applies a highlight style to the row where resultId equals `highlightResultId`.

Encapsulates the API call to removeSavedCalculationResult. When triggered with a resultId, it calls the API, removes the corresponding result from the calculations list on success, and updates the URL to clear `highlightResultId` if it referenced the removed item.

Constructs navigation from each saved bearing capacity result back to the calculator page. Uses the resultId to build a link to bearing_capacity_calculator.html with `calculationId` set to that resultId.
Displays the project team shortlist using enriched data from projectTeamShortlist. Allows navigation to individual team member profiles and supports removal from the shortlist.

Renders each shortlist item, showing the team member’s name, jobTitle, region, discipline, yearsOfExperience, and keySkills, along with the addedAt timestamp. Provides controls for navigating to the full profile and removing the item from the shortlist.

Handles calls to removeShortlistedTeamMember. When invoked with a shortlistItemId, it calls the API, and on success, removes the matching shortlist item from the local shortlist data set so that the team-shortlist-list re-renders without that entry.
Creates a navigation action from shortlist entries to the full team member profile page. Uses the teamMemberId to construct a URL to team_member.html with `id` populated appropriately.

Determines whether to show data lists or empty-state messaging in each tab. For each of the three datasets (savedInvestigationPlans, savedBearingCapacityResults, projectTeamShortlist), if the corresponding array from getMyPlansOverview is empty, it displays an empty-state message and may show contextual navigation guidance (for example, referring users back to the relevant functional pages).

Provides an optional user-triggered mechanism to re-fetch all My Plans data. When activated, it calls getMyPlansOverview again and replaces the existing in-memory collections for plans, calculations, and shortlist with the new response.

layout.grid_system: Implied 12‑column responsive grid (typical WordPress/corporate layout) with a full-width container for the hero and a centered content column.
layout.layout_pattern: The page follows the same linear, top-to-bottom structure as the mockup: global fixed header at the very top, then a full-width hero-style band introducing the 'My Plans & Project Team' workspace, and beneath that a centered content column that holds the tab navigation and a single active data panel at a time. Within each panel, content is stacked vertically: a slim panel header row (title + refresh/action control), then the main list of items (plans, calculations, or team members). All interactive elements (tabs, list items, inline forms) are aligned to this single central column, mirroring the centered hero content from the mockup.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page follows the same linear, top-to-bottom structure as the mockup: global fixed header at the very top, then a full-width hero-style band introducing the 'My Plans & Project Team' workspace, and beneath that a centered content column that holds the tab navigation and a single active data panel at a time. Within each panel, content is stacked vertically: a slim panel header row (title + refresh/action control), then the main list of items (plans, calculations, or team members). All interactive elements (tabs, list items, inline forms) are aligned to this single central column, mirroring the centered hero content from the mockup.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The fixed global header occupies roughly the top 10–15% of the viewport height. Directly beneath, a full-width hero background (technical geotechnical imagery with a subtle teal overlay) fills the rest of the initial viewport height, with its text block centered horizontally and vertically in the middle 40–60% of the hero. The main workspace content begins just below the hero: the tab navigation sits in the upper part of the scrollable content area with 48–64px of breathing room above and below. The active panel and its list take up the majority of the remaining vertical space, stretching down the page as needed. Horizontally, all workspace components are contained in a centered column spanning about 70–80% of the viewport on desktop (roughly 8 of 12 columns) with generous left/right margins; the hero background image continues to bleed edge-to-edge behind this column.
layout_strategies.content_density: Density is moderate and consistent with the mockup: very few elements per horizontal band, ample whitespace, and clearly separated vertical sections. The hero text block is compact but surrounded by generous breathing room. The tab row has enough spacing that each tab label feels tappable and not cramped. Lists inside panels use a card-like structure with 16–24px padding inside each item and 16–24px vertical gaps between items, respecting the 8px spacing scale. Section-level separations (hero to tabs, tabs to panel, between different content states) use 48–64px vertical margins to maintain a calm, corporate reading experience rather than a dense dashboard.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The visual flow mirrors the F/Z hybrid from the mockup. The eye first catches the site logo at the top-left and sweeps across the fixed header navigation to the right. It then drops into the center of the hero, where a compact category pill (e.g., 'Workspace') sits above a large, multi-line page title and a slimmer meta row. From that central focal point, the gaze continues downward to the tab navigation (scanned left to right across the three tabs), then diagonally down to the first item in the active list. Within each list row, the eye moves from left-aligned primary text (plan or team member name) across to right-aligned secondary actions. The central column, strong typographic hierarchy, and consistent alignment make the flow predictable and aligned with the marketing-style hero while still supporting a functional workspace.

layout.overall_description: Below the site’s fixed teal header and primary navigation, a full-width hero band introduces the 'My Plans & Project Team' area over a subtle technical geotechnical background image. In the center of this hero, a small category pill sits above a large, white, multi-line page title and a single-line meta row, all vertically centered in the middle of the viewport so that this content floats comfortably over the image. As you scroll past the hero, the page transitions into a clean, centered workspace column that floats over a white background. Approximately 64px beneath the hero text, a horizontally centered row of tabs labeled 'Plans', 'Calculations', and 'Project Team' lets the user switch between panels. The active tab is highlighted in the brand teal with an underline, while inactive tabs are mid-gray. Directly beneath the tabs, within the same central column, sits the active data panel: a slim header row with the panel title aligned left and a subtle refresh control aligned right, followed by a vertical stack of card-like items. Each item spans nearly the full width of the column with generous side padding, a left-aligned title that behaves as a navigation link, smaller meta text or supporting details beneath or beside it, and right-aligned controls for renaming or removal. When a list is empty, this card stack is replaced by an empty-state message centered within the panel with breathing room around it. The entire page feels calm, ordered, and consistent with the hero’s corporate aesthetic: strong central alignment, clear hierarchy, and comfortable spacing that guides the eye smoothly from top navigation to hero, then into the workspace content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This lifecycle controller sits conceptually at the root of the workspace content area, just below the global header and hero section. It has no visual representation and does not consume any spatial real estate; instead, it initializes data and state before the tab navigation and panels render. From a layout standpoint, it is effectively invisible, layered behind the main content column that starts with the tab navigation.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual, zero-footprint behavior; runs at page load and does not affect layout spacing directly.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The tab navigation is a horizontal strip centered at the top of the workspace column, positioned approximately 48–64px below the bottom edge of the hero text block. It spans about 60–70% of the page width on desktop, aligned to the implied 12-column grid so that it sits flush within the center 8 columns, leaving even margins on both sides. Three tab labels ('Plans', 'Calculations', 'Project Team') are laid out left-to-right, evenly spaced, with the active tab highlighted in the brand teal and underlined. Vertical padding of 16–24px above and below the tab labels ensures ample click/tap area and a comfortable breathing zone before the data panels begin. On narrower screens, the tab row remains centered, with tabs wrapping or spacing tightening slightly but still maintaining distinct, tappable targets.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the main scrollable content; it scrolls offscreen with the content and does not stick, but visually anchors the top of the workspace section.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The plans panel occupies the main content slot immediately beneath the tab navigation when the 'Plans' tab is active. It is confined to the same centered column (roughly 70–80% of the viewport width) and begins about 24–32px below the tab row to maintain a clear yet tight visual relationship. At the top of the panel is a slim header row: on the left, a medium-weight panel title such as 'Saved Investigation Plans' aligned with the left edge of the column; on the right, the refresh control aligns with the column’s right edge, creating a balanced horizontal bar across the full width. Below this header, the plans list fills the remainder of the panel with vertically stacked cards. The panel extends downward with as many cards as needed, maintaining the same left and right alignment and leaving at least 48px of bottom margin before the page footer or next major section.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Only one data panel (plans, calculations, or team) is visible at a time; this panel is shown when its tab is active and hidden (but preserving internal layout) when inactive.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Within the plans panel, the plans list is a vertical stack of card-like items that fills the width of the central column minus internal padding. Each plan row is a horizontally oriented card with 16–24px padding on all sides and a subtle divider or 16–24px vertical gap separating it from the next card, following the 8px spacing system. The primary plan title appears at the upper left of each card in a bold, high-contrast type, acting as the main clickable navigation link. Supporting details (e.g., site name, last modified date) appear in smaller, lighter text just beneath the title or to its right, depending on available width. On the right side of each card, aligned vertically with the title, sit compact action controls for renaming and removing the plan. The entire card area feels comfortably spacious, with text never touching edges, and aligns to the same left and right margins as other panel content, reinforcing the ordered, corporate look.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static vertical list; expands downward as items are added. Cards maintain consistent height based on content and remain within the main scroll flow.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The inline rename form appears in place of the plan title within a specific plan card when the user chooses to rename it. It is left-aligned in the same position the title text normally occupies, preserving the card’s existing padding and not altering the card’s overall left margin. The form consists of a single-line input field that spans roughly 60–70% of the card’s width from the left edge, with a thin border and comfortable vertical padding (8–12px) to match the typography. To the immediate right of the input, in the same horizontal line, sit compact confirm and cancel icons or buttons, aligned to the input’s baseline but stopping short of the card’s right-side actions. The rest of the card content (meta details and right-side action icons) shifts slightly down if needed but stays visually grouped with the renamed item, ensuring the card remains a coherent unit with minimal layout disturbance.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Contextual and transient; it only occupies space within the affected card and appears/disappears without reflowing surrounding cards beyond minor height adjustments.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This API action is not represented visually on the page. It is conceptually associated with the inline rename form inside each plan card and executes when the user confirms the rename. It does not add or remove any visible elements and therefore has no width, height, or position in the layout.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual, zero-footprint; operates behind the scenes without affecting layout spacing.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This API handler is tied conceptually to the delete/remove control located on the right side of each plan card. It triggers when the user confirms removal but has no visible representation. The only visible layout change is the removal of the corresponding card from the vertical plans list, causing the cards beneath it to shift upward and close the gap while maintaining the established vertical spacing between cards.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual; affects layout only indirectly by removing a card from the list, after which remaining items reflow upward in the same column.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The plans navigation link is embodied primarily in the plan title text within each card and optionally by making the entire card surface clickable. It is positioned at the top-left area of the card, aligned with the card’s left padding, and uses a slightly larger, bold font with high contrast (typically dark on light) so it stands out as the entry point. When hovered or focused, the title may gain an underline or subtle color shift in the brand teal, reinforcing its interactive nature, while the card’s background may slightly elevate (soft shadow or tint) without breaking the clean corporate aesthetic. This link leads users back to the specific tool or configuration screen used to create that plan.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, inline with the card text; participates in normal document flow. The interactive hit area may extend to the full card bounds while maintaining the same visual alignment.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The calculations panel occupies the exact same column and vertical slot as the plans panel but is shown only when the 'Calculations' tab is active. It begins 24–32px below the tab row and spans the central 70–80% of the page width. A slim header row at the top mirrors the plans panel: the left side holds a label such as 'Bearing Capacity Calculations', and the right side can reuse the refresh control for this data set. Below this header, the calculations list fills the panel’s vertical space with rows of saved calculation results, adhering to the same side margins and spacing scheme, ensuring consistency as the user switches between tabs.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Mutually exclusive with the other data panels; appears in the same scroll position and column when its tab is active and is otherwise hidden from view.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The calculations list presents saved bearing capacity or related results as a vertical sequence of more compact cards. Each card spans the panel’s width with 16–24px internal padding, and items are separated by 16–24px gaps or dividers. The left side of each card shows a concise, descriptive title (e.g., soil profile or project reference) as the main navigation link, with a secondary line beneath it for key parameters (e.g., load type, factor of safety) in smaller text. On the right side, aligned with the title, are small controls for viewing details and removing the calculation. The cards maintain the same visual rhythm and spacing as the plans list, but the content is slightly denser due to the nature of numeric data, still respecting whitespace to remain readable.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, vertically scrolling list; extends downwards within the central column with consistent spacing and alignment.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This API handler, linked conceptually to the removal control on each calculation card, has no visible UI element. It executes when a calculation is deleted and causes the corresponding card to be removed from the calculations list. The remaining cards slide upward to close the space, preserving the list’s even vertical rhythm.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual; affects layout indirectly by removing a card and allowing the list to reflow naturally.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The calculation navigation link is represented by the primary title text in each calculation card. It is placed at the top-left of the card, aligned to the card’s internal padding, in a slightly larger, bold font. On hover or focus, it adopts an interactive styling similar to the plans links (e.g., color shift to teal and/or underline) to show it leads back to the calculation tool or detailed result view.
