The surrounding card may also be clickable to create a large, easy target, but visually the link remains anchored to the left, maintaining the list’s strong left edge.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, inline with text; part of the normal flow inside each card, with the clickable area potentially extended across the card without changing its visible position.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The team panel shares the same spatial slot as the plans and calculations panels and appears when the 'Project Team' tab is active. It is anchored 24–32px below the tabs and fills the central column. The panel header row echoes the others: a left-aligned label such as 'My Project Team Shortlist' and an optional right-aligned control for refreshing or managing the shortlist. Below this header, the team shortlist list extends downward, presenting potential team members in individual cards. The consistent header placement and panel width ensure a seamless experience when moving between the three tabs.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Mutually exclusive with the other panels; appears in the same central position when its tab is selected and remains hidden otherwise.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Inside the team panel, the team shortlist list is a vertical stack of profile cards. Each card runs nearly the full width of the panel’s column with 16–24px padding and 16–24px vertical separation between cards. On the left side of each card, a small circular avatar or initials badge appears, followed to the right by the team member’s name in bold and their role or specialty (e.g., 'Senior Geotechnical Engineer') in smaller, lighter text below. Contact or location info may appear in a second line. On the far right, aligned horizontally with the name, sits a compact remove or 'unshortlist' control. The entire card feels lighter and more profile-like while maintaining the same structural rhythm and spacings as the other lists.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static vertical list; expands downwards as more team members are shortlisted, fully integrated into the main scroll flow.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This API handler is associated with the remove control on each team member card but is itself invisible. When activated, it removes that member’s card from the list. Visually, the only change is the card disappearing and the cards below moving upward to maintain consistent vertical spacing.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual; impacts layout by causing the list to reflow after a card is removed.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The team member navigation link is represented by the member’s name within each card and optionally the entire card body. The name sits to the immediate right of the avatar, aligned with the card’s top text line and left padding, using a bold, high-contrast style to signify its importance. On hover or focus, it gains subtle interactive styling (color shift to brand teal and/or underline). Clicking leads back to the expert’s full profile or detail page used during the shortlisting process, visually tying the workspace back to the broader site tools.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline within the card content; participates in the normal text flow but can be extended as a hit area across the card without altering its visual alignment.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The empty state handler occupies the same central column area where a list would normally appear, but only when there are no items in the active panel (e.g., no saved plans, no calculations, or no team members). It centers a concise message and optional icon or illustration both horizontally and within the upper-middle portion of the panel, roughly 25–35% down from the top of the panel content area. The message block spans about 40–60% of the column’s width, with generous white space (at least 32–48px) above and below it. If there’s a primary call-to-action button (e.g., 'Create your first plan'), it’s placed directly beneath the message, centered. This arrangement reflects the hero’s spacious, centered feel, creating a calm, inviting prompt rather than an error-like state.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Conditionally displayed; replaces the list region within the active panel while preserving the panel header and tab navigation above it.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The refresh data control is a small, unobtrusive action control positioned in the top-right corner of the active data panel’s header row, horizontally aligned with the panel title on the left. It may appear as a circular icon button or a compact text+icon control using the brand teal color. The control is vertically centered within a 40–48px-tall header strip so it feels balanced and easy to target without crowding surrounding text. Its right edge aligns with the right edge of the central content column, maintaining the clean, flush alignment seen throughout the layout. On smaller screens, it may drop onto a second line beneath the title but remains right-aligned, preserving the clear left-title/right-action structure.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static control anchored to the active panel header; scrolls with the content and is always located just above the list or empty state region of the currently selected tab.

name: About Us
title: About Us
description: Displays dynamically loaded information about the geotechnical consulting firm and provides basic contact details, navigation to related pages, and a newsletter subscription workflow.

core_features: Fetch and display the firm's mission, history, regions served, primary sectors, and core competencies using getAboutPageContent.
Show high-level contact information (phone, email, address) from getAboutPageContent and provide a link to schedule a consultation (consultation.html).
Provide direct navigation links from the About Us page to Services (services.html), Projects (projects.html), and Team (team.html).
Expose deep-linkable section anchors (mission, history, regions, sectors, competencies, contact, newsletter) controlled via URL parameters.
Allow users to subscribe to the firm's newsletter via subscribeToNewsletter with configurable topics, email frequency, and professional role.
user_workflows:
View firm background and capabilities:
1. User navigates to about.html (directly or via header/footer navigation).
2. On page load, the about-page-loader component calls getAboutPageContent.
3. When data is returned, missionHtml, historyHtml, regionsServed, primarySectors, coreCompetencies, and contactInfo are passed to respective content components.
4. The user scrolls or uses in-page section navigation to view mission, history, regions, sectors, and core competencies.

Navigate from About Us to other key pages:
1. From the About Us page, the user locates the navigation links section.
2. The user clicks the 'Explore our services' link to go to services.html.
3. Alternatively, the user clicks 'View our project experience' to go to projects.html.
4. Alternatively, the user clicks 'Meet the team' to go to team.html.
5. From the contact section, the user can click 'Schedule a consultation' to go to consultation.html, optionally with URL parameters indicating the source page.

Access a specific About Us section via URL parameters:
1. Another page or an external source links to about.html with a section parameter such as about.html?section=mission.
2. On load, the about-page-loader reads the section query parameter.
3. Once getAboutPageContent data is loaded, the in-page navigation logic scrolls/focuses the corresponding section component (e.g., mission, history, regions, contact) and marks it as active in the section navigation component.

Review contact information and initiate contact:
1. After getAboutPageContent resolves, the contact-summary component receives contactInfo (phone, email, address).
2. The user views the contact details and can click the email link to open a mail client, or the phone link to initiate a call when supported by the device.
3. The user may click the 'Schedule a consultation' link in the contact section to navigate to consultation.html (optionally passing a query parameter like source=about to track origin).

Subscribe to the newsletter from the About Us page:
1. User opens about.html and scrolls to (or is deep-linked to) the newsletter subscription section (e.g., about.html?section=newsletter).
2. The newsletter-subscription-form component initializes its local form state. It may read URL parameters such as newsletterEmail, newsletterTopics, newsletterFrequency, and newsletterRole to prepopulate fields if present.
3. The user enters an email address and optionally selects topics, email frequency, and professional role.
4. On form submission, the component validates required fields on the client (at minimum, a non-empty email and a valid emailFrequency).
5. If validation passes, the component calls subscribeToNewsletter with email, topics, emailFrequency, and professionalRole.
6. The component handles the response: if success is true, it displays the message returned and may clear or lock the form; if success is false, it displays the error message and allows the user to adjust inputs.
7. Optionally, the page can update the URL query string with a lightweight status indicator (e.g., newsletterStatus=success or newsletterStatus=error) without reloading the page, so a refresh can re-display the appropriate status message.
interactions:
Page initialization:
- Event: window.onload or equivalent initialization hook.
- Action: about-page-loader component invokes getAboutPageContent.
- Result: Page state transitions from 'loading' to 'loaded' or 'error', which controls rendering of content sections or error messaging.

Section navigation clicks:
- Event: User clicks a section navigation item (e.g., 'Mission', 'History', 'Regions & Sectors', 'Core Competencies', 'Contact', 'Newsletter').
- Action: Update the browser URL to include/update the section query parameter (e.g., about.html?section=history) via pushState/replaceState without reloading; scroll/focus the corresponding section component.
- Result: The selected section is brought into view and marked active in the navigation component.

External navigation links from About Us:
- Event: User clicks 'Explore our services'.
- Action: Browser navigates to services.html.
- Event: User clicks 'View our project experience'.
- Action: Browser navigates to projects.html.
- Event: User clicks 'Meet the team'.
- Action: Browser navigates to team.html.
- Event: User clicks 'Schedule a consultation'.
- Action: Browser navigates to consultation.html, optionally augmenting the URL with a query parameter indicating origin (e.g., consultation.html?source=about).

Contact method clicks:
- Event: User clicks on the phone number.
- Action: Browser attempts to initiate a call using a tel: link constructed from contactInfo.phone.
- Event: User clicks on the email address.
- Action: Browser opens the default mail client using a mailto: link constructed from contactInfo.email.

Newsletter subscription form input:
- Event: User types into the email field.
- Action: Update local form state for email; clear inline email validation errors for that field.
- Event: User selects or modifies topics (e.g., via multiselect or checkboxes).
- Action: Update local form state for topics array.
- Event: User selects an email frequency option.
- Action: Update local form state for emailFrequency.
- Event: User selects a professional role option.
- Action: Update local form state for professionalRole.

Newsletter subscription form submission:
- Event: User clicks the Subscribe button or submits the form.
- Action: Perform client-side validation. If validation fails, show inline error messages and prevent API call. If validation passes, call subscribeToNewsletter with the current form state values.
- Result: While awaiting the response, show a loading state (e.g., disabled submit button). On resolution, display success or error messaging based on the returned success boolean and message, and update internal form state accordingly.

Newsletter status restoration from URL state:
- Event: Page loads with a query parameter specifying newsletterStatus.
- Action: newsletter-feedback component reads newsletterStatus (and optionally a related message parameter) to determine whether to show a persistent success or error banner.
- Result: The user sees consistent feedback even after a page reload or when arriving from a redirected link that encoded status in the URL.
page_functionality.state_logic: The About Us page is primarily controlled by query parameters in the about.html URL. Key parameters include: (1) section: a string indicating which section should be treated as active and scrolled into view after data is loaded (e.g., mission, history, regions, sectors, competencies, contact, newsletter). On page load, about-page-loader reads section from the query string. Once getAboutPageContent has successfully returned, the loader instructs the section-navigation component and the relevant content section to mark that section as active and scroll/focus it. If section is missing or does not match a known section identifier, the page falls back to the implementation's default section behavior without hardcoding content. (2) newsletterEmail, newsletterTopics, newsletterFrequency, newsletterRole: optional parameters used to prepopulate the newsletter-subscription-form component. newsletterEmail is mapped directly to the form's email field if present. newsletterTopics is interpreted as a comma-separated list of topic identifiers; the component splits this value into an array for the topics parameter passed to subscribeToNewsletter. newsletterFrequency, when present, is used to initialize the emailFrequency field if it matches an allowed value defined by the NewsletterSubscription model. newsletterRole, when present, is used to initialize the professionalRole field if it matches an allowed professionalRole value. If any of these values are invalid or absent, the corresponding field uses the implementation's non-hardcoded default state. (3) newsletterStatus and newsletterMessage: optional parameters used by the newsletter-feedback component. If newsletterStatus indicates a successful subscription, the component displays a success message (using newsletterMessage if present, otherwise the message from the last subscribeToNewsletter response in memory). If newsletterStatus indicates an error, the component displays an error state analogously. The page does not rely on localStorage; instead, it uses URL parameters and in-memory state for all view logic. URL parameters may be updated via the History API without causing a full reload, ensuring the state remains shareable and bookmarkable.

components.functionality: Initializes the About Us page by calling getAboutPageContent, managing loading and error states, and distributing the returned content to child components while also interpreting URL query parameters such as section and newsletter-related parameters.
data_binding: getAboutPageContent (missionHtml, historyHtml, regionsServed, primarySectors, coreCompetencies, contactInfo)
event_handlers: onLoad (triggered when about.html is loaded; calls getAboutPageContent)
onDataLoaded (invoked after successful getAboutPageContent response; passes data to section components and triggers section-specific scrolling based on the section query parameter)
onDataError (invoked if getAboutPageContent fails; signals error state to content components)
onUrlChange (optional listener when the query string is updated via History API; updates active section without reloading)

components.functionality: Provides a list of in-page navigation links to each main section of the About Us content (mission, history, regions & sectors, core competencies, contact, newsletter) and synchronizes the active item with the section query parameter in the URL.
event_handlers: onClickSectionItem (updates the URL section query parameter using pushState/replaceState and scrolls the corresponding section into view)
onSectionChange (listens to changes in the section query parameter, e.g., via popstate, and updates the active state in the navigation list)

components.functionality: Displays the firm's mission using missionHtml from getAboutPageContent and exposes an anchor target corresponding to the mission section for deep linking and navigation syncing.
data_binding: URL query parameter: section (used to determine if this section is active or should receive focus)
event_handlers: onDataRender (sanitizes and renders missionHtml when data is available)
onSectionActivated (scrolls into view and sets focus when section=mission is detected)

components.functionality: Renders the firm's history content from historyHtml and provides an anchor for section-based navigation.
event_handlers: onDataRender (sanitizes and renders historyHtml when data is available)
onSectionActivated (scrolls into view and sets focus when section=history is detected)

components.functionality: Displays regionsServed and primarySectors from getAboutPageContent, grouping them in a way that allows users to understand where and in which sectors the firm operates.
event_handlers: onDataRender (renders regionsServed and primarySectors lists or equivalent structured content when available)
onSectionActivated (scrolls into view and sets focus when section is set to a value corresponding to this section, such as regions or sectors)

components.functionality: Lists the firm's coreCompetencies from getAboutPageContent, emphasizing key geotechnical capabilities without hardcoded labels.
event_handlers: onDataRender (renders a list or structured view of coreCompetencies when data is available)
onSectionActivated (scrolls into view and sets focus when section indicates this section, such as competencies)

components.functionality: Provides functional navigation links to other major pages: Services, Projects, and Team, leveraging the outgoing navigation specification.
data_binding: Static URL endpoints: services.html, projects.html, team.html (as destinations only, without embedding any static content values)
event_handlers: onClickServicesLink (navigates to services.html when the 'Explore our services' link is clicked)
onClickProjectsLink (navigates to projects.html when the 'View our project experience' link is clicked)
onClickTeamLink (navigates to team.html when the 'Meet the team' link is clicked)

components.functionality: Displays high-level contact information from getAboutPageContent.contactInfo (phone, email, address) and houses the 'Schedule a consultation' navigation link.
event_handlers: onDataRender (renders contactInfo fields when available and constructs tel: and mailto: links dynamically from the returned values)
onClickPhone (initiates a call via the tel: link when supported by the device)
onClickEmail (opens the default mail client via the mailto: link)
onClickScheduleConsultation (navigates to consultation.html, optionally appending a query parameter like source=about to the URL)

components.functionality: Collects newsletter subscription details (email, topics, emailFrequency, professionalRole) and submits them via subscribeToNewsletter, handling validation, loading, and success/error feedback.
data_binding: subscribeToNewsletter.topics (array derived from form inputs and optionally from the newsletterTopics query parameter)
subscribeToNewsletter.emailFrequency (selection bound to allowed values defined in NewsletterSubscription.emailFrequency)
subscribeToNewsletter.professionalRole (selection bound to allowed values defined in NewsletterSubscription.professionalRole)
URL query parameters: newsletterEmail, newsletterTopics, newsletterFrequency, newsletterRole (used to initialize form state when present)
event_handlers: onInit (reads newsletter-related URL query parameters and prepopulates form state where valid)
onChangeEmail (updates local email state and clears email-specific validation errors)
onChangeTopics (updates topics array state based on user selections)
onSubmit (validates form inputs; if valid, calls subscribeToNewsletter with current state; sets a loading state during the API call)

onSubmitSuccess (receives response from subscribeToNewsletter when success is true; displays the returned message, updates UI to reflect subscription status, and optionally updates URL query parameters such as newsletterStatus=success without reloading)
onSubmitError (receives response from subscribeToNewsletter when success is false or an error occurs; displays the returned message or a generic error; clears loading state and allows resubmission)

components.functionality: Shows persistent success or error messaging related to newsletter subscription, based on either the latest subscribeToNewsletter response or newsletter-related URL parameters.
data_binding: subscribeToNewsletter.return.message (captured from the last subscription attempt in the current page session)
event_handlers: onInit (reads newsletterStatus and newsletterMessage from the URL; if present, sets initial feedback state accordingly)
onSubscriptionResult (listens for events emitted by newsletter-subscription-form after subscribeToNewsletter completes; updates feedback state with success or error message)
onDismiss (allows the user to hide the banner; may update in-memory state and optionally clear newsletterStatus from the URL using the History API)

layout.grid_system: Implied 12‑column responsive grid (typical WordPress/corporate layout) with a full-width container for the hero and a centered content column.
layout.layout_pattern: All components sit within the main content area of an About Us page that inherits the global layout: a full-width fixed teal header and a full-bleed hero above these components. Below the hero, content flows in a linear, top-to-bottom order inside a centered max-width container. On larger screens, a left-aligned vertical section-navigation sidebar occupies roughly 1/4 of the container width, while the main content sections (mission, history, regions & sectors, core competencies, contact summary, newsletter, cross-page links) stack in a single primary column to the right. System-level UI (loader, newsletter feedback) overlays or sits within this flow but does not break the linear hierarchy.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: All components sit within the main content area of an About Us page that inherits the global layout: a full-width fixed teal header and a full-bleed hero above these components. Below the hero, content flows in a linear, top-to-bottom order inside a centered max-width container. On larger screens, a left-aligned vertical section-navigation sidebar occupies roughly 1/4 of the container width, while the main content sections (mission, history, regions & sectors, core competencies, contact summary, newsletter, cross-page links) stack in a single primary column to the right. System-level UI (loader, newsletter feedback) overlays or sits within this flow but does not break the linear hierarchy.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The header continues to occupy about 10–15% of the viewport height, and the hero occupies most of the remaining viewport above the fold. The About Us components begin just below the hero and extend down the page. Within the main content container, the section-navigation uses roughly 25–30% of the width (3/12 columns), and the main content column uses roughly 70–75% (9/12 columns). Each content section (mission, history, etc.) spans the full width of the main column, with vertical spacing between sections of about 48–64px to maintain a professional, spacious feeling. Cards and form blocks use internal padding around 16–24px following the 8px scale.
layout_strategies.content_density: The density echoes the hero: relatively few, well-structured elements in each section, generous margins and line spacing, and plenty of whitespace around text. Primary sections like Mission and Core Competencies get more breathing room and stronger typographic hierarchy. Secondary sections (history, regions & sectors, cross-page links, contact summary) are comfortable but slightly more compact. The navigation sidebar is clean and minimal with clear vertical spacing between links. Loader and notification components are visually simple, with minimal text and strong brand-color accents, avoiding visual clutter.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The visual journey mirrors the F/Z hybrid: the eye moves from the logo and top navigation in the header to the hero’s centered heading. After the hero, the eye lands at the top of the main content column (Mission) while being aware of the section-navigation on the left. From there, the flow is top-to-bottom through Mission → History → Regions & Sectors → Core Competencies → Contact Summary → Newsletter → Cross-page Links. The section-navigation on the left supports quick jumps and becomes a secondary anchor as users scroll. The newsletter feedback banner appears immediately adjacent to the form, providing inline confirmation or error messaging without stealing focus from the primary reading path.

layout.overall_description: Below the full-width fixed header and full-bleed hero, the About Us page content is housed in a centered, max-width container with generous left and right padding so no text feels close to the edges. On desktop, a slim vertical navigation sidebar sits aligned to the left edge of this container, listing each About section with comfortable spacing and a subtle highlight for the active section. To its right, a wider main content column runs from top to bottom, starting with a mission section that visually echoes the hero’s clarity and whitespace. Each subsequent section—history, regions & sectors, core competencies, contact summary, newsletter, and cross-page links—reads as a distinct horizontal band separated by 48–64px of vertical whitespace. Key sections use subtle background tints or card-like containers with 16–24px padding to align with the corporate feel. The newsletter area towards the bottom centers its form elements in the column, mirroring the hero’s centered alignment. A full-screen loader can overlay the entire layout when data is being fetched, while the newsletter feedback appears as a clean banner attached directly to the form, ensuring system feedback is visible yet unobtrusive. The resulting page feels structured, spacious, and consistent with the original corporate hero design, with a clear left-to-right then top-to-bottom reading rhythm.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The data loader appears as a full-viewport overlay that sits above all other content, including the header and hero. When active, it covers 100% of the width and height of the browser window with a semi-transparent dark teal or charcoal scrim (about 60–70% opacity) that subtly mutes the underlying layout. At the exact center of the viewport, a compact loading card (roughly 280–320px wide) contains a spinner or progress indicator above a single line of text such as “Loading About Us…”. This card uses generous internal padding (around 24px) and a high-contrast background (white or very light grey) to stand out against the scrim. The loader content is center-aligned both horizontally and vertically, mirroring the hero’s central emphasis and ensuring the user’s attention is drawn immediately to the loading state.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Fixed-position overlay that covers the entire viewport; appears and disappears based on loading state, with the rest of the layout scroll-locked while it is visible.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The section navigation is a vertical sidebar positioned within the main content area just below the hero’s bottom edge. It is aligned to the left inside the centered content container, occupying approximately 25–30% of the container width (3 of 12 columns). On desktop, its top edge starts about 48px below the hero bottom, aligning horizontally with the top of the mission section in the main column. The sidebar height stretches down alongside the main content, but its internal content—a simple list of section links—is vertically compact, leaving ample padding (16–24px) on all sides. Each link is left-aligned with consistent vertical spacing between items (12–16px), making scanning easy. The active section is indicated with a subtle left border or background tint in the brand color, while the rest remain neutral. On larger screens, the sidebar stays visible as the user scrolls, creating a stable anchor on the left side of the viewport, while the main content scrolls to its right.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Within the content container, the navigation is positioned on the left and uses sticky behavior: it remains fixed relative to the viewport once scrolled past its initial position, up to the bottom of the main content area. On small screens, it collapses to a horizontal section index placed above the main content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The mission section is the first and most prominent block in the main content column to the right of the section-navigation. It starts at the same vertical level as the top of the section-navigation, roughly 48px below the hero. The section occupies the full width of the main column (about 70–75% of the container width, or 9 of 12 columns) and is left-aligned relative to the container center. Inside, the content is arranged top-to-bottom with a small category-style label at the top (e.g., “Our Mission”) functioning like the hero’s category pill: a compact pill aligned to the left, with 8–12px vertical and horizontal padding and a strong brand-color border or fill. Below this, a large, multi-line headline (e.g., the firm’s mission statement) spans roughly 70–80% of the main column width, with generous top and bottom margins (24–32px). Supporting body text follows, set in readable line lengths and line heights, possibly broken into 2–3 short paragraphs. Overall vertical padding within this section is ample (24–32px), creating a hero-adjacent feeling and clearly signaling this as a primary section. There is about 64px of whitespace below the mission content before the next section begins.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the main flow of the right-hand content column; its top edge aligns horizontally with the section-navigation’s top, forming the primary entry point into the About content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The history section sits directly below the mission section within the same main content column, separated by approximately 48–64px of vertical whitespace. It spans the full width of the main column (9/12 columns) and maintains left alignment with consistent side padding. The section’s title (e.g., “Our History”) is left-aligned at the top with a slightly smaller type size than the mission headline, followed by a short introductory sentence. Below the heading, the content may be broken into 2–4 paragraphs or a simple timeline list stacked vertically. Internal spacing is disciplined but slightly tighter than the mission section, with vertical gaps around 16–24px between heading, subhead, and paragraphs, reflecting a more informational, secondary role. The right edge of the text aligns with the mission content above, creating a clean vertical column. The bottom of this section leaves a clear 48–64px gap before the regions & sectors section begins.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, stacked directly after the mission section in the main column, scrolling naturally with the page.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The regions & sectors section appears beneath the history section, again separated by about 48–64px of vertical whitespace. It uses the full width of the main column but introduces a subtle internal two-column layout on larger screens to organize information: the left half (about 50% of the main column) presents a heading (e.g., “Regions & Sectors We Serve”) and short descriptive text, while the right half can list regions and sectors in a neat vertical or two-column list. The heading is left-aligned and sized similarly to the history heading. The overall block may be visually grouped with a light background tint or a faint border to differentiate it slightly from the plain background sections above it, while still maintaining around 16–24px of internal padding on all sides. This gives the section some structural presence without competing with the mission hero-like feel. The two internal columns collapse into a single stacked column on smaller screens, preserving readability. There is a consistent 48–64px gap below this section before the core competencies section starts.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block within the main column; inside the block, content flows left-to-right in two subcolumns on desktop and top-to-bottom on mobile.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The core competencies section is positioned below the regions & sectors section, separated by roughly 64px to signal its importance. It spans the full width of the main column but feels visually more substantial, akin to a feature section. The section title sits at the top left, possibly accompanied by a small label or subtitle indicating “Core Expertise” or similar. Beneath the heading, the competencies are presented in a simple grid of cards: on larger screens, 2–3 cards per row, each card taking up equal width; on smaller screens, the cards stack vertically. Each card has internal padding of about 16–24px, a light border or subtle shadow, and a comfortable gap of 16px between cards horizontally and 24px vertically. This structured repetition echoes a technical, methodical brand, in line with the geotechnical context. The overall section may be placed on a very light alternate background color strip that stretches the full width of the centered container, providing gentle emphasis without overwhelming the layout. Vertical padding for the entire section is larger than other secondary sections (32–48px top and bottom), creating a sense of primary importance near the middle-lower portion of the page.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, occupying a larger vertical footprint than prior sections, visually anchoring the mid-lower portion of the main column.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The cross-page links section is placed after the core competencies section, separated by roughly 48px of vertical space. It acts as a transition zone toward the bottom of the page, offering navigation to related content such as Services, Projects, or Careers. It spans the full width of the main column and uses a horizontal arrangement of 2–3 link tiles or buttons on desktop: each tile is a clean, rectangular card with 16–24px of internal padding, a concise label (e.g., “Our Services”), and possibly a short one-line description. The tiles are evenly spaced with about 16–24px between them. The section header is left-aligned above the tiles, with a modest font size similar to history and regions headings, and a small gap (16px) between the header and the row of tiles. On smaller screens, the tiles stack vertically with consistent vertical spacing. The section keeps its background neutral to maintain subtlety, ensuring it feels like a helpful extension of the current page rather than a competing hero element.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the main column; its horizontal tile layout reflows to a vertical stack on smaller screens.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The contact summary section sits close to the bottom of the main content column, ideally just above or just below the cross-page links section depending on business priority; here it is placed directly below the cross-page links section with about 48–64px of vertical separation. It spans the full width of the main column but uses a card-like presentation to feel concise and scannable: a single panel with a clear heading (e.g., “Contact Our Team”) followed by key contact details (phone, email, office locations) arranged in a compact vertical stack or a simple two-column layout. The card has internal padding of 16–24px and possibly a subtle border or background tint to differentiate it from the page background without overpowering the design. Iconography (phone, email) can appear to the left of each line to aid quick scanning, but spacing between lines remains generous (8–12px) to prevent crowding. The overall height is moderate, prioritizing clarity and quick access over long narrative content, and leaves about 48px of whitespace below before the newsletter subscription section begins.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, appearing as a self-contained information card near the bottom of the main column; scrolls naturally with the page.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The newsletter subscription form is positioned near the bottom of the About page, directly beneath the contact summary section with approximately 48–64px of separation. It visually echoes the hero’s centered, focused layout but on a smaller scale. The section spans the full width of the main column, but the form itself is constrained to the central 60–70% of that width, leaving visible whitespace on both sides. At the top of the section, a heading and a short explanatory line are center-aligned. Below this, the form fields (e.g., email input and submit button) are arranged horizontally on desktop—email field taking roughly 70% of the form width and the button 30%—with consistent vertical padding (16–24px) around the form area. On smaller screens, fields stack vertically with 8–16px gaps. The section may sit on a subtly tinted background band within the content container to give it a distinct but understated presence. Generous top and bottom padding (24–32px) make the form feel comfortable and approachable, consistent with the site’s spacious corporate aesthetic.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, centered within the main column; the internal layout of form elements adapts between horizontal (desktop) and vertical (mobile) alignments while maintaining central alignment.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The newsletter feedback appears as a slim, horizontally oriented notification banner directly associated with the newsletter subscription form. It is positioned immediately below the form fields, aligned to the same central width as the form (about 60–70% of the main column width), so the left and right edges of the banner line up with the form’s edges. The banner height is modest (approximately 40–56px) with 8–16px of vertical padding, and contains a short message describing success or error states. The background color shifts clearly based on state—e.g., a soft green for success, a soft red/orange for errors—while text remains high contrast for readability. The banner content is left-aligned within the band, though the band itself is centered with the form, maintaining the visual language of the hero’s centered text. When not needed, the banner space collapses so that no extra gap remains below the form, preserving the clean vertical rhythm.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Appears and disappears inline within the newsletter section; does not overlay other content. It pushes content below it down when visible and collapses to zero height when hidden, maintaining the central alignment of the form.
